title: Stage 1 method overview (the map)
retrieve_when: orienting to how Stage 1 (Foundations) is structured — a parent asks why their plan includes or orders certain foundational pieces (safe sleep, sleep environment, schedule, nutrition, parenting strategies), or the assistant needs the big-picture Stage 1 map to place a question in context. High-level structure; the situational detail lives in the individual resources it cross-references.
body:
**Purpose:** the holistic work that needs to be done before habit changes begin. Stage 1 is not temporary. Families maintain these foundations through every later stage.

**What Stage 1 asks of the family:**
- Spend a few full days on this prep work before moving on (ideally three days). Even the seemingly insignificant recommendations are extremely important for sleep success.
- Make sleep the priority: be home for most naps and bedtimes for the next couple of weeks, and avoid travel or big disruptions. This stretch is temporary (see resource_make_sleep_the_priority).
- If other caregivers are part of bedtime and naps, everyone needs to be on the same page.
- Read Stage 1 and the Getting Started Guide, listen to the audio messages from Jessie, and log all sleep and nutrition in the Log tab. The more detailed the log and notes, the better the assistant can support them (see resource_logging_drives_personalization).

Stage 1 covers these areas:

**Welcome**
- Welcome / What to Expect: the opening block for every family except the fully-independent family. Explains that the plan is organized in intentional stages that build on one another.
- Your Sleep Goals: reflects back the sleep goals the parent selected at intake.
- Fully-independent families get their own versions instead: "You've got an independent sleeper!" plus a "Where to focus" note pointed at their top struggle.

**Things to Do First**
- Safe Sleep: fires for every family. Asks the parent to confirm their child is cleared by the pediatrician to work on sleep and to read the safe sleep section of the Getting Started Guide. Individual safety notes stack on top based on intake answers (swaddle, lounger, car seat, swing, inclined sleeper, bassinet, bedsharing, climbing out of the crib). Unsafe sleep practices must end before the plan begins (see resource_safe_sleep_no_accommodations; for why each item is unsafe, see resource_unsafe_items_why).
- A Note About Floor Beds: only for families using a floor bed (see resource_bed_before_3_stance and resource_toddler_bed_and_bedding).
- Weaning Off the Snoo: only for families using a Snoo (full steps in resource_snoo_wean).

**Sleep Basics**
- The Sleep Environment and Bedtime Routine: fires for every family. Starts with the "How Sleep Works" audio (see resource_audio_how_sleep_works).
- The core items, always shown:
  - Dark sleep space: cave-dark for every nap and through the night (see resource_darkness and resource_darkening_a_room).
  - White noise: one constant low, deep sound, every sleep (see resource_white_noise).
  - Temperature and dressing (see resource_room_temperature and resource_dressing_chart).
  - Sleep sack: every nap and bedtime (see resource_sleep_sack).
  - A simple sleep routine: same order every time, ending with a consistent "sleep phrase."
- An OK-to-Wake clock item shows for children about 8 months and older (see resource_ok_to_wake_clock).
- Room Sharing Tips (the visual barrier between the parents' bed and the crib) shows only for families keeping the child in the parents' room in a crib (see resource_room_sharing_setup).

**Schedule**
- Every family gets a by-the-clock schedule card built from age-appropriate wake windows: sleep needs, wake windows, a sample schedule, and things to know (see resource_clock_led_not_cue_led; the wake-windows audio is resource_audio_wake_windows_overtiredness).
- The schedule variant is chosen by the child's age, and at the two nap-transition windows (around 7 months for the 3-to-2 transition and around 12-17 months for the 2-to-1 transition), by nap count. Every other age routes on age alone (see resource_nap_transition_routing). Each variant is backed by its own library entry, from resource_schedule_5mo through resource_schedule_2yr; those entries hold the exact age bands and wake windows.
- V1 covers 5 through 29 months, capped at the 2 Year schedule.
- Preemies: if the child was born before 37 weeks and the parent chose adjusted age at intake, the schedule routes by adjusted age (see resource_adjusted_age).
- The app auto-advances the schedule as the child ages. The current schedule is right-for-now, not permanent.
- Making Your Schedule Work: a companion to the schedule card. A daycare section is added when the child regularly naps somewhere other than home (see resource_daycare_home_is_the_anchor and the other daycare resources).

**Nutrition and Sleep**
- A universal opener for every family. The "Nutrition and Sleep" audio plays only for families whose child receives overnight feeds or calories (see resource_nutrition_and_sleep_opener and resource_audio_nutrition_and_sleep).
- Age-routed content: a baby version (under 12 months, milk-fed) and an older-baby version (12 months and up, or under 12 and fully weaned).
- Goals: good, full feedings all day long, and helping the child associate eating with nutrition, not sleep. Feeding is responsive and cue-led, never clock-led (see resource_complete_feeding_tips and resource_no_feeding_schedules).

**Parenting Strategies**
- Fires for every family, babies included (see resource_parenting_strategies_philosophy; the audio is resource_audio_parenting_strategies). The strategies: prioritize connection where your child can see (see resource_parenting_connection), revisit freedoms and choices (see resource_parenting_freedoms_and_choices), be consistent all day long (see resource_parenting_consistency), and reframe "my child won't let me" (see resource_parenting_wont_let_me_reframe).
- Independent playtime: daytime practice being in the sleep space. Adapts to the family's setup, crib playtime for crib families, room time for floor bed families (see resource_parenting_independent_playtime; for floor beds, resource_independent_playtime_room_time).
- A toddler cooperation layer (bedtime checklist and similar tools) is added for children 18 months and older (see resource_toddler_family_meetings).

title: Lead with reassurance and a few first steps from their plan
retrieve_when: a parent sounds overwhelmed getting started, or asks where to begin / what to do first.
body: Lower overwhelm, don't add to it. Meet them with warmth and confidence, point them to their concrete first steps, and reassure them that the plan is built specifically for their child. Don't add new steps, just give them a few steps from stage 1 of their plan (like get the room environment set up and start working on a consistent morning wake time).

title: Protect the "make it the priority" ask without inducing guilt
retrieve_when: a parent worries they can't stay home, travel-free, or be "perfect" during the working period, or asks about travel, vaccines, appointments, or moving during it.
body: The intro asks families to stay home for most naps and bedtimes for a couple of weeks. When a parent worries they cannot do this perfectly, reassure them: it is about doing their best during a short, temporary stretch. They don't need to be 100% perfect, but it is really important for them to prioritize their child's sleep during this time. They should try to be home for most naps and bedtimes for the next couple of weeks, and avoid travel or big disruptions, including vaccinations, if they can. If they can't, they should reconsider the timing of their sleep support. Once their child is sleeping well, their days can go back to being more flexible. If they're moving, they'll want to consider sleep support either before the move or 2 to 6 weeks after it.

title: How long the plan takes, and setting the pace
retrieve_when: a parent asks how long the plan takes, worries they're behind, or feels it's taking too long.
body: Most families take about 2-4 weeks to work through their child's sleep plan. In general, this is a realistic timeline to go by. However, some families, especially those who have highly sensitive children or parents who are extremely sensitive to crying, may take closer to 6-8 weeks. This is normal for these types of families. The family can set the pace that works for them. As long as they are intentionally moving forward, it's ok to take their time if that's their preference. If a family is taking longer than 2-4 weeks and they are feeling frustrated, the assistant should help move them along through their plan. It can do this by assessing where they're at in the plan, how their child is tolerating things, and guiding them into the following steps. Some families need a little push to know it's ok to move to the next steps. Realistically, a highly motivated family who is willing to be extremely consistent could move through the core plan in as little as two weeks (Stage 1: 3 days, Stage 2: 3-5 days, Stage 3: 3-5 days). Naps can typically be started in the second half of that second week, and often take about 1-2 weeks to 100% settle in. So all together, a family moving quickly is usually looking at about 2.5-3.5 weeks from start to finish.

title: Reinforce logging as the engine of personalization (and what to log)
retrieve_when: logging is sparse, or a parent asks why, how much, or what to log.
body:
The more detail parents include in their log, the more the assistant can support, personalize, and troubleshoot. When a parent's logging is sparse, the assistant should let the family know: it cannot give detailed, personalized guidance without consistent sleep and nutrition logs and good notes. Frame it as the thing that unlocks better help, not as a chore or a scolding.

What the parent should log each day:

- The time the sleep attempt begins (in Stage 2 this is the time the parent starts assisting the child to sleep; in Stage 3, this is the time the parent lays the child down in their sleep space).
- The time the child fell asleep.
- Any wakings (be that night wakings or wakings during naps) and what happened during the waking (any interventions), how long they were awake, and the time they fell back to sleep.
- Their mood before and after each sleep.
- Any nighttime feedings and/or nutrition.
- Daytime feedings, meals, and snacks (regardless of how short or small).

The logging tab in the app allows parents to log all of this very quickly. Any notes the parent adds to the logs will be given to the assistant as well to work from.

title: When a family isn't following the plan, be honest about what that means
retrieve_when: logs or chat show a family isn't following the plan (e.g. missing naps, not using a consistent morning wake time) and goals may need a reset.
body: The assistant is a coach; it should not tell the parents only what they want to hear. When the logs or the chat conversation show a family is not following the plan, for example missing lots of naps because they are out of the house, the assistant is honest and kind about what that means: progress is going to be slower or the family may not reach their sleep goals. If the family is unable to follow the plan, they may need to change their sleep goals to match what they are willing to do. Deliver this as "here is the why, and here is what would help," never as "you are doing it wrong." Keep it warm, name the constraint without judgment, and point back to one or two changes that would make the biggest difference. If the parent is simply overwhelmed or in an extra busy season, the assistant can also help them brainstorm solutions (e.g. making lifestyle changes for a short time or pausing their sleep plan for a few weeks but setting a date to pick back up).

title: Medical conditions and sleep: route, don't adapt
retrieve_when: a parent raises a medical condition or symptom that may affect sleep (e.g. reflux, eczema, snoring or breathing concerns, ear infections, restless sleep, feeding or growth concerns, or a developmental, neurological, or genetic condition), or asks the assistant to adjust the plan around one.
source: Getting Started Guide (mirrors the "speak with your pediatrician if you notice" section — keep in sync)
body:
The Goodnight House provides non-medical pediatric sleep consulting: support and education. The assistant does not diagnose, does not give medical advice, and does not adapt the plan or build custom strategies around a medical condition. Certain medical conditions can affect sleep, so when a medical condition or symptom comes up, the assistant tells the parent this, points them to their pediatrician, and lets them know they can reach out to The Goodnight House (www.thegoodnighthouse.com) for 1:1 individualized sleep support.

The assistant should suggest speaking with the pediatrician when a parent notices things like:

- Snoring, gasping, choking, pauses in breathing, or consistent mouth breathing, which can be signs of airway concerns or obstructive sleep apnea.
- Restless sleep, frequent night wakings, or difficulty settling despite healthy sleep habits. In some cases, low iron, ferritin, or vitamin D levels can contribute to sleep disruptions.
- Uncontrolled eczema or other conditions causing discomfort during sleep.
- Feeding, growth, weight gain, or milk transfer concerns. Consider working with a certified lactation consultant if additional feeding support is needed.

Other medical conditions that can impact sleep include reflux, chronic congestion, allergies, constipation, and certain developmental, neurological, genetic, or medical conditions.

Suggested response: "Because children with complex medical needs often have unique considerations that can impact sleep, this is outside the scope of the recommendations I can safely provide. I would encourage you to discuss your child's sleep concerns with their pediatrician or healthcare team. If you're interested in working directly with The Goodnight House (www.thegoodnighthouse.com), please reach out for individualized sleep support."

title: Transcript — "How Sleep Works" (`AUDIO_HOW_SLEEP_WORKS`)
retrieve_when: a parent asks how sleep works, why babies wake between sleep cycles, why depending on a prop or a person to fall asleep makes wakings worse, or references the "How Sleep Works" audio.
body:
Hey, Jessie here.

I wanted to take a really quick minute to explain how your child's sleep works. Having a foundational understanding of this will help the rest of your sleep plan make a lot more sense.

We all sleep in cycles. As we move from one sleep cycle to the next, we briefly come into a lighter stage of sleep and experience a mini waking. These little sleep cycle transitions happen several times throughout the night.

For many adults and children, this looks like briefly waking, looking around, recognizing that everything is normal, safe, and familiar, and then rolling right back over and going back to sleep.

However, when a baby or child depends on someone or something else to fall asleep, those sleep cycle transitions can become more difficult.

When they briefly wake between sleep cycles, they often notice that something has changed. Maybe they fell asleep in mom's arms and now they're in their crib. Maybe they fell asleep while nursing and now they're no longer latched. Maybe they were being rocked and now the rocking has stopped.

Instead of easily settling back to sleep, they often call for help to recreate the sleep conditions they had when they first fell asleep.

As a result, what could have been a brief sleep cycle transition becomes a full waking. Instead of quickly drifting back to sleep, they need assistance getting comfortable and falling asleep again.

An example I like to give families is this:

Imagine you fell asleep in your bed at the beginning of the night. Then during a sleep cycle transition, you briefly wake up and realize you're suddenly sleeping in the bathtub.

That would be pretty startling.

You'd probably get up, reorient yourself, maybe grab a drink or a snack, climb back into bed, get comfortable again, and then work on falling back to sleep. It wouldn't just be a little blip in your sleep.

For many children, this is exactly what happens during those sleep cycle transitions. They briefly wake up, notice that something is different from when they first fell asleep, and need help recreating that familiar sleep situation before they can settle back to sleep.

The Goodnight House sleep plans focus heavily on helping children develop the confidence and skills they need to transition through sleep cycles as smoothly as possible.

### Foundations → The Consulting Relationship

title: Consulting is an ongoing relationship; the family's effort drives progress
retrieve_when: a parent questions or shows a misunderstanding of their role, expects the plan to work without consistently implementing it, or asks whether The Goodnight House can guarantee success or offer a refund if the sleep plan "doesn't work."
body: Consulting is an ongoing relationship. Progress depends on the family's commitment to implement the plan and put in the effort. The assistant can give the example of a personal trainer: a personal trainer can give a workout and meal plan, offer encouragement and troubleshoot along the way, but if the client doesn't implement the strategies or do the plan, they won't see results.

title: A family doesn't have to commit to the whole method to benefit
retrieve_when: a parent is hesitant to do the full plan, wants to go only partway, or asks whether it's worth starting if they're not ready to commit to everything (including independent sleep).
body: A family doesn't have to commit to the whole method, or to giving up a chosen sleep association, to benefit. Most of the foundational work is holistic, non-association-specific sleep support: timing and routines, light and dark, environment, nutrition. Many families are comfortable with Stage 1, and many with Stage 2 as well, even if they don't plan to go further. So the assistant engages warmly and helps them with the foundational work, and the full ladder still aims toward an independent sleeper if and when the family chooses to go there. The point is that a family doesn't have to commit to the whole plan to benefit; the foundations help regardless.

title: Core coaching principles (confidence is contagious, consistency is communication, crying is communication)
retrieve_when: grounding the assistant's coaching stance while supporting a parent through the emotional side of habit changing — projecting calm confidence, encouraging consistency, or interpreting and responding to crying.
body:
**Confidence is contagious.** Children often take their cues from the adults caring for them. When parents appear calm and confident, even during a difficult bedtime or night waking, they communicate that everything is okay. The child may not like the change, but they can also sense that they are safe. Remind parents that they do not have to feel perfectly confident on the inside. It's completely normal to feel nervous, discouraged, or second-guess themselves. The goal is simply to remain calm and confident on the outside as they lead their child through the change.

**Consistency is communication.** One of the clearest forms of communication parents have with young children is consistency. Children cannot sit down and have a conversation about new expectations. They learn what to expect by experiencing the same response over and over again. This is why consistency is so important during habit changing. It helps children understand the new expectation, reduces confusion, and provides a sense of security. When parents are tempted to change course after one difficult night, remind them that consistency is one of the primary ways children learn new sleep habits.

**Crying is communication.** Treat crying as a normal form of communication, not a problem that must always be stopped immediately. Children cry for many reasons, including frustration, disappointment, fatigue, discomfort, hunger, and needing comfort. The assistant should never assume the reason for crying without first asking thoughtful questions. Never shame parents for responding to their child, comforting them, or using feeding as a sleep strategy in the past. Those patterns develop naturally in many families. Also remember that temperament plays a significant role. Two children can experience the exact same change and respond very differently. Some children hardly react at all, while others have a much bigger response. The parent's job is not to control their child's emotions. Their job is to respond with comfort, reassurance, and consistency while confidently leading the change.

title: Cry Scale (AI reference — the shared 0–5 language for coaching crying across Stages 1–4)
retrieve_when: the assistant needs to explain or apply the Cry Scale, help a parent understand what a cry level means, or coach whether to wait, observe, or intervene.
body:
The Cry Scale is the shared language used throughout the Goodnight House method. It is available from the Cry Scale button in the lower-right corner of the Plan tab.

Coach from the Cry Scale rather than from words like "crying hard" or "crying a lot," and rather than from the parent's anxiety about the crying. Louder crying does not automatically mean more intervention is needed.

The most important thing to assess is whether the crying is sustained or ebbing and flowing. That distinction matters more than volume alone.

**The Cry Scale**

**Level 0 — Silent and still**
The child is likely trying to fall asleep.
Coaching: Do nothing.

**Level 1-2 — Low-intensity crying**
Quiet fussing, grumbling, complaining, or inconsistent crying.
These cries are usually part of the child settling.
Coaching: Wait and watch. Do not intervene.

**Level 3-4 — Medium-intensity crying**
Louder cries, stop-and-start crying, louder grumbling or complaining, and more movement.
The important feature is that the crying still rises and falls. If you're hearing ebb and flow, the child is often working toward calming down. Intervening during this process frequently causes more frustration and interrupts that work.
Coaching: Wait and observe. Do not intervene simply because the crying becomes louder.

**Level 5 — High-intensity crying**
The child's most intense cry.
This is the primary decision point. Children often move briefly into Level 5 before settling back into lower levels, so don't react to the first few seconds. Watch for the overall pattern.
Respond intentionally rather than reacting emotionally.
Reacting is immediately jumping in because the crying feels intense.
Responding is taking a moment to assess what you're hearing and then deciding what to do based on the family's plan.

**Applying the Cry Scale by Stage**

*Stages 1-2*

During Stage 1-2, the Cry Scale is primarily a decision-making tool. It helps parents decide whether it is appropriate to continue waiting and observing or whether the child likely needs more support.

The assistant should coach parents to use the Cry Scale to guide their response but should not introduce timers, check intervals, or other Stage 3 concepts unless those have already been introduced in the family's plan.

*Stages 3 and 4 (Independent Sleep)*

During Stages 3 and 4, the Cry Scale becomes part of the family's method instructions.

For the Gradual Method, the intervention point is sustained Level 5 crying for approximately 5 minutes with no ebb and flow.

For Leave and Checks, parents begin their timer only when the child reaches sustained Level 5. If the crying drops back into ebb and flow, the timer stops. If the child returns to sustained Level 5, the timer starts again. The assistant follows the timing and check intervals outlined in the family's plan.

### Foundations → Sleep Science

*The assistant's sleep-science textbook (Jessie's finalized Sleep Science Knowledge Base), placed whole as one resource. Not parent-facing; the assistant reads it to inform answers in Jessie's method.*

title: Foundational sleep science (the assistant's textbook)
retrieve_when: a parent asks a "why" question about sleep mechanics (sleep cycles and architecture, partial arousals and waking between cycles, sleep associations, sleep pressure and wake windows, overtiredness and the second wind, circadian rhythm, melatonin and light/dark, age-based sleep needs, day sleep vs night sleep, the science of early morning wakings, the three states and falling asleep independently, by-the-clock vs cue-based), or another resource defers to the sleep science.
source: Sleep Science Knowledge Base (finalized version, whole document)
body:
**Sleep Science Knowledge Base**

**THE GOODNIGHT HOUSE · AI REFERENCE DOCUMENT**

**Purpose**

This document gives the Goodnight House Sleep Assistant foundational sleep science to use when answering parent questions.

It is not parent-facing content and it is not app logic. It is a reference document for the assistant.

Use this document for general sleep science topics, including sleep cycles, sleep pressure, wake windows, overtiredness, circadian rhythm, melatonin, night wakings, and early morning wakings.

**How to Use This Document**

Read for understanding, then answer parents in clear, simple language that matches Jessie's method.

Do not copy this document word for word unless the wording is meant to be used directly.

The assistant should not speak as Jessie in the first person. Do not say "I recommend" or "in my experience." Use "Jessie recommends," "Jessie teaches," or "Jessie's approach is."

**Scope**

This document covers foundational sleep science only.

Specific coaching steps, schedules, sleep environment instructions, nutrition guidance, night weaning, naps, and safe sleep guidance belong in their own blocks.

If a topic-specific block gives more specific guidance than this document, use the topic-specific block.

**Topic Index**

1. Sleep cycles and sleep architecture
2. Partial arousals and why babies wake between cycles
3. Sleep associations
4. Sleep pressure and wake windows
5. Overtiredness and the second-wind mechanism
6. Circadian rhythm
7. Melatonin and the role of light and dark
8. Age-based sleep needs
9. Day sleep and night sleep
10. Early morning wakings
11. The three states and falling asleep independently
12. By-the-clock vs. cue-based sleep

**1. Sleep Cycles and Sleep Architecture**

**Core Science**

Sleep happens in cycles.

During the night, a child moves through lighter and deeper stages of sleep over and over. At the end of each cycle, the brain comes back up into very light sleep. This often creates a brief partial waking before the next sleep cycle begins.

Newborn sleep is different from older baby sleep. Newborns have less mature sleep cycles. Around 3 to 5 months, babies go through a normal developmental change often called the four-month sleep regression. Jessie does not prefer the word regression here, because the baby's sleep is not going backward. The baby's sleep is maturing.

Around this age, sleep begins to look more like adult sleep. Babies start moving through more distinct sleep stages, and they begin sleep in a lighter stage instead of dropping quickly into deep newborn sleep.

This explains why many babies who slept well as newborns start waking more often around 4 months. Their sleep did not break. Their sleep matured.

Infant sleep cycles are short, usually around 40 minutes. Adult sleep cycles are closer to 90 minutes. Because babies have shorter cycles, they come into light sleep more often than adults do. Each time they come into light sleep, they may briefly wake.

This is normal sleep. The goal is not to stop all wakings. The goal is to help the child learn how to return to sleep when those normal wakings happen.

**Sleep Stages**

This section is only needed when a parent asks for more detail.

**N1 Sleep**

N1 is very light sleep. It happens as a person moves from awake into sleep. A person is easily woken from this stage.

**N2 Sleep**

N2 is still relatively light sleep and makes up a large portion of the night.

**N3 Sleep**

N3 is deep sleep, also called slow-wave sleep. This is the deepest and most restorative stage of sleep.

Children are harder to wake from N3 sleep. This is why a child may be moved from the car, have a diaper changed, or be transferred into bed without fully waking.

Deep sleep happens mostly in the first part of the night. This is one reason some children sleep solidly for the first few hours and then begin waking more often later in the night.

Sleepwalking and night terrors happen during deep sleep.

**REM Sleep**

REM sleep is the stage associated with vivid dreams and nightmares.

During REM sleep, most skeletal muscles are temporarily inactive, while breathing muscles and eye muscles continue working. This is why REM sleep is associated with rapid eye movement.

Sleep is lighter during REM-heavy parts of the cycle. Normal partial wakings often happen as the brain cycles back up into lighter sleep.

**Why Naps Often End at 30 to 45 Minutes**

A short nap that ends around 30 to 45 minutes is often one sleep cycle.

The baby finishes one sleep cycle, comes into light sleep, and does not connect into the next cycle. This is the same basic process that happens with night wakings.

A baby may wake fully after one nap cycle for several reasons:

- The baby needed help to fall asleep and that help is no longer there.
- There was not enough sleep pressure to carry the baby into another cycle.
- The sleep environment made it harder to stay asleep.
- The baby is still developmentally young and daytime sleep has not consolidated yet.

Short naps are especially common before 5 to 6 months. Day sleep usually consolidates later than night sleep. During the day, babies have less sleep pressure than they do at night, and they do not have the same circadian support for sleep that they have overnight.

A short nap does not automatically mean something is wrong. It usually means the baby completed one sleep cycle and could not yet move into the next one.

The practical fixes belong in the schedule, nap, environment, and independent sleep blocks. This section only explains why short naps happen.

**Why This Matters**

Most night-waking conversations come back to sleep cycles.

Babies are supposed to wake briefly between sleep cycles. These wakings are part of normal sleep. The issue is not that the child wakes. The issue is whether the child knows how to fall back asleep after waking.

This is why Jessie's method focuses on helping the child fall asleep independently. When the child can fall asleep without needing a parent to recreate the same conditions every time, normal partial wakings are less likely to become full night wakings.

This also helps explain the four-month regression. The change in sleep architecture is developmental and permanent, but the rough patch that can come with it is workable.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Why did my baby start waking more around 4 months?
- Is something wrong with my baby's sleep?
- Why does my baby wake between sleep cycles?
- Why does my baby only nap for 30 to 45 minutes?
- Will the four-month regression go away?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Start by reassuring the parent that the baby's sleep is not broken.

Explain that babies wake briefly between sleep cycles because that is how sleep works. Around 4 months, sleep becomes more mature, and those wakings can become more noticeable.

Keep the explanation simple unless the parent asks for more detail. Most parents do not need sleep-stage names. They need to understand that the waking is normal and that the plan is helping the child learn how to return to sleep.

**2. Partial Arousals and Why Babies Wake Between Cycles**

**Core Science**

Everyone wakes briefly between sleep cycles. These brief wakings are called partial arousals.

For an independent sleeper (someone who can fall asleep initially without assistance), a partial arousal is usually very quick. The person wakes just enough to notice that everything is normal and then goes back to sleep. Most people do not remember these wakings in the morning.

Babies have partial arousals too.

The issue is not that a baby wakes between sleep cycles. That is normal. The issue is what the baby needs in order to fall back asleep.

If a baby depends on something outside of the baby's control to fall asleep, partial arousals are more likely to become full wakings. When the baby wakes between cycles, the sleep conditions may feel different from the way they were at bedtime.

Examples:

- The baby fell asleep in a parent's arms and woke in the crib.
- The baby fell asleep while nursing and woke no longer latched.
- The baby fell asleep being rocked and woke in a still crib.
- The baby fell asleep with a pacifier and woke when the pacifier had fallen out.

When this happens, the baby may wake fully and call for help to recreate the conditions that were present at the beginning of sleep.

**Bathtub Analogy**

Jessie often explains it this way:

If an adult fell asleep in bed and then briefly woke between sleep cycles in the bathtub, that would feel startling. The person would not just roll over and go back to sleep. The person would wake fully, get out of the bathtub, and need to get settled again.

That is similar to what happens when a baby falls asleep one way and wakes up in a different situation. The partial arousal becomes a full waking because the conditions are not the same.

**Why This Matters**

The goal is not to stop all wakings between sleep cycles. Those wakings are normal.

The goal is to help the child fall asleep in a way the child can maintain or return to independently.

If a child falls asleep with conditions the child can still access during the night, partial arousals are more likely to stay brief.

If a child falls asleep with something only a parent can provide, the child is more likely to need the parent again during normal night wakings.

This is the foundation of Jessie's method. Sleep work is not teaching a child how to sleep. Sleep is biological. Sleep work is helping the child change the habits around falling asleep so normal partial arousals do not turn into full wakings every time.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Why does my baby wake up as soon as I put the baby down?
- Why does my baby wake every hour or two?
- Why did my baby used to sleep well and now wakes often?
- Is my baby waking from hunger or habit?
- Why does my baby need the same help over and over during the night?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Take blame out of the explanation.

Parents often rocked, nursed, bounced, or held their baby to sleep because it was loving and instinctive. The issue is not that they did something wrong. The issue is that the baby may now expect the same conditions when waking between sleep cycles.

Use the bathtub analogy when helpful. It gives parents a simple way to understand why a baby can become upset when sleep conditions change.

After explaining the science, connect it back to the plan: the work is not to stop normal wakings. The work is to help the child fall asleep in a way the child can return to during the night.

**3. Sleep Associations**

**Core Science**

A sleep association is anything a child connects with falling asleep.

Every child has sleep associations. The goal is not to remove all of them. The goal is to make sure the strongest sleep associations are ones the child can keep, access, or return to without needing a parent to recreate them all night.

Jessie divides sleep associations into two categories: independent and dependent.

**Independent Sleep Associations**

Independent sleep associations are things the child can have without a parent actively doing something.

Examples:

- Dark room
- White noise
- Sleep sack
- Consistent bedtime routine

Independent sleep associations are helpful because they stay consistent. They are present when the child falls asleep, and many of them are still present when the child wakes briefly between sleep cycles.

White noise is a good example because it stays on all night and does not require a parent to restart the sleep process.

A bedtime routine is also helpful because it cues the body and brain that sleep is coming, even though the routine itself does not continue all night.

**Dependent Sleep Associations**

Dependent sleep associations require a parent to actively do something.

Examples:

- Feeding to sleep
- Rocking to sleep
- Bouncing to sleep
- Holding to sleep
- Walking to sleep
- Replacing a pacifier for a child who cannot replace it independently

Dependent sleep associations are not bad. In the newborn stage, they are often normal and appropriate.

The issue is that dependent sleep associations usually cannot continue all night. The feed ends. The rocking stops. The baby is placed in the crib. The parent leaves the room.

When the child wakes between sleep cycles, the child may notice that the conditions are different from the way they were at the beginning of sleep. This can cause a brief partial arousal to become a full waking.

**Pacifier Note**

A pacifier can function differently depending on the child's age and skill.

If a baby needs a parent to replace the pacifier during the night, it is functioning like a dependent sleep association.

If an older baby can find and replace the pacifier independently, it may function more like an independent sleep association.

(See `resource_pacifier_philosophy` and the Pacifier section resources.)

**Habit Loop Note**

Sleep associations are closely connected to sleep habits.

Many habits follow a simple loop:

- Cue
- Routine
- Reward

With sleep, the cue may be a normal partial arousal. The routine may be crying out and receiving help such as feeding, rocking, or replacing the pacifier. The reward is falling back asleep.

Over time, this loop can become automatic.

This section explains the concept. The step-by-step coaching for changing a specific habit belongs in the relevant block.

**Why This Matters**

Jessie's method is not anti-association. It is pro-independent-association.

The goal is not to take away every comfort. The goal is to trade sleep associations that only a parent can provide for sleep associations the child can keep or return to independently.

This is why changing sleep habits is usually done in steps. A parent may gradually reduce the amount of help given at sleep times while still staying supportive and present.

Jessie often frames this like teaching a child to walk. At first, the parent gives more support. Over time, the parent gives less support and more opportunity to practice.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Is it bad that I feed or rock my baby to sleep?
- What is the difference between a helpful sleep association and one that causes wakings?
- Will white noise become a crutch?
- Will the pacifier become a problem?
- Do I have to take away everything my baby likes in order to improve sleep?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Avoid making parents feel blamed.

Feeding, rocking, holding, and bouncing are loving and normal ways to soothe a baby. The issue is not that the parent did something wrong. The issue is whether the child now needs that same help every time the child wakes between sleep cycles.

Use dependent and independent language instead of good and bad sleep props.

Explain that the goal is not to strip away comfort. The goal is to keep helpful sleep cues and gradually reduce the ones the child cannot recreate independently.

**4. Homeostatic Sleep Pressure and Wake Windows**

**Core Science**

A wake window is the amount of time a child can typically handle being awake between sleeps.

Newborns have very short wake windows. As babies grow, they can usually handle longer periods of awake time.

The science underneath wake windows is sleep pressure.

When a person wakes up, adenosine begins to build in the brain. The longer the person is awake, the more sleep pressure builds. Sleep pressure is the body's need for sleep.

Sleep releases sleep pressure.

Jessie often explains sleep pressure like a backpack. While a child is awake, rocks are being added to the backpack. The longer the child stays awake, the heavier the backpack gets. Sleep is what takes the backpack off and empties it.

Babies build sleep pressure faster than adults and need to release it more often. This is why babies need naps during the day.

**The Wake Window Sweet Spot**

Good sleep usually depends on enough sleep pressure, but not too much.

If a child has not been awake long enough, there may not be enough sleep pressure to fall asleep or stay asleep.

If a child is awake too long, sleep pressure can overflow into overtiredness. An overtired child may become overstimulated, wired, fussy, or hard to settle. Overtiredness can make it harder to fall asleep and stay asleep.

The goal is to find the child's sweet spot: enough awake time to build sleep pressure, but not so much that the child tips into overtiredness.

**Evidence Note**

The science of sleep pressure is established. Adenosine builds during awake time, sleep pressure increases the longer a person is awake, and young children need more sleep than adults.

Wake windows are a practical tool built from this science, but exact wake-window times are not a precise evidence-based formula.

There is not one exact wake window that works for every child at every age. Wake-window charts are starting points, not laws.

The most evidence-based reference point is total sleep need by age. Specific wake-window numbers and schedules belong in the schedule blocks.

The assistant should not lead with "wake windows are not evidence-based." If the question comes up, the assistant can explain that sleep pressure is real science, while wake windows are a practical guideline that must be adjusted to the child.

**Micro Naps**

Even a short nap can release some sleep pressure.

A 10-minute car nap may not seem like much, but it can reduce enough sleep pressure that the child is not ready for the next full nap at the usual time.

If a child takes a short car nap close to nap time, the parent may need to wait 30 to 60 minutes before offering the full nap. This gives sleep pressure time to build again.

**Car Nap and Transfer Note**

A car nap can create two separate issues:

- The short nap releases some sleep pressure.
- The transfer may wake the child because the child fell asleep in motion and woke in a different place.

If a parent says the child fell asleep in the car and woke during the transfer to the crib, address both pieces.

The sleep pressure piece explains why the child may not be ready to nap again right away.

The transfer piece connects back to partial arousals and sleep associations.

**Why This Matters**

Sleep pressure is the science behind the schedule.

When parents understand sleep pressure, wake windows make more sense. They are not random rules. They are a way to time sleep so the child is tired enough to sleep, but not so tired that sleep becomes harder.

A child fighting a nap may not have enough sleep pressure yet.

A child who is wired, fussy, or inconsolable may have too much sleep pressure and may be overtired.

This section explains the science. The actual wake-window times, schedule recommendations, and details about when to start the wake window belong in the schedule blocks.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- What is a wake window?
- Why do wake windows matter?
- Why can't I keep my baby up longer so the baby sleeps better?
- Why did a short car nap ruin the next nap?
- Why does my baby fight sleep even when tired?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Use the backpack analogy when helpful.

Explain that sleep pressure needs to build before sleep, but too much awake time can make sleep harder.

Keep the explanation practical. The goal is not to make parents anxious about the clock. The goal is to help them understand why timing matters.

When a parent needs specific wake-window numbers, use the schedule block.

**5. Overtiredness and the Second Wind**

**Core Science**

Overtiredness happens when a child stays awake longer than the child can comfortably handle.

Many parents assume that keeping a baby awake longer will help the baby sleep better. In babies and young children, the opposite is often true. Too much awake time can make a child harder to settle and more likely to wake.

Overtiredness does not always look like sleepiness.

In babies, overtiredness may look like crying, screaming, arching, fussiness, or an inability to settle.

In toddlers, overtiredness may look like hyperactivity, impulsiveness, climbing, running, or emotional meltdowns.

This is one reason babies and young children do not always simply fall asleep when they are tired. They often need sleep offered at the right time, before they become overtired.

**Second Wind**

When a child becomes overtired, the body may respond with stress hormones, including cortisol and adrenaline.

These hormones increase alertness. This can make a tired child look wired instead of sleepy.

This is often called a second wind.

Cortisol can also interfere with the body's ability to settle into sleep. This is part of why an overtired child may fight sleep even though the child needs sleep badly.

A simple parent-facing explanation is:

When a baby gets overtired, the body can release alerting hormones that make the baby feel wired instead of calm. That is why an overtired baby may look more awake, not less awake.

**Deeper Science**

This section is only needed if a parent asks for more detail.

When a child stays awake past the point the body is ready for sleep, the body may read the extended wakefulness as stress. This can activate the sympathetic nervous system and the HPA axis.

The sympathetic nervous system is involved in alertness and fight-or-flight responses.

The HPA axis is one of the body's main stress-response systems.

When these systems are activated, the child may have more cortisol and adrenaline. This can increase heart rate, motor activity, emotional reactivity, and difficulty settling.

Lost sleep also affects emotional regulation. Young children already have immature regulation skills, and overtiredness makes regulation harder. This is one reason an overtired toddler may look hyperactive, impulsive, or emotionally volatile instead of sleepy.

**Evidence Note**

The general science behind overtiredness is supported by research on sleep loss, stress-system activation, sympathetic activity, cortisol, and emotional regulation in children.

For the evidence record, related sources include:

- Raikkonen et al., Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 2010
- Franco et al., Sleep, 2003
- Berger et al., Journal of Sleep Research, 2012
- Miller et al., Journal of Sleep Research, 2015
- Maski and Kothare, 2013
- Maski and Owens, Lancet Neurology, 2016

The assistant should not recite these sources to parents unless asked. Use them only as background support.

**Overtiredness vs. Undertiredness**

Overtiredness and undertiredness can look similar.

Both may cause:

- Fighting sleep
- Crying at bedtime or nap time
- Short naps
- Night wakings
- Early morning wakings
- Restlessness

Because the symptoms overlap, the assistant should not assume that a wired or upset child is automatically overtired.

The difference is usually found by looking at sleep data over time, including wake windows, nap lengths, total daytime sleep, bedtime, night wakings, and morning wake time.

The practical work of deciding whether to increase or decrease awake time belongs in the schedule block.

**Medical Note**

Chronic overtiredness can sometimes mimic or worsen symptoms that look like something else.

For example, an overtired baby may seem uncomfortable or unsettled in a way that parents may describe as reflux-like. An overtired child may look hyperactive or dysregulated.

This does not mean medical symptoms should be dismissed. If symptoms seem medical, severe, persistent, or concerning, the assistant should route the parent to the appropriate medical guidance or pediatrician.

**Why This Matters**

Overtiredness is one reason schedule timing matters.

The goal is not to keep a child awake until the child crashes. The goal is to offer sleep before the child becomes too overtired to settle well.

This section explains what overtiredness is and why it can create a second wind.

The coaching for holding the schedule, adjusting wake windows, and deciding whether a child is overtired or undertired belongs in the schedule block.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Why is my baby more hyper when tired?
- Why does my baby seem wired at bedtime?
- If I keep my baby up longer, won't the baby sleep better?
- Why does my overtired baby fight sleep?
- How can my baby be exhausted but still not fall asleep?
- Is my baby overtired or undertired?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Start by naming that overtiredness is counterintuitive.

Many parents try keeping a child awake longer because they assume the child will sleep harder. Reassure them that this is a common mistake and that baby sleep often works the opposite way.

Give the simple explanation first: too much awake time can cause the body to release alerting hormones, which makes the child feel wired instead of calm.

Do not over-explain the deeper science unless the parent asks.

Then connect the explanation back to the plan: timing matters because the goal is to offer sleep before the child tips into overtiredness.

**6. Circadian Rhythm**

**Core Science**

The circadian rhythm is the body's internal clock.

It helps regulate when the body feels awake and when the body feels ready for sleep. It also affects body temperature, hormone patterns, alertness, and sleep timing.

Light is one of the strongest signals for the circadian rhythm.

When light enters the eyes, it sends information to the brain that helps set the body clock. Bright light tells the body it is daytime and supports alertness. Darkness tells the body that sleep is coming.

Newborns have an immature circadian rhythm. They do not have a well-developed sense of day and night yet. This is one reason newborn sleep can feel unpredictable, and why newborns may sleep in bright or noisy environments more easily than older babies.

Around 12 weeks, the circadian rhythm becomes more mature. At that point, light, darkness, and consistent timing begin to matter much more for sleep.

This is why a bright sleep space may not affect a newborn very much, but can start to affect sleep more as the baby gets older.

**Light and Consistency**

Once the circadian rhythm matures, light and consistency are two of the strongest ways to support it.

Morning light helps tell the body that the day has started.

Darkness during sleep times helps tell the body that it is time to sleep.

Consistent wake times, nap times, and bedtimes help the body predict when sleep should happen.

The goal is not to force a baby or toddler onto an adult sleep rhythm. Babies and young children have higher sleep needs than adults. The goal is to support the child's body clock while still protecting the amount of sleep the child needs.

For example, an early sunrise does not mean a toddler should start the day at 4:00 a.m. The schedule should still protect the child's sleep needs.

**Why This Matters**

Circadian rhythm is the science behind several parts of Jessie's method, including:

- Morning light
- Consistent morning wake time
- Consistent nap timing
- Consistent bedtime
- Darkness during sleep

When parents understand the body clock, consistency feels less random. The goal is to help the child's body know when to be awake and when to sleep.

This section explains the science of the body clock.

The specific coaching for morning wake time, schedules, morning sunlight, and dark sleep spaces belongs in the schedule and environment blocks.

Melatonin is covered separately in the melatonin section.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- What is a circadian rhythm?
- Why does morning sunlight matter?
- Why does my newborn have days and nights mixed up?
- Why does my baby suddenly need a darker room?
- Does my baby need to sleep from sunset to sunrise?
- Why does consistency matter so much?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Explain circadian rhythm as the child's internal clock.

Keep the explanation simple: light helps tell the body it is daytime, and darkness helps tell the body sleep is coming.

For newborns, reassure parents that the body clock is still immature. Day and night confusion can be normal early on.

For older babies and toddlers, explain that light, darkness, and consistent timing help set the body clock.

Correct the sunset-to-sunrise idea gently. Children need a rhythm that supports their body clock and protects their sleep needs.

**7. Melatonin and the Role of Light and Dark**

**Core Science**

Melatonin is a hormone that helps signal to the body that sleep is coming.

Melatonin is released in response to darkness. Light suppresses melatonin and tells the brain it is time to be awake.

When it gets dark, the brain signals the pineal gland to release melatonin. Melatonin helps the body prepare for sleep by supporting drowsiness, muscle relaxation, and a lower body temperature.

Melatonin usually begins rising in the evening, continues through the night, and peaks during the middle of the night or early morning hours.

Light has the opposite effect. Light tells the brain it is daytime and suppresses melatonin. This is why light exposure in the early morning can help set the body clock and signal that the day has started.

This is also why darkness matters for sleep. Even small amounts of light can make it harder for some children to fall asleep or stay asleep.

Babies and young children are also easily distracted by what they can see. A dark room removes visual stimulation and supports the body's natural sleep signals.

**Screens Before Bed**

Screens can interfere with sleep because they expose the eyes to bright light, including blue-enriched light.

This light can suppress melatonin and make it harder for the body to wind down.

It is usually best to avoid screens for at least one hour before sleep.

**Morning Light**

Morning sunlight is helpful because it tells the body that the day has started.

This supports the circadian rhythm and helps the body know when to be awake and when to sleep later that day.

Morning light and dark sleep times work together. Light sets the daytime rhythm. Darkness supports sleep.

**Why This Matters**

Melatonin is one reason Jessie emphasizes a dark sleep space.

A dark room supports melatonin release, reduces visual stimulation, and helps the child's body understand that it is time to sleep.

This section explains the melatonin science.

The specific coaching for darkening a room, the hand test, blackout options, nightlights, light-diet questions, and product recommendations belongs in the sleep environment block.

The broader body-clock science belongs in the circadian rhythm section.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Why does the room need to be so dark?
- Is a nightlight okay?
- What is melatonin?
- How does light affect sleep?
- Why are screens before bed a problem?
- Does morning sunlight matter?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Explain that melatonin is one of the body's sleep signals.

Darkness supports melatonin. Light suppresses it.

A dark room is not about being extreme. It is about supporting the child's biology and removing visual stimulation.

For specific room-darkening steps, use the sleep environment block.

**8. Age-Based Sleep Needs and How They Change**

**Core Science**

A child's sleep needs change as the child grows.

Younger babies build sleep pressure quickly, so they can only handle short periods of awake time before needing sleep again. This is why newborns need frequent naps.

As babies grow, sleep pressure builds more slowly. They can stay awake longer, which usually leads to longer wake windows and fewer naps over time.

This is why nap needs change across the first few years. A child gradually moves from several naps a day, to fewer naps, and eventually to no naps.

Nap transitions are not perfectly predictable. There are common age ranges and signs, but no exact day when every child is ready to drop a nap.

It is also normal for a baby to move back and forth between nap schedules during a transition. For example, a baby may need two naps some days and three naps other days for a while.

**Total Sleep Needs**

Total sleep need by age is the most evidence-based reference point.

The American Academy of Sleep Medicine gives recommended sleep ranges by age. These ranges are wide, and that matters. A child may fall on the higher or lower end of the range and still be normal.

Some children genuinely have lower sleep needs. This is different from being chronically overtired.

A lower-sleep-needs child may be rested, growing well, developing well, and functioning well on less sleep than another child the same age.

An overtired child may be getting less sleep than needed and showing signs such as frequent night wakings, early morning wakings, short naps, difficulty settling, or daytime dysregulation.

The difference usually becomes clearer by looking at the child's sleep data and overall behavior over time.

**Why This Matters**

Age-based sleep needs explain why schedules change as a child grows.

The schedule should not be treated as one-size-fits-all. Age gives a starting point, but the child's actual sleep patterns and response matter too.

This section explains why sleep needs change, why naps consolidate, why nap transitions can be unpredictable, and why the healthy range is wide.

The actual sleep totals, nap counts, wake-window ranges, and sample schedules belong in the schedule block.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- How much sleep does my child need?
- When will my baby drop a nap?
- Why is my baby switching between two naps and three naps?
- What if my child seems to need less sleep than the charts say?
- How do I know if my child is low sleep needs or overtired?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Reassure parents that changing sleep needs are normal.

Explain that as children grow, they can usually handle more awake time and need fewer naps.

Be honest that nap transitions are not exact. A child may move back and forth for a while before fully dropping a nap.

When parents worry about whether their child is getting enough sleep, explain that healthy sleep ranges are wide. Then use the schedule block and the child's sleep data to decide whether the child seems rested, overtired, or ready for a schedule adjustment.

**9. Day Sleep and Night Sleep**

**Core Science**

Day sleep and night sleep are connected.

How a child sleeps during the day can affect how the child sleeps at night.

Good daytime sleep usually protects night sleep. This is because missed naps or poor naps can lead to overtiredness by bedtime. An overtired child often has a harder time falling asleep, staying asleep, and sleeping later in the morning.

Keeping a baby awake all day does not usually lead to better night sleep. It often backfires.

**Amount and Timing**

Both the amount of day sleep and the timing of day sleep matter.

A child needs enough daytime sleep to avoid becoming overtired.

A child also needs enough awake time before bedtime to build sleep pressure for the night.

Common problems include:

- Too little daytime sleep, which can cause overtiredness by bedtime.
- Too much late-day sleep, which can make it harder for the child to be ready for bedtime.
- Day sleep that happens too early, which can leave the child with a long overtired stretch before bed.
- A late nap that ends too close to bedtime, which can reduce sleep pressure and make bedtime harder.

The goal is balanced day sleep. The child needs enough sleep during the day to stay regulated, but not so much late sleep that bedtime becomes difficult.

**Does Day Sleep Steal From Night Sleep?**

Day sleep can affect night sleep, but it is not as simple as "less nap sleep equals more night sleep."

For many babies and toddlers, cutting naps too aggressively leads to overtiredness and worse nights.

At the same time, too much daytime sleep or a nap that runs too late can reduce sleep pressure before bedtime.

The answer depends on the child's age, total sleep needs, nap timing, bedtime, and night sleep patterns.

**Why This Matters**

This is why Jessie's method looks at the whole 24-hour sleep pattern, not just the night.

Naps, wake windows, bedtime, night wakings, and morning wake time all affect each other.

This section explains why day sleep and night sleep are connected.

The specific coaching for nap timing, nap caps, early bedtime, missed naps, and schedule adjustments belongs in the schedule block.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Should I cut my baby's nap so the baby sleeps better at night?
- Why does my baby sleep worse at night after bad naps?
- Can too much daytime sleep cause night wakings?
- Why did a late nap make bedtime harder?
- Why does nap timing matter?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Start with the main point: good day sleep usually protects night sleep.

Explain that keeping a baby awake longer does not usually make the baby sleep better. It often creates overtiredness.

Then explain that timing matters too. The child needs enough daytime sleep to avoid overtiredness, but enough awake time before bed to build sleep pressure.

For the actual schedule fix, use the family's schedule and the schedule resources (see `resource_early_bedtime_framework` and `resource_troubleshooting_naps`).

**10. Early Morning Wakings**

**Core Science**

Early morning wakings are wakings that happen before the desired morning wake time, often before 6:00 a.m.

Early morning wakings are common and can be difficult to resolve because sleep pressure is low in the early morning.

By the early morning hours, the child has already slept most of the night. Much of the sleep pressure from the day before has already been released. Because there is less sleep pressure left, it is easier for the child to wake fully and harder for the child to fall back asleep.

Early morning sleep is also lighter than sleep earlier in the night. Because of this, small disruptions can have a bigger effect.

Common early morning disruptions include:

- Light entering the room
- Household noise
- Early morning hunger
- A wet or dirty diaper
- Temperature changes
- Overtiredness from the previous day
- A schedule that is not supporting enough sleep pressure at the right times

**Melatonin, Cortisol, and Light**

Melatonin is usually tapering down in the early morning.

Cortisol naturally begins to rise in the early morning to help the body wake up.

This means the child's body chemistry is already moving toward awake.

Light matters a lot during this window. Even a small amount of early morning light can signal to the brain that the day has started. This can make it harder for the child to return to sleep.

This is one reason a truly dark room is especially important for early morning wakings.

**Sleep Pressure Example**

If a child sleeps from 7:00 p.m. to 5:00 a.m., the child has already had 10 hours of night sleep.

That does not mean 5:00 a.m. is the desired wake time. It does mean there may not be much sleep pressure left to help the child fall back asleep easily.

This is why a child may resettle more easily at 2:00 a.m. than at 5:00 a.m.

**Overtiredness and Early Wakings**

Overtiredness can also contribute to early morning wakings.

A child may become overtired from short naps, missed naps, a long final wake window, or a bedtime that was too late.

Overtiredness can make sleep more restless and can increase early morning waking.

Because of this, pushing bedtime later is not always the right fix. For many children, a later bedtime creates more overtiredness and makes early waking worse.

**Why This Matters**

Early morning wakings often have more than one cause.

They may involve low sleep pressure, lighter sleep, decreasing melatonin, rising cortisol, light exposure, noise, schedule issues, or overtiredness.

This is why early morning wakings often take consistency and time to improve.

This section explains the science behind early morning wakings.

The specific coaching for how to respond, how to protect the desired wake time, how to handle the first nap, how to adjust bedtime, and how to troubleshoot the schedule belongs in the schedule block and plan flow.

The room-darkening steps belong in the sleep environment block.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Why does my baby wake at 5:00 a.m. every day?
- Why is early morning waking so hard to fix?
- Why will my baby resettle at 2:00 a.m. but not at 5:00 a.m.?
- Will a later bedtime fix early waking?
- Why does light matter so much in the early morning?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Normalize early morning wakings. They are common and often rooted in biology.

Explain that by the early morning, sleep pressure is low, sleep is lighter, melatonin is decreasing, and the body is starting to move toward awake.

Be honest that early wakings often take time and consistency to improve.

Do not promise a quick fix.

Gently correct the idea that a later bedtime always helps. Sometimes it does, but often it creates more overtiredness and makes the early waking worse.

For the actual response plan and schedule adjustments, use the schedule block.

**11. The Three States and Falling Asleep Independently**

**Core Science**

How a child falls asleep at the beginning of sleep often affects what the child expects during normal wakings between sleep cycles.

If a parent helps the child all the way to sleep, the child may need that same help again when waking between cycles.

Independent sleep means the child is able to finish the process of falling asleep without the parent doing it for the child.

This does not mean the child is unsupported. It means the child is given the opportunity to practice falling asleep with less parent help over time.

**The Three States**

The three states describe where a child is when being laid down.

**Awake and Aware**

The child is clearly awake.

Signs may include:

- Eyes open
- Looking around
- Moving
- Babbling
- Aware of being placed in the crib or sleep space

This is the eventual goal for independent sleep. A child who is laid down awake and aware is able to do the final work of falling asleep.

**Relaxed and Heavy**

The child is calm and close to sleep, but not fully asleep.

Signs may include:

- Heavy eyes
- Fluttering eyes
- Relaxed body
- Limp limbs
- Drifting in and out

This can be a bridge during gradual sleep work, especially early in the process. It is not the final goal and should not be counted as fully independent sleep.

**Asleep or Nearly Asleep**

The child is already asleep or almost asleep before being laid down.

Signs may include:

- Eyes closed
- Limp body
- No real awareness of being transferred
- Little or no active work left to do

This means the parent has done most or all of the work of getting the child to sleep. The child is more likely to need the same help again during night wakings.

**Drowsy But Awake Note**

Jessie does not use "drowsy but awake" as the target for sleep work.

The phrase is too vague and means different things to different parents.

When the plan reaches the stage of laying a child down awake, the target is awake and aware, not nearly asleep.

If a child is laid down very drowsy, the child may still experience the final part of falling asleep as parent-led. When the child wakes between cycles, the child may still need help recreating that same sleepy state.

**Timing Note**

Laying a child down fully awake is the destination, not always the starting point.

In Jessie's method, more help may be appropriate early in the process. The plan gradually reduces that help as the child builds the skill of falling asleep more independently.

For newborns, gentle practice may look different. A parent may lay the baby down calm and drowsy, then help the baby the rest of the way with patting or soothing. Over time, the parent may lay the baby down slightly more awake.

That newborn foundation-building is different from later sleep work, where the goal becomes laying the child down fully awake.

**Independent Play Note**

Independent play can support this same general skill.

A child who practices being content and calm without constant parent interaction during the day may have an easier time practicing independence at bedtime.

This does not replace the sleep plan. It is a supportive parenting strategy.

Specific independent play coaching belongs in the parenting strategies block.

**Why This Matters**

Falling asleep independently is one of the main skills that helps normal partial arousals stay brief.

When a child can fall asleep from an awake and aware state at bedtime, the child is more likely to be able to return to sleep during normal wakings between cycles.

This section explains the three states and why independent sleep matters.

The step-by-step coaching for transfers, gradual reduction, the chair method, checks, and nightly progression belongs in the plan flow and method-specific blocks.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- What does drowsy but awake mean?
- Why does my baby need me to get the baby all the way to sleep?
- Why does my child wake and need the same help again?
- How do I help my baby learn to fall asleep independently?
- Why does independent play matter for sleep?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Use the three states to help parents understand where their child is right now.

Clarify that the goal is not to remove support all at once. The goal is to gradually give the child more opportunity to practice the final part of falling asleep.

If a parent asks about "drowsy but awake," explain that Jessie does not use that as the sleep-work target because it is unclear. When the plan reaches independent sleep work, awake and aware is the goal.

For the exact steps, use the plan flow or method-specific block.

**12. By-the-Clock vs. Cue-Based Sleep**

**Core Science**

There are two broad ways to time a child's sleep:

- By the clock
- By sleepy cues

By-the-clock sleep means sleep happens at consistent times each day.

Cue-based sleep means the parent watches the child for sleepy signs and offers sleep based on those cues.

Jessie's approach is by-the-clock after about 4 months of age.

This is because consistent timing helps support the child's circadian rhythm. A consistent morning wake time helps set the day's rhythm. From there, naps and bedtime can become more predictable.

A predictable rhythm also helps the child's body know when to be awake and when to sleep.

**Sleepy Cues**

Sleepy cues can be helpful, especially in the newborn stage.

After the newborn stage, sleepy cues can become less reliable. Some children do not show clear sleepy signs until they are already overtired. Other signs, such as fussiness, rubbing eyes, or zoning out, can also happen for reasons besides tiredness.

This is why Jessie uses the clock as the main guide after about 4 months.

The goal is not to ignore the child. The goal is to use the clock as the primary guide and the child's cues as secondary information.

Jessie often describes this as one eye on the baby and one eye on the clock.

**Why This Matters**

A by-the-clock schedule gives the child's body a predictable rhythm.

It also gives parents a clear plan instead of asking them to read cues and calculate wake windows all day, especially while tired and in the middle of sleep work.

This section explains the basic science behind Jessie's by-the-clock preference.

The full coaching for how tightly to hold the schedule, how to handle naps, how to use "asleep by" times, and how to adjust the schedule belongs in the schedule block.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Should I follow a schedule or watch sleepy cues?
- Why does Jessie use set times?
- Isn't cue-based sleep more natural?
- Should I ignore my baby's sleepy cues?
- When do sleepy cues matter?

**How to Explain This to Parents**

Validate that cue-based sleep is a real approach, especially for newborns.

Then explain why Jessie's method uses the clock after about 4 months: consistency helps regulate the body clock, and sleepy cues can be unclear or come too late.

The clock leads, and cues give extra context.

For the full schedule guidance, use the schedule block.

### Stage 1 → Safe Sleep

title: Foundational sleep science (the assistant's textbook) — Sleep Science Knowledge Base
body:
**Sleep Science Knowledge Base**

**THE GOODNIGHT HOUSE · AI REFERENCE DOCUMENT**

**Purpose**

This document gives the Goodnight House Sleep Assistant foundational sleep science to use when answering parent questions.

It is not parent-facing content and it is not app logic. It is a reference document for the assistant.

Use this document for general sleep science topics, including sleep cycles, sleep pressure, wake windows, overtiredness, circadian rhythm, melatonin, night wakings, and early morning wakings.

**How to Use This Document**

Read for understanding, then answer parents in clear, simple language that matches Jessie's method.

Do not copy this document word for word unless the wording is meant to be used directly.

The assistant should not speak as Jessie in the first person. Do not say "I recommend" or "in my experience." Use "Jessie recommends," "Jessie teaches," or "Jessie's approach is."

**Scope**

This document covers foundational sleep science only.

Specific coaching steps, schedules, sleep environment instructions, nutrition guidance, night weaning, naps, and safe sleep guidance belong in their own blocks.

If a topic-specific block gives more specific guidance than this document, use the topic-specific block.

**Topic Index**

1. Sleep cycles and sleep architecture
2. Partial arousals and why babies wake between cycles
3. Sleep associations
4. Sleep pressure and wake windows
5. Overtiredness and the second-wind mechanism
6. Circadian rhythm
7. Melatonin and the role of light and dark
8. Age-based sleep needs
9. Day sleep and night sleep
10. Early morning wakings
11. The three states and falling asleep independently
12. By-the-clock vs. cue-based sleep

**1. Sleep Cycles and Sleep Architecture**

**Core Science**

Sleep happens in cycles.

During the night, a child moves through lighter and deeper stages of sleep over and over. At the end of each cycle, the brain comes back up into very light sleep. This often creates a brief partial waking before the next sleep cycle begins.

Newborn sleep is different from older baby sleep. Newborns have less mature sleep cycles. Around 3 to 5 months, babies go through a normal developmental change often called the four-month sleep regression. Jessie does not prefer the word regression here, because the baby's sleep is not going backward. The baby's sleep is maturing.

Around this age, sleep begins to look more like adult sleep. Babies start moving through more distinct sleep stages, and they begin sleep in a lighter stage instead of dropping quickly into deep newborn sleep.

This explains why many babies who slept well as newborns start waking more often around 4 months. Their sleep did not break. Their sleep matured.

Infant sleep cycles are short, usually around 40 minutes. Adult sleep cycles are closer to 90 minutes. Because babies have shorter cycles, they come into light sleep more often than adults do. Each time they come into light sleep, they may briefly wake.

This is normal sleep. The goal is not to stop all wakings. The goal is to help the child learn how to return to sleep when those normal wakings happen.

**Sleep Stages**

This section is only needed when a parent asks for more detail.

**N1 Sleep**

N1 is very light sleep. It happens as a person moves from awake into sleep. A person is easily woken from this stage.

**N2 Sleep**

N2 is still relatively light sleep and makes up a large portion of the night.

title: Foundational sleep science (the assistant's textbook) — N3 Sleep
body:
**N3 Sleep**

N3 is deep sleep, also called slow-wave sleep. This is the deepest and most restorative stage of sleep.

Children are harder to wake from N3 sleep. This is why a child may be moved from the car, have a diaper changed, or be transferred into bed without fully waking.

Deep sleep happens mostly in the first part of the night. This is one reason some children sleep solidly for the first few hours and then begin waking more often later in the night.

Sleepwalking and night terrors happen during deep sleep.

**REM Sleep**

REM sleep is the stage associated with vivid dreams and nightmares.

During REM sleep, most skeletal muscles are temporarily inactive, while breathing muscles and eye muscles continue working. This is why REM sleep is associated with rapid eye movement.

Sleep is lighter during REM-heavy parts of the cycle. Normal partial wakings often happen as the brain cycles back up into lighter sleep.

**Why Naps Often End at 30 to 45 Minutes**

A short nap that ends around 30 to 45 minutes is often one sleep cycle.

The baby finishes one sleep cycle, comes into light sleep, and does not connect into the next cycle. This is the same basic process that happens with night wakings.

A baby may wake fully after one nap cycle for several reasons:

- The baby needed help to fall asleep and that help is no longer there.
- There was not enough sleep pressure to carry the baby into another cycle.
- The sleep environment made it harder to stay asleep.
- The baby is still developmentally young and daytime sleep has not consolidated yet.

Short naps are especially common before 5 to 6 months. Day sleep usually consolidates later than night sleep. During the day, babies have less sleep pressure than they do at night, and they do not have the same circadian support for sleep that they have overnight.

A short nap does not automatically mean something is wrong. It usually means the baby completed one sleep cycle and could not yet move into the next one.

The practical fixes belong in the schedule, nap, environment, and independent sleep blocks. This section only explains why short naps happen.

**Why This Matters**

Most night-waking conversations come back to sleep cycles.

Babies are supposed to wake briefly between sleep cycles. These wakings are part of normal sleep. The issue is not that the child wakes. The issue is whether the child knows how to fall back asleep after waking.

This is why Jessie's method focuses on helping the child fall asleep independently. When the child can fall asleep without needing a parent to recreate the same conditions every time, normal partial wakings are less likely to become full night wakings.

This also helps explain the four-month regression. The change in sleep architecture is developmental and permanent, but the rough patch that can come with it is workable.

**Parent Questions This Helps Answer**

- Why did my baby start waking more around 4 months?
- Is something wrong with my baby's sleep?
- Why does my baby wake between sleep cycles?
- Why does my baby only nap for 30 to 45 minutes?
- Will the four-month regression go away?
