Jessie uses OK-to-wake clocks with children as early as 8 months old, and they catch on surprisingly fast. They work well for babies, toddlers, and preschoolers, and they grow with the child. Set it to the child's wake times (the end of naps and morning wake). Jessie prefers a WiFi/Bluetooth-free option to reduce EMFs in the sleep environment.

**What it helps with.** An OK-to-wake clock helps with a few common troubles:

- Early mornings. The most common use. It gives a child who is too young to understand time a signal for when it's sleep time and when it's time to wake up.
- Night wakings. It also signals when it is still time to be asleep. A child who wakes curious whether it is morning gets a visual reminder to lie back down.
- Time changes. It helps keep a child in bed through daylight savings shifts while their body clock adjusts to the new pattern.
- Naps and rest time. It answers "is nap over?" and "is rest time over?" on its own.

**How to actually use it.** Introduce the child to the clock first: let them look at it and get used to it, and explain how it works, rather than having it just appear in their room one night. The simplest setup: steady white noise overnight with no light, then a distinct wake signal, a gentle sound plus a green light, at the designated wake time. Green is the common "okay to get up" convention. The key move is to go into the room the moment the signal turns on and make a big, celebratory deal of it: cheer that it is time to wake up, and praise how well they slept or how patiently they waited. After several mornings in a row, the child starts to make the association. Many parents notice their child stays quiet until the signal, then starts talking or calling out, even at a young age.

**Consistency is everything.** If the signal turns green but the parent takes fifteen minutes to come in, the child stops believing the clock and it loses its meaning. To be fair to the child and help them succeed, the parent has to honor the signal promptly and consistently. The clock only works if the parent is consistent.

**For older toddlers and preschoolers.** If a child is old enough to understand, explain the boundaries up front and practice during the day before relying on it at night. Role-play it: green light means hop up, the nighttime sound means still sleeping, pretend to be asleep. For a child in a big-kid bed, the rule is they can get up or call out when the light turns green, and if it is still the nighttime setting, it is still nighttime. An older toddler is fully capable of this with a little practice.

**Note on the Hatch Rest.** Jessie's first recommendation is no longer the Hatch Rest, because of its WiFi/Bluetooth capabilities and the EMF proximity to the child; she now prefers the low-EMF OK-to-wake clock. That said, if a parent asks specifically about the Hatch, it is fine to recommend it with that caveat, since the strategy works with any OK-to-wake clock. The recommendation change is about EMF, not about the method.

**Product.** Jessie loves a low-EMF OK-to-wake clock option, around $20. Offer the affiliate link.

title: Nightlights
retrieve_when: a parent asks about nightlights or about nightmares affecting sleep. (For fear of the dark specifically, see resource_fear_of_the_dark.)
body: Most children do not need a nightlight, and Jessie discourages any nightlight before age two. Many parents worry about fear of the dark or nightmares, but children do not typically start having nightmares until they can imagine things; true nightmares become more plausible from around 18 months to 2.5 years and are much more common from about age 3, depending on the child (see resource_nightmares). If an older toddler genuinely wants or needs a nightlight (for comfort, or to see for potty trips once in a big-kid bed, or for a child with vision needs), use a warm red tone at very low brightness. Avoid cool or blue-toned light, which suppresses melatonin, the sleep hormone.

title: Room sharing with a parent: the visual barrier and why it helps
retrieve_when: a parent asks how to make room sharing work, or about setting up a crib in their own room.
body:
**Sharing a room with a parent.** The dark sleep space and white noise still apply when a child shares a room with a parent. Many families room-share by choice or because of space, and that is completely fine. When the child will sleep in their own crib in the parent's room going forward, the most helpful addition is a visual barrier between the parent's bed and the crib. Jessie's favorite is a SlumberPod over a pack n play, or a cheap garment rack (about $10 at Target or Walmart) with a sheet hung over it. It needs to be positioned out of the child's reach so they cannot pull it down on themselves or pull the sheet into the crib.

**Why the barrier helps.** This sometimes feels unkind to parents, and it helps to explain the thought process behind it. Children wake briefly between sleep cycles all night long, which is completely normal. In those half-awake moments, if the child looks over and sees the parent, it can be stimulating enough to pull them fully awake, essentially "there are mom and dad, I want to be with them." A visual barrier removes that distraction so the child has a better chance of getting through the brief waking and settling back into sleep on their own. The barrier protects the child's sleep. The child stays right there beside the parent; it just removes the visual distraction. Frame it warmly as a kindness that sets the child up to sleep better.

title: Sibling room sharing: get sleep solid before combining kids
retrieve_when: a parent asks about moving siblings into one room, or about combining kids while sleep work is in progress.
body: Most important for this app: the child currently getting sleep support should be sleeping well before the family tries to combine kids into one room. Do not rush room-sharing on top of active sleep work. (For the full how-to once a family is ready, see the sibling room sharing guidance in Troubleshooting.)

title: Sensory support for sleep (filling the sensory cup)
retrieve_when: a parent asks about sensory needs and sleep, calming or regulating an active or dysregulated child for sleep, or sensory strategies before bed.
source: Getting Started Guide ("fill the sensory cup" section)
body:
**Scope (assistant-facing).** This is sleep support, not medical advice and not treatment for a sensory processing disorder. The assistant offers these as general regulation strategies only. If a parent suspects a sensory processing disorder, or the child has a diagnosed one, the assistant does not coach around it; it warmly points the family to their pediatrician, and to a physical or occupational therapist as appropriate.

Some children sleep more soundly when their sensory needs have been met throughout the day. While these strategies are not a treatment for sensory processing disorders, many families find them helpful:

- Heavy-work activities throughout the day and before bedtime, such as bear crawls, pushing a laundry basket, wall pushes, tug-of-war, or carrying heavy items.
- A warm bath before bed. Some families find Epsom salt baths helpful a few times per week.
- Low lighting before bedtime.
- Limiting screens, especially within two hours of bedtime.
- Deep-pressure activities such as the "burrito game" (rolling your child snugly in a blanket) or the "steam roller game" (gently rolling a therapy ball over your child's back and legs).
- Blowing bubbles, singing, calm music, or other regulating activities before bed.
- Crunchy or cold snacks, or drinks through a straw earlier in the evening.

Sensory support is often most effective when it's incorporated throughout the day, not just at bedtime.

title: Fear of the dark (toddlers and preschoolers)
retrieve_when: a parent mentions that their child seems afraid of the dark, resists being alone in their room because of the dark, or expresses fear at bedtime.
body:
**Surface this only when the parent brings it up. Do not introduce this topic unless the parent raises it.**

**Jessie's philosophy.** Fear of the dark is a normal developmental stage for many toddlers and preschoolers. As imagination develops, children become capable of imagining things that aren't actually present. That wonderful imagination during the day can sometimes create fears at night. Treat the fear as real, even if there is nothing to be afraid of. Validate briefly, offer confidence, and provide practical tools. Avoid lengthy reassurance conversations or repeatedly checking for monsters, as these can unintentionally reinforce the fear.

**Children under 24 months.** For babies and younger toddlers, fear of the dark is not typically developmentally expected. If a parent believes their child under 24 months is afraid of the dark, gently reassure them that something else is much more likely contributing to bedtime struggles, such as:
- Overtiredness
- Hunger
- A sleep association
- Separation from a parent
- Needing additional comfort

Continue recommending a dark sleep environment while helping the parent troubleshoot the underlying cause.

**Practical strategies.** Match the strategy to the child's age. The gentler tools below suit any child old enough for a genuine fear of the dark (roughly 2 years and up). The imagination-based tools that follow rely on pretend play and a sense of using "tools," which younger toddlers can't meaningfully use — save those for children around 3.5 years (about 42 months) and older.

*Nightlight.* A small nightlight is a reasonable exception to Jessie's usual recommendation for a dark sleep space when a child has a genuine fear of the dark. Choose a dim amber or red nightlight rather than a bright blue-white light. Jessie's favorite is the Circadian Night Light from The Healthy Home, which is specifically designed to support healthy sleep and melatonin production; offer the affiliate code (see resource_affiliate_links).

*Brave stuffy.* Invite the child to help a favorite stuffed animal who feels nervous about sleeping in the dark. Many children enjoy stepping into the role of helper or protector, which can gently shift their focus away from their own worries.

*Gradual exposure.* If a child has been sleeping with bright lights on or the bedroom door fully open, avoid making the room completely dark all at once. Instead, gradually dim the room or close the door a little more every few nights until you reach the family's long-term goal.

**Imagination-based strategies — for children around 3.5 years (about 42 months) and older only.** The tools below rely on pretend rituals and imaginative play. They don't land for a younger toddler, and "monster spray" in particular can introduce the idea of monsters to a child who hasn't raised it. Do not suggest these for a 2-year-old. (This age sits above the app's current range, so within the standard plan these generally won't come up.)

*Flashlight.* Placing a small flashlight beside the bed can help an older child feel more confident if they wake overnight.

*Flashlight scavenger hunt.* Before bedtime, hide a few small toys or stuffed animals around the bedroom. Dim the lights and let the child find them using a flashlight. This can help build positive experiences in the dark and make the bedroom feel less intimidating.

*Monster spray or brave spray.* Some families enjoy making a simple "monster spray" or "brave spray" using water in a small spray bottle (and lavender if they already use essential oils). This isn't presented as proof that monsters exist. Instead, it's simply a playful bedtime ritual that can help some children feel more confident.

**Coaching parents.** Validate the child's feelings without amplifying the fear. Examples:
- "I know the dark can feel scary sometimes."
- "Your room is safe."
- "Mom and Dad are nearby."

Stay calm, warm, and confident. Avoid lengthy reassurance conversations, repeated monster checks, or changing the bedtime routine significantly, as these can unintentionally reinforce the fear.

**When to refer.** If the fear seems significantly more intense than typical bedtime fears, interferes with daily life, or appears to be part of broader anxiety, encourage the family to discuss their concerns with their pediatrician. Evaluation and treatment of childhood anxiety are outside the scope of Jessie's sleep consulting.

### Stage 1 → Suggested Schedule

title: How nap transitions are routed at intake (by age and nap count, not readiness signs)
retrieve_when: a parent asks how their schedule was chosen, when their child will drop a nap, or about the 3-2 or 2-1 nap transition.
body:
When a family first joins and receives their schedule, nap transitions are routed by age and current nap count only, not by "signs of readiness." This applies to both the 3-2 nap transition (around 7/8 months) and the 2-1 nap transition (around 12-17 months).

**Why.** The classic signs that a child is ready to drop a nap (short naps, early morning wakings, bedtime resistance, unusual night wakings) are NOT reliable indicators in a child who isn't yet sleeping well. A child whose sleep is disorganized will likely show short naps and early wakings regardless of nap-transition readiness; those symptoms come from the underlying sleep problem, not a genuine need to drop a nap. Reading them as transition signs at this stage would lead to mis-timed nap drops and more overtiredness. Transition "signs" only become meaningful once a child is sleeping well (more consolidated night sleep, can fall asleep independently for naps). At that point, typically in Stage 4, the signs can be used to help troubleshoot the schedule.

**How the 3-2 transition is routed (around 7/8 months).** A 7-month-old on 3 or more naps gets the transition schedule (eases them toward 2 naps). A 7-month-old already on 2 naps, and any 8-month-old, gets the settled 2-nap schedule, because they're developmentally ready for the longer wake windows.

**How the 2-1 transition is routed (around 12-17 months).** A 12-15 month old still on 2 naps gets the Holding onto 2 Naps schedule (not yet ready to transition). A 12-15 month old on 1 nap gets the 2-1 Transition schedule with its built-in safeguards, because nap count alone doesn't tell us whether 1 nap is actually working for a younger toddler. A 16-17 month old on 2 naps gets the 2-1 Transition schedule because of their age. A 16-17 month old already on 1 nap gets the settled 1-nap schedule.

**Why the transition schedules are safe defaults.** Both the 7/8 month 3-2 transition schedule and the 12-17 month 2-1 transition schedule have built-in safeguards: an optional catnap and an early-bedtime fallback for short-nap days. This lets a child stay on the lower number of naps if they're handling it well, while automatically protecting against overtiredness if they're not. The parent and the assistant don't have to predict readiness; the schedule self-corrects day to day.

title: Redirect early "is my child ready to drop a nap?" questions
retrieve_when: a parent asks early on whether their child is ready to drop a nap, often citing short naps or early wakings.
body: If a parent asks early on whether their child is "ready to drop a nap" because they're seeing short naps or early wakings, gently redirect. Explain that those symptoms are most likely tied to their child experiencing disorganized sleep, and that the schedule they've been given is a conservative place to start. Reassure them that once their child is sleeping well, you'll revisit nap timing and watch for true readiness signs together.

title: Clarify actual nap times before assessing a transition (don't trust nap labels)
retrieve_when: the assistant is assessing whether a child is ready to drop a nap.
body: When assessing whether a child is ready to drop a nap, the assistant clarifies the actual clock times the nap starts and ends, rather than inferring timing from how the parent labels the naps.

title: When a parent says the assigned schedule is wrong (early-plan mis-route check)
retrieve_when: early in the plan (Stage 1), a parent pushes back that their assigned schedule doesn't fit, or describes nap timing that sounds off. Not for Stage 4 schedule tuning of a child who is already sleeping well — that is the separate advancement framework.
body:
`flag:` the Settings path for changing nap count / schedule is not yet finalized in the app. "Walk the parent through updating the nap count in Settings" is a placeholder until Jessie supplies the actual how-to.

Schedules are assigned by age and nap count and are right for the large majority of families. How strongly a parent feels the schedule is wrong is not evidence that it is, and the assistant does not weigh it. It checks objective facts, in order.

**First: is this the age-appropriate schedule?** The schedule the app assigns for the child's age, once the nap count reflects their real day, is the age-appropriate one. If the child is on it and simply struggling — for example an 18-month-old correctly on one nap who still falls asleep in the car some mornings — do not change schedules. A schedule the child has aged out of is never added back. The child needs practice stretching their wake windows, and will handle the awake time more easily as their sleep consolidates. Hold the schedule, reassure, coach consistency. Most pushback ends here.

**Second, only if the assigned count may not be age-appropriate for this child: read the child's body, not the parent's claim.** This applies almost entirely to the transition windows (3-2 around 7/8 months, 2-1 around 12-17 months), where the same age can correctly take a different schedule depending on real nap count. What the child does involuntarily settles it:

- Reported on more naps than they use, but stays comfortably awake through the longer window (a 14-month-old listed as 2 naps who happily waits until 12/12:30) → tolerating the longer window, real count is lower.
- Reported on fewer naps than they need, but cannot stay awake for the required window (a 13-month-old listed as "solidly 1 nap" who falls asleep in the car at 8:30 every morning) → not tolerating it, real count is higher.

A child who cannot stay awake overrides any parental claim.

**Action: correct the nap count; let the app re-route.** When the real day contradicts the reported count, walk the parent through updating the nap count in Settings; the app re-routes them. Never name a schedule, pick a variant, or improvise nap times or wake windows — correct the one input and the routing does the rest. To read the real day, pin the actual clock times naps start and end rather than trusting how the parent labels them.

**When ambiguous, bias toward more day sleep** (more naps or the catnap structure). Point an unsure or pushing-back parent to the flexibility already inside their current schedule — the optional catnap, the early-bedtime fallback — rather than switching. Logs over the next few days confirm the call.

**Scope.** Early-plan only: catching an intake mis-route in the first days. Not mid-plan timing changes (the assistant reinforces consistency and does not change the schedule off a few noisy days), and not Stage 4 fine-tuning.

---

**Sample schedules by age (Part B).** The parent-facing schedule shown on each family's plan card. Retrieved when a family at that age needs their schedule or asks about timing, wake windows, or sleep needs. The nap-extension / catnapping troubleshooting that appeared in the original schedules is pulled out and gated separately (see Troubleshooting), so a getting-started family isn't told to adjust wake windows.
gate_evidence: ...age 4 schedule tuning of a child who is already sleeping well — that is the separate advancement fram...

title: 5 Month Old Sample Schedule (3 naps)
retrieve_when: a family with a 5-month-old needs their schedule, or asks about timing, wake windows, or sleep needs at 5 months.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 5 Months Old:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~15 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: ~12 hours
- Daytime sleep (across all naps): ~3 hours
- Number of naps needed: 3 naps
- Total awake time across the day: ~9 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your baby is awake for roughly 2 to 2.5 hours between sleeps. Wake windows are relatively short and fairly even throughout the day, the morning one is just slightly shorter than the rest.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 2 hours awake
- Nap 1 to Nap 2: 2.25 hours awake
- Nap 2 to Nap 3: 2.25 hours awake
- Nap 3 to Bedtime: 2.25 hours awake

A typical day looks like 2 / 2.25 / 2.25 / 2.25, with total awake time across the whole day landing around 9 hours.

5 Month Old on 3 naps:

- 7:00am - Wake for the day and eat
- 9:00-10:30am - Nap 1
- 12:45-2:00pm - Nap 2
- 4:15-5:00pm - Nap 3
- 7:00pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- A stabilized 3-nap schedule is the goal at this age. A 4th nap tends to cause early morning wakings and night wakings, so it's time to consolidate down to 3 if you haven't already.
- First nap should be no earlier than 8:30am. Starting earlier than that tends to push the rest of the day too early and can lead to early morning wakings.
- Use an early bedtime on bad nap days. If your baby had a rough day (for example, three 30-minute naps), bedtime should be 5:30-6:00pm to compensate for the lost day sleep. An early bedtime at this age will not cause early morning wakings. It actually helps prevent them.
- You're aiming for about 12 hours at night and 3 hours in naps for a total of ~15 hours of sleep in 24 hours.

title: 6 Month Old Sample Schedule (3 naps)
retrieve_when: a family with a 6-month-old needs their schedule, or asks about timing, wake windows, or sleep needs at 6 months.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 6 Months Old:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~14-14.5 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: 11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep (across all naps): 2.5-3.5 hours
- Number of naps needed: 3 naps
- Total awake time across the day: ~9.5-10 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your baby is awake for roughly 2.25 to 2.5 hours between sleeps. Wake windows are still fairly even across the day, with the middle two being just slightly longer than the bookends.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 2.25 hours awake
- Nap 1 to Nap 2: 2.5 hours awake
- Nap 2 to Nap 3: 2.5 hours awake
- Nap 3 to Bedtime: 2.25 hours awake

A typical day looks like 2.25 / 2.5 / 2.5 / 2.25, with total awake time across the whole day landing around 9.5-10 hours.

6 Month Old on 3 naps:

- 7:00am - Wake for the day and eat
- 9:15-10:30am - Nap 1
- 1:00-2:00pm - Nap 2
- 4:30-5:00pm - Nap 3
- 7:15pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- You're aiming for about 11-12 hours at night and 2.5-3.5 hours in naps, for a total of ~14-14.5 hours of sleep in 24 hours.
- Use an early bedtime on rough nap days. If naps were short and total day sleep is running below 2.5 hours, an earlier bedtime (as early as 6:00pm) helps your baby compensate without causing early morning wakings.

title: 7/8 Month Old Sample Schedule (Starting the 3-2 Nap Transition)
retrieve_when: a family with a 7- or 8-month-old on the 3-2 transition schedule needs their schedule, or asks about timing, wake windows, the optional catnap, or sleep needs during the 3-2 transition.
body:
7/8 Month Old Starting the 3-2 Nap Transition:

- 7:00am - Wake for the day and eat
- 9:30-11:00am - Nap 1
- 2:00-3:30pm - Nap 2
- 3:00/4:00pm - Optional catnap between 3-4pm if Nap 1 and Nap 2 were short
- ~7:00pm - Bedtime if no catnap happened
- ~7:30pm - Bedtime if catnap happened

Things to Know:

- There is usually a disruption in sleep around this age, in part due to milestones (sitting, crawling) and partially due to your baby getting ready to drop to two naps. Shorter naps, bedtime resistance, unusual night wakings, and earlier morning wakings are all common right now.
- A good rule of thumb: keep Nap 1 stable, then let it tell you how the day will go. If Nap 1 is short, plan for 3 naps with shorter wake windows. If Nap 1 is long, plan for 2 naps with a slightly earlier bedtime.
- Bedtime depends on the catnap: ~7:00pm bedtime if no catnap happened (total awake time ~9 hours); ~7:30pm bedtime if the catnap happened (total awake time ~10 hours).
- On 2-nap days, follow these rules: no nap before 9:00am; no nap before 1:00pm for Nap 2; no bedtime before 6:00pm.
- Total sleep targets during the transition: if the catnap happens, ~11-11.5 hours at night plus 2.5-3 hours in naps equals ~14 hours total. If only 2 naps happen, ~12 hours at night plus 3 hours in naps equals ~15 hours total.
- Use an early bedtime on rough nap days: bedtime as early as 6:00pm helps your baby compensate without causing early morning wakings.

title: 7/8 Month Old Sample Schedule (Settled on 2 naps)
retrieve_when: a family with a 7- or 8-month-old settled on 2 naps needs their schedule, or asks about timing, wake windows, or sleep needs at 7/8 months on 2 naps.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 7/8 Months Old on 2 Naps:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~14 hours
- Nighttime sleep: 11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep (across both naps): 2.5-3.5 hours
- Number of naps needed: 2 naps
- Total awake time across the day: 9-10 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your baby is awake for roughly 2.5 to 3.5 hours between sleeps. Wake windows get a little longer as the day goes on, the morning one is the shortest, the one before bed is the longest.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 2.5-3 hours awake
- Nap 1 to Nap 2: 3-3.25 hours awake
- Nap 2 to Bedtime: 3-3.5 hours awake

When your baby is first settling into 2 naps, start on the shorter end (around 2.5 / 3 / 3.5). As the 2-nap schedule becomes more established, you can gradually stretch the morning wake window so the day looks more like 3 / 3.25 / 3.5. Total awake time across the whole day works out to about 9 hours early in the transition, climbing toward 10 hours once 2 naps are settled.

7/8 Month Old on 2 naps:

- 7:00am - Wake for the day and eat
- 9:45-11:15am - Nap 1
- 2:15-4:00pm - Nap 2
- 7:00/7:30pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- Early in the 2-nap transition, expect total awake time to land closer to 9 hours. As your baby adjusts, it'll naturally climb toward 10, that's a sign the 2-nap schedule is settling in.
- Aim for 11-12 hours of night sleep and 2.5-3.5 hours of day sleep across the two naps. If day sleep is running shorter than 2.5 hours, an earlier bedtime (as early as 6:00pm) can help compensate.

title: 9-11 Month Old Sample Schedule (2 naps)
retrieve_when: a family with a 9-11 month old needs their schedule or asks about timing, wake windows, or sleep needs at 9-11 months; or a 9-11 month old is having short naps, bedtime resistance, or night wakings and the family is wondering whether to adjust the schedule or drop a nap. (Dual-use: applies both early in the plan and in later troubleshooting.)
body:
Average Sleep Needs Between 9-11 Months Old:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~13.5-14 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: 11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep (across both naps): 2-3 hours
- Number of naps needed: 2 naps
- Total awake time across the day: ~10-10.5 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your baby is awake for roughly 3 to 3.75 hours between sleeps. Wake windows get a little longer as the day goes on, the middle one (between Nap 1 and Nap 2) is typically the longest.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 3-3.25 hours awake
- Nap 1 to Nap 2: 3.5-3.75 hours awake
- Nap 2 to Bedtime: 3-3.5 hours awake

Early in this age range, a typical day looks like ~3-3.25 / 3.5 / 3-3.5 with total awake time around 10 hours. As your baby matures through this range, wake windows gradually stretch and the day looks more like ~3.25 / 3.5-3.75 / 3.5 with total awake time climbing to about 10.5 hours.

9-11 Month Old on 2 naps:

- 7:00am - Wake for the day and eat
- 10:00-11:15am - Nap 1
- 2:45-4:00pm - Nap 2
- ~7:30pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- You're aiming for about 11-12 hours at night and 2-3 hours in naps, for a total of ~13.5-14 hours of sleep in 24 hours.
- This age range is generally a stable, predictable stretch on 2 naps. The 2-1 nap transition is still a ways off (most babies aren't ready for that until 14+ months at the earliest), so resist the urge to drop a nap if things get briefly bumpy. Short naps, bedtime resistance, or night wakings during this window are almost always tied to a milestone (pulling up, cruising, first words) rather than a true schedule change.
- Use an early bedtime on rough nap days. If day sleep is running below 2 hours, an earlier bedtime helps your baby compensate without causing early morning wakings.

title: 12-15 Month Old Sample Schedule (Holding onto 2 Naps)
retrieve_when: a 12-15 month old family on the Holding onto 2 Naps schedule needs their schedule, or asks about timing, nap-limiting, holding onto 2 naps as long as possible, or the daycare-puts-them-on-1-nap situation.
body:
12-15 Month Old on 2 naps:

- 7:00am — Wake for the day
- 10:30-11:30am — Nap 1 (you can limit this nap to 45 minutes-1 hour to preserve the 2nd nap if needed)
- 3:00-4:00pm — Nap 2 (this nap can be more of a catnap, a bridge to bedtime, or even an on-the-go nap in the carseat or stroller)
- 7:00-8:00pm — Bedtime

Things to Know:

- Around 12 months, you may start seeing early signs of the 2-1 nap transition: shorter naps, taking longer to fall asleep at bedtime, and earlier morning wakings. These signs don't necessarily mean it's time to drop a nap right away, many children can hold onto 2 naps for several more months with a few small adjustments.
- Unlike other nap transitions which typically only take a couple of weeks to settle, the 2-1 transition takes a few months to fully stabilize. It's worth holding onto 2 naps as long as you can.
- You can hold onto 2 naps longer by nap-limiting. Cap Nap 1 at 45 minutes to 1 hour to preserve Nap 2. This often buys you weeks (or months) of extra time on 2 naps. However, you may still see some bedtime resistance and occasional early morning wakings as your child grows out of this schedule.
- Some daycares will put a child on 1 nap as early as 12 months. When this happens, work with what daycare is doing rather than fighting it. A 12-15 month old who is already on 1 nap (whether daycare-driven or parent-led) is routed to the 2-1 Transition schedule, which has built-in safeguards (an 11am earliest nap, an optional 30-minute catnap, and an early bedtime fallback) that protect against a too-early one-nap day going wrong.

title: 12-17 Month Old Sample Schedule (The 2-1 Nap Transition)
retrieve_when: a 12-17 month old family on the 2-1 Transition schedule needs their schedule, or asks about timing, stretching the morning wake window, the optional catnap, alternating 1-nap and 2-nap days, or sleep needs during the 2-1 transition.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 12-17 Months During the 2-1 Transition:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~13-14 hours
- Nighttime sleep: ~11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep: 1.5-3 hours (varies day to day during transition)
- Number of naps needed: 1 nap (with an optional catnap on short-nap days)
- Total awake time across the day: ~10 hours starting out, gradually climbing towards ~11 hours as the transition settles

Wake Windows:
This is a transitional stage, so wake windows are stretching significantly and the day is shifting from 2 naps to 1. Starting out, wake windows look like roughly 4 to 5.5 hours, and they'll continue to stretch over the coming weeks and months until you're settled on 1 nap.

- Morning wake-up → Nap 1: 4-4.5 hours awake (starting point, stretching toward 5-6 hours over time)
- Nap 1 → Bedtime (no catnap): 5-5.5 hours awake
- Nap 1 → Catnap → Bedtime (if catnap happens): shorter wake windows, with catnap around 3-4pm

Starting wake windows for the transition are typically 4-4.5 / 5-5.5. From there, you gradually keep stretching the morning wake window first (for example, 4.75/5.25, then 5/5) until the nap is starting around 12:00-12:30pm.

12-17 Month Old in the 2-1 Transition:

- 7:00am — Wake for the day
- 11:00am — Nap 1 (nap no earlier than 11:00am for the 2-1 transition, wake window ~4-4.5 hours to start)
- 3:00/4:00pm — Optional catnap between 3-4pm if Nap 1 was less than 1.5 hours (cap at 30 minutes; this can be a stroller nap, carseat nap, or quiet-time-in-crib)
- ~6:00pm — Bedtime if no catnap happened (wake window of 5-5.5 hours from when the nap ended — so if the nap was 11:00am-12:30pm, bedtime is 6:00pm)
- 7:00-7:30pm — Bedtime if the catnap happened

Things to Know:

- This schedule serves two distinct populations:
  - (1) 12-15 month olds who are already on 1 nap. Whether their parent moved them too early, or daycare put them on 1 nap, or they genuinely seem ready, the Transition schedule is the safe default for this age. The safeguards built into this schedule (the 11am earliest nap, the optional 30-minute catnap, the early bedtime fallback) catch the case where 1 nap isn't quite working yet, while still being a reasonable fit for the rare 14-15 month old who is genuinely thriving on 1 nap.
  - (2) 16-17 month olds who are still on 2 naps. At this age, the default is to start moving toward 1 nap regardless of "readiness signs," because signs are unreliable in children who aren't yet sleeping well (falling asleep independently with consolidated night sleep), and 16-17 months is the developmental window when most children are ready to transition.
- Before formally beginning the transition, two things should ideally be in place: night feeds are out, and your child is sleeping well through the night. This won't apply to most of our families at the start, but it's worth noting as the ideal sequence. Unlike other nap transitions which typically only take a couple of weeks to settle, the 2-1 transition takes a few months to fully stabilize.
- To start the transition, gradually stretch the first wake window to 4 hours and don't limit the nap. The morning nap should never start before 11:00am during the 2-1 transition.
- The morning wake window is what moves the transition forward. As the first wake window increases, the nap time gradually moves later — from 11:00am to 11:15am, 11:30am, and so on. Once the nap is consistently starting around 12:00/12:30/1:00pm (a 5-6 hour first wake window), 1 nap is established and you're settled with a total awake time of about 10-11 hours (total sleep ~13-14 hours).
- The catnap is your fallback on short-nap days. If Nap 1 is shorter than 1.5 hours, offer a catnap around 3-4pm. Cap the catnap at 30 minutes (long enough to take the edge off, short enough to protect bedtime). This can be a stroller nap, carseat nap, or quiet time in the crib if it doesn't turn into sleep. If the catnap happens, bedtime is 7:00-7:30pm. If it doesn't, bedtime is around 6:00pm.
- You may need to alternate 1-nap and 2-nap days for a few weeks. This is normal. Some days your child will handle 1 nap well, and other days they'll need the structure of 2 naps. Read your child's cues and adjust day to day.
- Watch bedtime closely. Overtiredness slowly builds up during the 2-1 transition, so early wakings are often a sign that bedtime isn't early enough. When you see early wakings start to creep in, move bedtime earlier to compensate, then push the nap time back out again once your child is caught up on sleep.

title: 16-17 Month Old Sample Schedule (Settled on 1 Nap)
retrieve_when: a 16-17 month old family settled on 1 nap needs their schedule, or asks about timing, wake windows, the afternoon-wake-window lever, or sleep needs at 16-17 months on 1 nap.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 16-17 Months Old on 1 Nap:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~13-14 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: ~11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep: 2-3 hours
- Number of naps needed: 1 nap
- Total awake time across the day: ~10-11 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your child is awake for roughly 5 to 5.5 hours between sleeps. With only one nap in the day, the morning and afternoon wake windows are similar in length.

- Morning wake-up → Nap 1: 5-5.5 hours awake
- Nap 1 → Bedtime: 5-5.5 hours awake

A typical day looks like 5-5.5 / 5-5.5, with total awake time landing around 10-11 hours.

16-17 Month Old on 1 Nap:

- 6:30/7:00am — Wake for the day
- 12:00/12:30pm — Nap 1 (nap starting between 12:00-12:30pm)
- ~6:30-7:00pm — Bedtime (wake window of 5-5.5 hours from when the nap ended)

Things to Know:

- You're aiming for about 11-12 hours at night and 2-3 hours in the nap. If the nap is consistently shorter than 2 hours, watch bedtime closely — moving it slightly earlier can help prevent overtiredness from building up.
- On days when the nap is short or skipped entirely, use an early bedtime (as early as 6:00pm) to help your child compensate without causing early morning wakings.

title: 18-23 Month Old Sample Schedule (1 Nap)
retrieve_when: an 18-23 month old family on 1 nap needs their schedule, or asks about timing, the naptime lever, bedtime resistance or stalling, or sleep needs at 18-23 months.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 18-23 Months Old on 1 Nap:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~12-13 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: 11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep: 1.5-2 hours
- Number of naps needed: 1 nap
- Total awake time across the day: ~11-12 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your child is awake for roughly 4 to 6+ hours between sleeps. The morning wake window is now noticeably longer than the afternoon wake window.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 5.5-6+ hours awake
- Nap 1 to Bedtime: 4-5 hours awake

A typical day looks like 5.5-6+ / 4-5, with total awake time landing around 11-12 hours.

18-23 Month Old on 1 Nap:

- 6:30/7:00am - Wake for the day
- 12:00/1:00pm - Nap 1 (1.5-3 hours)
- ~7:00-7:30pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- Sleep needs start to drop a little around 18 months, and the nap naturally moves a little later in the day. You're aiming for about 11-12 hours at night and 1.5-2 hours in the nap, for a total of 12-13 hours of sleep in 24 hours.
- Expect some bedtime shenanigans. This is a stage of huge language and developmental leaps, new words, new opinions, new milestones, and they tend to show up at bedtime. Stalling, requests for "one more" book, and bedtime resistance are all really common right now. Stay the course and be consistent with bedtime routines and night wakings. Hold your boundaries around sleep.
- On short or skipped-nap days, use an early bedtime to help your child compensate without causing early morning wakings.

title: 2 Year Old Sample Schedule (1 Nap)
retrieve_when: a 2 year old family on 1 nap needs their schedule, or asks about timing, the naptime lever, nap refusals around 24 months, bedtime resistance, or sleep needs at 2 years.
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 2 Years Old on 1 Nap:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~12-14 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: 11-12 hours
- Daytime sleep: 1.5-2 hours
- Number of naps needed: 1 nap
- Total awake time across the day: ~11-12 hours

Wake Windows:
At this age, your child is awake for roughly 4 to 6+ hours between sleeps. The morning wake window is noticeably longer than the afternoon wake window.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 5.5-6+ hours awake
- Nap 1 to Bedtime: 4-5 hours awake

A typical day looks like 5.5-6+ / 4-5, with total awake time landing around 11-12 hours.

2 Year Old on 1 Nap:

- 6:30/7:00am - Wake for the day
- 12:30/1:00pm - Nap 1 (1.5-2 hours)
- ~7:00-8:00pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- The schedule doesn't change much from 18-23 months. Sometimes the nap shifts another 15-30 minutes later, but the overall structure stays the same. You're aiming for about 11-12 hours at night and 1.5-2 hours in the nap, for a total of ~12-14 hours of sleep in 24 hours.
- Expect some bedtime shenanigans. This stage brings even more language, opinions, and big-kid milestones and they tend to show up at bedtime. Stalling, requests for "one more" book, and bedtime resistance are all really common right now.
- Nap refusals are common around 24 months. It's normal to see your child fight the nap for a week or two right around their second birthday. Continue to require a nap and be consistent. Naps almost always fall back into place after a brief refusal phase.
- Stay the course and be consistent with bedtime routines, nap refusals, and night wakings. Avoid starting up unsustainable habits. Holding your boundaries now protects sleep for the months ahead.
- On days when the nap is short or skipped, use an early bedtime to help your child compensate without causing early morning wakings.

---

**Schedules beyond the intake range (Part C).** The app does not create plans for these ages, so no family enters here. These schedules exist for continuing subscribers whose child ages past the V1 plan-creation range, so the assistant (and the app's schedule-update mechanism) can keep serving them as the child grows. Because no getting-started family is ever at these ages, the getting-started-vs-solid-sleeper gating that splits the younger schedules does not apply here, so these entries stay whole and ungated.

title: 2.5-3.5 Year Old Sample Schedule (Napping)
retrieve_when: a continuing family whose child has reached 2.5-3.5 years needs an updated schedule, or asks about napping, nap-capping, missed naps, or bedtime at this age. (Beyond intake range: only continuing subscribers reach this.)
backs_block: none (beyond V1 spreadsheet scope; see Part 3 dev note on continuing-family schedules)
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 2.5-3.5 Years Old:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~11-13 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: ~10-11 hours
- Daytime sleep: 1-2 hours (tapering toward the shorter end as your child nears 3.5)
- Number of naps needed: 1 nap

Wake Windows:
At this age, your child is awake for roughly 5 to 6+ hours between sleeps. The afternoon wake window is the longer of the two.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 5-6 hours awake
- Nap 1 to Bedtime: 5-6+ hours awake

2.5-3.5 Year Old on 1 Nap:

- 6:30/7:00am - Wake for the day
- 1:00/2:00pm - Nap 1 (cap at ~2 hours, trimming toward 1-1.5 hours as your child nears 3.5)
- ~7:30-8:00pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- The goal at this age is still a daily nap. Offer it in a dark room at a consistent time. You can't make a toddler sleep, but you can continue to offer the opportunity.
- Missed-nap fallback: if your child hasn't fallen asleep within 60-90 minutes, you can end the nap and opt for an early bedtime instead. On a missed-nap day, bedtime as early as 6:00pm prevents over-tiredness. The younger the child, the earlier the bedtime on missed-nap days (a 7:30-8:00pm bedtime is too late when no nap happened).
- Cap the nap to protect night sleep. Up to 2 hours is fine for a younger toddler, but as your child approaches 3.5, trim the nap toward 1-1.5 hours. If you see bedtime resistance or early morning wakings, shorten the nap before making any other change. At this age, too much day sleep is the most common culprit.
- If your child consistently takes longer than 30 minutes to fall asleep at both nap and bedtime, that's an early sign the nap may be winding down. Continue offering a nap until ~3.5, which is when rest time gradually becomes the new normal.
- Potty training and the transition to a toddler bed will throw things off. When these big changes happen, stay conservative with bedtime and be consistent with early morning wakings. Calmly remind your child that it isn't time to wake up yet and that they need to go back to sleep. Holding your boundaries during these transitions is what protects sleep long-term. An Ok-to-Wake clock can be a very helpful tool.

title: 3.5-4 Year Old Sample Schedule (Starting the 1-0 Nap Transition)
retrieve_when: a continuing family whose child has reached 3.5-4 years needs an updated schedule, or asks about the 1-0 nap transition, dropping the nap, rest time, or bedtime at this age. (Beyond intake range: only continuing subscribers reach this.)
backs_block: none (beyond V1 spreadsheet scope; see Part 3 dev note on continuing-family schedules)
body:
Average Sleep Needs at 3.5-4 Years Old:

- Total sleep in 24 hours: ~11-13 hours (not including night wakings)
- Nighttime sleep: ~10-11 hours
- Daytime sleep: 1-1.5 hours (on days when the nap happens)
- Number of naps needed: 1 nap, transitioning gradually to none

Wake Windows:
At this age, your child is awake for roughly 5 to 6+ hours between sleeps. The afternoon wake window is the longer of the two.

- Morning wake-up to Nap 1: 5-6 hours awake
- Nap 1 to Bedtime: 5-6+ hours awake

3.5-4 Year Old on 1 Nap:

- 6:30/7:00am - Wake for the day
- 1:00/2:00pm - Nap 1 (1.5 hours or less)
- ~8:00pm - Bedtime

Things to Know:

- The 1-0 nap transition is unlike any other nap transition. It's a gradual transition that can take a couple of years to fully complete (often stretching through ages 3-5). Your child may miss one nap a week, then two naps a week, then every other day, then just nap once a week, and so on. This usually starts somewhere between 3-3.5 years old.
- You can try pushing the nap later in the day to preserve it. Often times this will push bedtime later as well. Just don't push bedtime past 8:30pm. Kids tend to get a "second wind" if they're up too late, which makes falling asleep harder rather than easier.
- When your child is ready to begin dropping the nap, implement a "rest time" in place of the afternoon nap. This gives them a chance to recharge without requiring sleep. On days the nap is skipped, rest time is the fallback. Your child still goes to their sleep space at a consistent time, sleeps if they need it, and rests quietly if they don't. Jessie loves introducing a Yoto player around this age during rest time (once child consistently stops falling asleep during their rest time).
- Expect extra shenanigans at naptime and bedtime. Stay the course and be consistent with bedtime routines and naps. Avoid starting up unsustainable habits.
- Potty training and the transition to a toddler bed can throw things off. When these big changes happen, stay conservative with bedtime and be consistent with early morning wakings. Calmly remind your child that it isn't time to wake up yet and that they need to go back to sleep. Holding your boundaries during these transitions is what protects sleep long-term. An Ok-to-Wake clock can be a very helpful tool.
- On days when the nap is short or skipped entirely, use an early bedtime to help your child compensate without causing early morning wakings.

---

**Adjusted age (preemies).** For babies born before 37 weeks. At intake the parent chooses whether to use adjusted age; if yes, the app routes by adjusted age (through 24 months) and the callout appears on the schedule card. The foundational explainer below is ungated (any preemie family needs it at intake, including the first 3-7 day "which age fits" check). The deeper judgment of *easing off* adjusted age toward chronological as the baby catches up over time is gated to solid sleepers and lives in the Troubleshooting cluster.

title: Adjusted age — what it is, when The Goodnight House uses it, and why 37 weeks
retrieve_when: a parent of a baby born early (before 37 weeks) asks about adjusted or corrected age, which age their schedule uses, or why the app asked about prematurity at intake.
backs_block: schedule_card_SHELL (the adjusted-age callout; driven by the `gestational_age_weeks` and `uses_adjusted_age` questionnaire fields)
body:
What is adjusted age?
Adjusted age (also called corrected age) is your baby's chronological age minus the number of weeks or months they were born early. Example: a baby who is 7 months old chronologically but was born 8 weeks (2 months) early has an adjusted age of 5 months.

When does The Goodnight House use adjusted age?
If your baby was born before 37 weeks gestation, The Goodnight House can use adjusted age to choose your baby's sleep schedule and recommend wake windows. Whether to use it is the parent's choice at intake:

- Born at 37+ weeks → chronological age (age from birthday).
- Born before 37 weeks, using adjusted age → the plan routes by adjusted age, available through 24 months.
- Born before 37 weeks, going by age from birth → chronological age.

The app applies your choice automatically and your schedule reflects it.

Why 37 weeks?
A full-term pregnancy is about 40 weeks, and 40 is the middle of a normal 37-42 week range, so how much to adjust depends on how early your baby actually arrived. A baby born around 37-38 weeks is usually within the typical range and most don't need adjusting at all. A "late preterm" baby born around 36 weeks is only about one to two weeks behind, not a full month. A baby born genuinely early, roughly 30-34 weeks, is where adjusted age matters most, especially in the early months. The earlier your baby arrived, the more their adjusted age is worth leaning on. Prematurity tells us where to start, not where your baby will end up.

What if I'm not sure which age to use?
Start with adjusted age. Follow the schedule for 3-7 days and watch for these signals:

- Going well? Stay on the adjusted-age schedule.
- Consistently undertired (hard to fall asleep, fighting naps, short naps)? Try the schedule one age range up.
- Consistently overtired (fussy, hysterical at sleep times, very short naps, early morning wakings)? Stay on adjusted age, or try slightly shorter wake windows within that schedule.

If you're unsure, ask the assistant for help interpreting your baby's signals.

### Stage 1 → Making the Schedule Work

*The reasoning behind the schedule, so the assistant can answer the questions parents have after they receive their schedule and instructions. Backs the "Making Your Schedule Work" card, which has a baby variant (`making_the_schedule_work`, age route <24mo) and a toddler variant (`making_the_schedule_work_toddler`, 24-29mo); the reasoning here is general and applies to both. A separate daycare/childcare section also lives under this card (`childcare_affects_naps`) and can be added when that content comes through.*

title: The method is clock-led, not cue-led (and why)
retrieve_when: a parent asks whether to follow wake windows / the clock or watch their child's cues, asks why the schedule is by-the-clock, asks how to run a by-the-clock schedule or why a consistent morning wake time matters, or treats the current schedule's times as permanent.
body:
Jessie's schedules are by-the-clock on purpose. They are built from age-appropriate wake windows, but those windows are baked into a fixed schedule the parent follows by the clock, rather than something the parent recalculates daily off sleepy cues, the last nap, or how the child seems in the moment.

This is a deliberate design choice, and the assistant should treat it as a feature, not a limitation. A fixed clock schedule means an exhausted parent doesn't have to read cues or do wake-window math in real time. It also gives the child a predictable rhythm, which is what regulates their internal clock and stabilizes their days.

Jessie's approach to schedules is rooted in reducing confusion for both the child and the parent. When sleep is already disorganized, sleepy cues can be difficult to interpret because overtired, undertired, and dysregulated children often show very similar signs. Some sleep professionals prefer to start by having parents closely follow sleepy cues and wake windows throughout the day. While that approach can work well for many families, Jessie finds that exhausted parents are often overwhelmed by constantly watching the clock, calculating wake windows, and trying to determine whether a behavior is truly a sleep cue or simply a sign of boredom, frustration, overstimulation, or overtiredness.

Instead, she begins with a consistent, age-appropriate baseline schedule built around age-appropriate wake windows and a predictable morning wake time. This gives families a clear starting point and removes much of the guesswork while sleep is being stabilized. Once a child is sleeping more predictably and a true baseline is established, thoughtful adjustments can be made based on that child's unique needs and responses. The schedule still fits each child. Its job is to create enough consistency that the parent and assistant can clearly see what is and isn't working. In Jessie's experience, this approach is simpler to follow, less stressful to implement, and easier to maintain long-term.

When a parent asks "should I just follow wake windows or watch his cues instead?", the assistant reinforces the by-the-clock approach during the working period. Acknowledge that cue-reading is a real approach, and explain that for now, while we're actively working on their child's sleep, the clock leads. Cues become useful again once the child is sleeping well.

How to run a by-the-clock schedule: it depends on a consistent morning wake-up time, which is why Jessie uses a consistent out-of-crib time each morning. A consistent morning wake-up keeps the first nap at about the same time each day. A first nap that starts at a consistent time tends to run a consistent length, which keeps the second nap on time, and the rest of the day stays on schedule. A simple way to set it up: keep a stable out-of-crib time each morning, trying not to let it drift more than about 30 minutes, and set a consistent time for the first nap of the day.

Schedules are not permanent. They're age-based, so they shift as a child's sleep needs drop with age and through nap transitions. Just because a child naps at 9am now doesn't mean that's fixed forever. This is why Jessie uses age-based schedules: the app advances the times as the child grows. The assistant should treat the current schedule as right-for-now, not permanent, and when a child's needs are clearly changing, point the parent to their updated age-based schedule (the suggested-schedule resources) rather than improvising new nap times itself.

title: Why we hold the schedule boundaries firmly during the working period
retrieve_when: a parent asks why they have to follow the times so strictly, pushes back on the rigidity, or believes keeping their child awake longer will help them sleep.
body:
Consistency is extremely important — it makes the biggest difference in how well this works. The reason the assistant should encourage parents to hold the times tightly while we work together comes down to a few linked ideas, and the assistant can draw on whichever fits the parent's question:

- **Overtiredness is the main thing to prevent, and it's the opposite of what most parents expect.** Many parents believe keeping a child awake longer will make them sleep better, "wear them out." The opposite is true. An overtired child is going to be overstimulated and harder to settle, not easier. Overtiredness can look like hyperactivity, climbing the walls, crying, or an inability to settle. Protecting the schedule prevents this build-up.
- **Sleep timing only works if it's protected.** We are resetting the child's body clock. That reset depends on sleep happening at consistent times and on the full sleep period being honored. Letting the schedule drift, or ending sleep early, undercuts the very thing we're building.
- **Asleep-by, not starting-at.** The scheduled time is the target for being asleep, which means the parent starts assisting to sleep a little before it. Being asleep on time is what prevents overtiredness from creeping in.
- **Waking matters as much as sleeping.** Ending naps on time protects the next nap and bedtime. Too much day sleep, or sleep at the wrong times, is just as disruptive as too little. Having them asleep on time and waking them on time are equally important.
- **Consistency makes future changes easier.** Once a child is caught up on sleep debt and their nervous system is calmer, everything else (changes around sleep, schedule tweaks, flexibility) gets smoother. Closely following the schedule at the start is what makes things easier later.

title: Keeping the child in the dark sleep space for the full sleep period (and the pushback)
retrieve_when: a parent resists keeping their child in the dark sleep space for the whole nap or sleep period, wants to end a nap early to rescue a wake window, or says "my child won't let me keep them in there." This also includes the early-morning version, where a child wakes before the usual wake time, will not fall back asleep, and the parent feels there is no point keeping them in the dark, so they might as well start the day.
body:
Keeping a child in their dark sleep space for the full sleep period is one of the parts parents resist most, and the assistant should expect pushback and meet it with both warmth and firmness.

The reasoning the assistant can share: darkness signals the brain to produce melatonin, while light triggers a whole different cascade of hormones to be released. Time spent resting in the dark is restorative even when the child isn't asleep, and it continues to help regulate the body clock. So ending a nap early to "rescue" the wake window does more harm than good.

When a parent says "my child won't let me keep them in there," the assistant gently reframes: Sometimes when we say, "My child won't let me," what we really mean is, "My child doesn't like this." Those are not always the same thing.

Our children may need time to adjust to a change, and they may not appreciate it at first, but that doesn't necessarily mean the change is wrong. As parents, we can acknowledge their frustration while still lovingly holding boundaries and leading them forward.

This applies to early morning wakings too. When a child wakes before their usual wake time and will not fall back asleep, parents often feel there is no reason to keep them in the dark, so they start the day. Keeping the child in the dark sleep space until the usual wake time is still restful and restorative even when the child does not fall back asleep. Starting the day at the early time teaches the child's body to keep waking early. Keeping the child in the dark until the usual wake time is what helps the early wakings settle over time.

title: The strictness is a season, not forever (frame it as temporary)
retrieve_when: a parent feels overwhelmed or defeated by how strict the schedule is, or worries they can't keep it up.
body: This level of consistency is a season, not forever, and the assistant should say so often. Many parents, especially moms, find the idea of strictly following a schedule overwhelming or defeating. Reassure them: once their child is sleeping well, flexibility comes back. A skipped nap or a late night here and there will not undo their work. The strict period of closely following the schedule is the start of the process, not how things will always be. This framing keeps parents from burning out or abandoning the plan because it feels too rigid.

title: The Early Bedtime Framework: rescue a rough nap day with an earlier bedtime
retrieve_when: a child has had an unusually rough nap day (missed or short naps) and the assistant needs to set an appropriate earlier bedtime to help make up the missed daytime sleep.
body:
When a child has an unusually rough nap day use an earlier bedtime to help the child recover some of the daytime sleep they missed.

Within the Goodnight House framework, bedtime adjustments are based primarily on daytime sleep debt. Because children following the Goodnight House plans should have remained in their dark sleep space for the full scheduled nap opportunity, the schedule itself does not change throughout the day. The only part of the schedule that becomes flexible is bedtime.

**Step 1: Determine the expected daytime sleep.**
Using the child's current schedule, calculate how much daytime sleep the child would typically receive if they slept through each scheduled nap.
Example (5-month schedule):
- Nap 1: 9:00-10:30 (90 minutes)
- Nap 2: 12:45-2:00 (75 minutes)
- Nap 3: 4:15-5:00 (45 minutes)

Expected daytime sleep = 210 minutes (3.5 hours).

**Step 2: Calculate actual daytime sleep.**
Add together the amount of time the child actually slept during each scheduled nap opportunity.
Do not count quiet time spent awake in the crib as sleep. The child should remain in their sleep space for the full scheduled nap period, but only actual sleep counts toward their daytime total.

**Step 3: Calculate sleep debt.**
Sleep debt = Expected daytime sleep - Actual daytime sleep

**Step 4: Adjust bedtime.**
Use the amount of missed daytime sleep to determine how much earlier bedtime should be.

| Daytime sleep missed | Bedtime adjustment |
|---|---|
| 0-30 minutes | Keep the normal bedtime. |
| 31-60 minutes | Move bedtime about 15 minutes earlier. |
| 61-89 minutes | Move bedtime about 30 minutes earlier. |
| 90-120 minutes | Move bedtime about 45 minutes earlier. |
| 121-150 minutes | Move bedtime about 60 minutes earlier. |
| More than 150 minutes | Move bedtime 60-90 minutes earlier, but generally not before 6:00 pm. A 5- or 6-month-old can occasionally handle a ~5:30 pm bedtime when truly needed, but this should be used sparingly and generally not for older babies and toddlers. |

Round bedtime to the nearest practical 15-minute increment.

**Additional Principles**
- Sleep debt is cumulative across the day. A long nap later in the day does not erase sleep that was missed earlier.
- Likewise, several shorter naps do not necessarily eliminate sleep debt if the total amount of sleep remains low.
- A child who appears to be handling the day well may still benefit from an earlier bedtime if they accumulated significant daytime sleep debt.
- One rough day does not require overcorrecting. Early bedtime is simply a way to provide an additional opportunity for restorative sleep overnight.
- In most cases, do not recommend a bedtime earlier than 6:00 pm.

**AI Reasoning**
When determining bedtime, the assistant should:
- Identify the child's scheduled daytime sleep total.
- Calculate how much daytime sleep the child actually obtained.
- Calculate the total sleep debt.
- Recommend an earlier bedtime using the framework above.
- Explain the recommendation briefly and confidently without asking the parent to do the math.

Example:
"Your child normally gets 3½ hours of daytime sleep but only slept 2 hours today, leaving a 90-minute sleep debt. I'd move bedtime about 45 minutes earlier tonight and aim for a 6:15 pm bedtime. That gives them an opportunity to begin making up some of the missed sleep overnight."

title: Shifting the schedule for a real constraint vs. holding it against an arbitrary preference
retrieve_when: a parent asks to change their child's wake or sleep times, wants to shift the schedule for daycare, work, a commute, or another external commitment, or wants unusual hours or only a later bedtime.
body:
When a parent asks about changing their child's wake or sleep times, first sort which kind of request it is.

A real constraint is an external, fixed limit on the parent's day, like daycare hours, a work commute, or a school run. For these, help them shift the entire schedule (wake, naps, and bedtime) earlier or later by the same amount, up to a one-hour maximum. Walk them through the plan settings. Reassure them this is a reasonable accommodation.

An arbitrary preference is wanting unusual hours with no external constraint (for example, a 10:00am-10:00pm day, or pushing only bedtime later). For these, gently reinforce the by-the-clock approach. Explain that the given times are what Jessie has found works best, that consistency during the working period is what gets their child sleeping well, and that they'll be able to adjust freely once they're past this stage. Don't entertain unusual schedule variations during the working period.

Never volunteer the shift override unprompted. It's included in the plan only as a note to the assistant, on purpose, so families don't drift toward arbitrary changes. Surface the full how-to only when a parent raises a genuine need.

**Guardrails:** hold the one-hour cap. Always move the whole schedule together, never bedtime alone; shifting only bedtime, or stretching the day to unusual hours, tends to backfire into early wakings and overtiredness. When in doubt about whether a constraint is "real," err toward encouraging the standard schedule while staying warm and non-judgmental.

title: Don't troubleshoot schedule timing during the working period; reinforce consistency
retrieve_when: a parent reports messy naps, early wakings, or a rough day during the working period and may be looking to change nap times or wake windows, or asks the assistant to fine-tune the schedule before their child is sleeping well.
body:
The by-the-clock schedules are designed to work when followed steadily and allowed to advance with the child's age. During the working period the assistant should NOT try to change or fine-tune a family's schedule timing. The child isn't experiencing solid sleep yet, so day-to-day data is unreliable. It's not reliable to tweak the schedule based off of unreliable sleep.

A useful way for the assistant to frame this with parents: their log is data, and we never want to rush to change things based on a small amount of data. A few rough days is too small a sample to act on, and reacting to it usually creates more chaos, not less. This is honest, good guidance on its own, and it also helps the parent stay patient and consistent rather than tinkering.

So when a parent reports messy naps, early wakings, or a rough day, the assistant reassures them this is normal before sleep is consolidated, reminds them a few days isn't enough data to change course, reinforces following their assigned schedule consistently, and uses the tools that already exist (the Early Bedtime Framework to rescue an off day). It does not adjust nap times or wake windows. Genuine schedule tuning is something the parent and assistant can work through once the plan is complete and their child is sleeping well enough to read things clearly (towards the end of stage 4, once a child is falling asleep independently for at least some naps).

title: Expect pushback on the schedule; meet it with patient, repeated education
retrieve_when: a parent pushes back on the by-the-clock schedule (often repeatedly, sometimes with frustration or blame), fears overtiredness, resists stretching wake windows when their baby is fussy, or blames a rough nap or bedtime on the longer wake window or on following the clock.
body:
Some parents, often the more sleep-knowledgeable ones, will push back hard on the by-the-clock schedule, sometimes repeatedly and sometimes with frustration or blame. This is one of the most common and predictable challenges of the method, not a sign anything is wrong. The assistant should expect it and respond with patience, warmth, and repeated education rather than treating repeated questions as a problem.

The specific fear is almost always overtiredness. Parents struggle most with stretching wake windows when their baby is fussy, because their instinct tells them a fussy baby must be a tired baby who needs to sleep now. When the assistant asks them to hold to the clock and the next nap or bedtime is then rough, they often blame the longer wake window and the instruction to follow the clock. The assistant should not get defensive about this. It should calmly reassure: rough naps and bedtimes are normal and expected while sleep is still being consolidated, one rough patch is not proof the wake window was wrong, and a fussy child is not automatically an overtired child (fussiness has many causes, and undertiredness can look the same, see the knowledge base on overtiredness). Reaffirm that their child can handle these wake windows, that this is a process of resetting the body clock, and that consistency is what makes it work.

A specific recurring sticking point: parents tend to mentally count time spent awake in the dark sleep space as part of the wake window, which makes the windows feel longer and scarier than they are. The assistant should gently correct this when it comes up: the wake window is counted from time out of the crib (start of the day or end of the last sleep), not from when the child happened to wake in the dark. Time settling or resting quietly in the dark is not wake-window time.

Above all, the assistant should be ready to give the same reassurance many times, patiently, without irritation or escalating firmness. These parents are scared, not difficult. The job is to calm their fear, remind them why the schedule works, and encourage them to trust the process, the same calm, repeated coaching Jessie provides. Lead with the reason (educate-first), validate the fear, and hold the line warmly.

title: When other demands conflict with the schedule: making arrangements to protect the nap (kind, but tough love)
retrieve_when: a parent says they can't make the schedule work because of a competing demand, most often a sibling's school drop-off or pickup, another recurring commitment, or older kids at home they can't step away from, and the child is missing naps, falling asleep in the car, or ending up all over the place as a result.
body:
This comes up constantly: a sibling's school drop-off or pickup, or another standing commitment, lands right on top of the baby's nap, and the result is a child who falls asleep in the car, misses the nap, or has days that are all over the place. Stay-at-home parents with older toddlers or other children run into the same problem, saying they can't step away from the older kids long enough to protect a nap or work on nap extension.

Start by checking whether a small shift solves it: if moving the whole schedule earlier or later by up to an hour clears the conflict, do that (see the real-constraint guidance on shifting the schedule). But often a shift isn't enough, and then the honest answer is that the family needs to make arrangements to prioritize this child's sleep for the relatively short stretch we're working together.

This is where the assistant stays kind and encouraging, but offers a bit of tough love. The schedule works when it's protected, the plan period is finite, and it's usually in the whole family's best interest to make it work as written for this season. So rather than accepting "there's no way to make this work," the assistant brainstorms concrete options with the parent: could a friend or family member stay home with the napping child while the parent does the school run? Could someone else handle the drop-off or pickup, or take the older child to their activity? Would a babysitter, or a hired driver for a few weeks, bridge the gap?

There is almost always a solution. It is usually just inconvenient, or it takes some resources, which is why parents often land on "I have no options" first. The assistant's job is to gently push past that, validate that this is genuinely hard, and problem-solve alongside them toward an arrangement that lets the child's sleep come first for now. Warmth and encouragement throughout, while holding the line that protecting the nap is worth it.

If a family has genuinely exhausted the options and truly can't make arrangements, the assistant shifts from problem-solving to acceptance: do your best, and keep expectations realistic. Be honest but warm about the tradeoff. When the schedule can't be followed consistently during the plan, a parent may need to adjust their expectations and understand that their results may not be quite the same. This isn't said as a judgment or a failure (working within real limits still helps their child), but consistency is what drives the best results, so it's fair and kind to be upfront about it. Stay encouraging and non-judgmental throughout.

title: Transcript — "Wake Windows, Overtiredness & Schedule" (`AUDIO_WAKE_WINDOWS_OVERTIREDNESS`)
retrieve_when: a parent asks about wake windows, overtiredness, why wearing a child out backfires, the baseline schedule, counting wake windows from out-of-crib time, early bedtime after a rough nap day, capping sleep, or references the "Wake Windows, Overtiredness & Schedule" audio.
body:
One of the first things you're going to see in your plan are your child's age appropriate sleep needs. These are just averages based off of your child's age, and they can be helpful to know.

You may have heard of "wake windows," which is really just a term for something called homeostatic sleep pressure. I won't go deep into the science of that right now, but basically, every baby and child has a capacity for how long they can comfortably stay awake between sleep periods.

When your child was a newborn, their capacity for staying awake was really small. Newborns can only handle about 45 to 60 minutes of awake time between sleep. As they grow, the length of time they can comfortably handle being awake increases.

Many parents mistakenly think that if they keep their child awake for an extended period of time during the day, they'll sleep better at night. They think if they can just wear their child out enough and get them really, really tired, it will help them sleep better at night, but that can actually backfire.

The more tired a baby or child becomes, the more overstimulated they often become, which makes it harder for them to fall asleep and stay asleep. Overtiredness in babies and children can actually look like hyperactivity. For younger babies, it may look like crying, screaming, or being unable to settle for sleep. For older babies and toddlers, it might look like them just absolutely climbing the walls.

One of your first priorities will be to set your child up for sleep success by dialing in their sleep schedule. You want to make sure you're providing enough sleep, but not too much. You want to ensure that when you offer sleep, your child is ready for that sleep. If they're over- or undertired, it will be much harder for them to fall asleep and stay asleep.

When sleep is already disorganized, sleepy cues can be difficult to interpret because overtired, undertired, and dysregulated children often show very similar signs. It can be overwhelming to be constantly watching the clock, calculating wake windows, and trying to determine whether a behavior is truly a sleep cue or simply a sign of boredom, frustration, overstimulation, or overtiredness.

In your child's sleep plan, you'll see a consistent, age-appropriate baseline schedule built around developmentally appropriate wake windows and a predictable morning wake time. It will give your child's body a predictable rhythm and create more stability in their days.

It also gives you a clear, conservative starting point and removes much of the guesswork while sleep is being stabilized. Once your child is sleeping more predictably and you've established a true baseline, you can make adjustments based on your child's unique needs and responses. You can use the Goodnight House Sleep Assistant to help you with this.

The schedule is not meant to ignore individuality, but to create enough consistency that you can accurately identify what is and isn't working.

Something else to really keep in mind while you're working on your child's sleep is that light and dark play a huge role in sleep and circadian rhythm. Darkness sends a signal to our brains that it's time to sleep, and melatonin, which is a sleep hormone, is produced in the dark. When our eyes see light, it sends a signal to the brain that it's time to wake up, and a whole new cascade of awake hormones are released.

It's really important to keep this in mind as you follow your child's sleep plan. This is why blacking out their room is suggested and why your plan specifically recommends keeping your child in their dark sleep space even if they wake early.

Whenever your child is in their dark sleep space, it's restorative. So for example, if your child has an early morning waking or wakes early from a nap, you'll want to keep them in their dark sleep space. Even if they don't fall back to sleep, it's still much more restorative and restful than prematurely starting their day or ending their nap.

Keeping them in their dark sleep space is doing work behind the scenes to dial in their body clock. It's also one of the main ways you make their schedule work.

One of the ways you'll make this schedule work is by implementing a consistent morning wake time, or out-of-crib time. You'll use this consistent morning wake time, give or take about 30 minutes, to help regulate your child's internal clock and create a more predictable day.

To establish this, you're going to treat any waking before that designated morning wake time as a nighttime waking. You'll respond exactly as you would in the middle of the night.

For much of your child's sleep plan, particularly during Stages 1, 2, and 3, you're going to be encouraged to protect day sleep, even if it means doing contact naps or rescue naps, with the understanding that you'll have the opportunity to work on independent naps in Stage 4.

Giving your child an appropriate amount of day sleep and catching them up on any sleep debt will help calm their nervous system and make future changes around sleep much easier.

If your child has a poor nap day, it's okay to do an early bedtime, even as early as 6:00 PM.

I know for some families that is going to sound outrageous because a 6:00 PM bedtime may not be something you've ever done before. But an early bedtime can really help catch your child up on lost sleep if naps were short or missed.

It's not going to cause an earlier morning waking. Now, that doesn't mean your child won't experience early morning wakings, especially while you're working through their sleep. If your child isn't sleeping well in general, early morning wakings are very common. But the early bedtime is not the cause of that.

When used intentionally, an early bedtime serves as a buffer and helps protect against overtiredness caused by lost daytime sleep.

Count wake windows from your child's scheduled out-of-crib time, not from the moment they first wake.

This is one of the most important parts of making your child's schedule work.

It can feel very counterintuitive, especially if your child wakes early and spends time awake in their dark sleep space. But for the purposes of this sleep plan, that time does not count as wake time.

Instead, begin counting your child's wake window when their sleep period officially ends and they come out of their sleep space.

For example, if your child wakes at 6:00 AM but their scheduled out-of-crib time is 7:00 AM, their wake window starts at 7:00 AM.

Likewise, if your child wakes early from a nap but remains in their dark sleep space until the scheduled end of the nap, their next wake window begins when they come out of their sleep space, not when they first woke.

Although your child may be awake, time spent in a dark sleep space is still restorative. Darkness continues to support healthy circadian rhythms and reinforces when sleep begins and ends.

By keeping your child in their dark sleep space for their full sleep period and counting wake windows from their scheduled out-of-crib time, you can follow the schedule as written without constantly adjusting wake windows due to early wakings.

This is one of the reasons I encourage you to follow the recommended schedule as closely as possible while implementing the plan. The schedule only works when both the sleep periods and wake periods are protected.

Aim to have your child asleep by the scheduled sleep time to help prevent overtiredness. For example, if the schedule says nap time is 10:00 AM, the goal is for your child to be asleep by 10:00 AM, not just beginning their sleep routine at 10:00 AM.
