Likewise, wake your child at the scheduled out-of-crib time in the morning and at the scheduled end of a nap. Capping sleep helps protect the rest of the day's schedule, including the next nap and bedtime.

Having your child asleep at the appropriate time and awake at the appropriate time are equally important. Together, they help regulate your child's body clock and create a predictable rhythm.

I know for a lot of parents, the idea of following a schedule this closely can feel overwhelming and maybe even a little defeating.

I just want to encourage you that once your child is sleeping better, you will absolutely be able to be more flexible. Your child will be able to skip a nap or go to bed late here and there without it undoing all of your hard work.

But while you're intentionally working on sleep and trying to get things ironed out, the closer you can stick to the schedule, the better.

Another thing to keep in mind for the future is that as your child grows, their schedule grows with them because they'll be able to handle more and more awake time between sleep.

The Goodnight House Sleep App will automatically update your child's schedule. You can also chat with the Goodnight House Sleep Assistant to troubleshoot or make intentional changes to your child's schedule.

title: Awake and content in the sleep space
retrieve_when: a parent mentions their child was awake but calm, happy, or content during a sleep period, or describes trying to "make" a content child sleep. Applies across the board (any sleep period, any stage). A parent may not ask directly — they often just drop a comment, e.g. "yeah my baby was just awake staring at me and when I tried to get them to sleep nothing would work."
body:
If a child is awake and content during a sleep period, the parent does not need to intervene or stress about "making" their child sleep. It's really healthy practice for a child to be awake and content in their sleep space, and intervening denies them that practice.

### Stage 1 → Daycare & Childcare

*For families whose child regularly naps out of the home (daycare, a nanny, a grandparent). Gated by the questionnaire item `childcare_affects_naps` ("Does your child regularly nap somewhere other than home?" = Yes); this content backs the daycare sub-section of the "Making Your Schedule Work" card (`making_the_schedule_work`, DAYCARE_SECTION). The main idea: control what you can at home, keep home consistent, and use bedtime on daycare days as the main way to adjust.*

title: Daycare naps you can't control: make home the anchor and keep expectations realistic
retrieve_when: a parent is frustrated or worried about imperfect daycare naps, fears daycare is undoing their progress, or feels they're failing because out-of-home naps aren't going well.
body:
When a parent is frustrated about naps they cannot control, redirect energy toward consistency at home rather than toward winning battles with the provider. Frame home as the consistent base: a child can sleep well overall even when daycare naps are imperfect, as long as nights and home days stay consistent. Roughly half of children nap well for their caregivers and about half struggle, so a parent whose child is in the struggling group is completely normal and not doing anything wrong.

Children genuinely learn "this is how I sleep at daycare" and "this is how I sleep at home," and adapt better than parents expect. Reassure parents that doing things differently at daycare rarely undoes their progress, as long as they stay consistent at home. Do not treat daycare differences as a threat to the plan. Reassure parents of this often; the fear that daycare will "ruin" progress is extremely common.

Home days (weekends, or whatever days the child is home) are the catch-up. It's fine to run a different schedule at home than at daycare, and don't be surprised if a child sleeps more at home after a week of daycare. The overall tone is do your best and don't stress. Daytime sleep directly affects night sleep, which affects the parent too, so it's worth addressing, but realistic expectations are part of the deal. On daycare days a child may come home a little overtired, and that's okay. Don't imply a parent is failing because daycare naps aren't perfect.

title: Daycare days: bedtime is the main lever (6:00 pm floor, 8:30 pm ceiling)
retrieve_when: a parent asks what to do about bedtime on daycare days, or how to handle a daycare day where the child got too little or too much day sleep.
body: On daycare days, bedtime is the first thing to adjust, and the one thing the parent fully controls, so use it. Too little day sleep means an earlier bedtime that night (down toward 6:00 pm). If the child got more day sleep than usual, bedtime can shift slightly later, but never past 8:30 pm. Hold 8:30 pm as a hard ceiling. The rule of thumb: if the choice is an additional late nap or an early bedtime, choose the early bedtime.

title: Working with the daycare provider, with grace
retrieve_when: a parent wants to communicate with their daycare or caregiver about nap times or the sleep environment, feels awkward or like they're overstepping, is unsure what to ask for, or has a caregiver who isn't honoring their requests.
body:
It is okay, and good, for a parent to raise sleep issues with their caregiver. Parents often feel like they're overstepping, so reassure them they're not. Caregivers don't know what they don't know. The parent knows their child best and should advocate for them, the same way they'd speak up about nutrition. Frame this as helpful, not pushy.

Encourage parents to keep their caregiver updated on where they are in the plan and to share target nap times and environment requests in a friendly, realistic way. Acknowledge that many centers are understaffed and cannot accommodate everything, and help the parent prioritize the one or two requests most likely to be honored. Frame requests as advocacy delivered with grace, not demands, and remember there's only so much a parent or caregiver can control.

When a caregiver isn't respecting requests, explain the reasoning. The parent isn't trying to make the caregiver's life harder, and some flexibility (especially on schedule) is fair, but it isn't fair to the parent to only get small pockets of time with a cranky, overtired child. Keep communication open: let the caregiver know that when nap efforts aren't made, night sleep suffers too, which feeds the cycle. Be specific in requests and tell the caregiver exactly what's working for the child.

title: Choosing a daycare with sleep in mind
retrieve_when: a parent is choosing or evaluating a daycare and wants to know what to look for, or asks what matters for sleep when picking a center.
body:
These are the things worth evaluating for sleep:

- Where does everyone nap? Is it the same room where the other children play, or is there some separation? The best setup is sleeping somewhere dark and away from stimulation.
- Will they follow an age-appropriate schedule, even as the child gets older and the center may want to put everyone on the same schedule?
- Are they willing to lay the child down awake, and to give the child a chance to return to sleep after very short naps?

title: Starting daycare: the adjustment period, and easing in
retrieve_when: a child is about to start daycare, is starting a new room or caregiver, or the parent is returning to work, and naps are short or skipped, the child comes home overtired, or night sleep takes a hit during the transition.
body:
When a child first starts daycare, or moves to a new room or caregiver, expect a genuine adjustment period. Naps are often short or skipped, the child may come home overtired, and night sleep can take a hit during this window. This is normal and almost always temporary. It can take a few days for some kids and a couple of months for others to settle into napping in a new environment.

Set this expectation early so parents don't panic during exactly the window when they're most likely to. Reassure them that daycare kids very often become great nappers once they adjust, and that the job right now is to hold steady: stay consistent at home, use early bedtimes to absorb the rough days, and give both the child and themselves a lot of grace. This is a "ride it out and stay consistent" moment, not a "something is broken" moment.

When returning to work, consider easing in with partial daycare days: a couple of hours for a few days, then half days for a week or so, working up to full days. This softens the adjustment.

title: Accommodations to ask a daycare for (framed as "if you're able to")
retrieve_when: a parent wants to know what specific requests to make of their daycare or caregiver to support their child's sleep.
body:
Frame all of these as "if you're able to" requests, never demands. Pick the ones most likely to stick rather than asking for everything.

- Ask what can be done about the sleep environment, for example the crib furthest from noise, away from windows. A separate room is ideal but often not possible at larger centers.
- Ask whether the caregiver would use a SlumberPod over the crib or pack n play if the parent provides it. It helps a lot with blocking light and sound. Offer the SlumberPod affiliate code and link when you mention it.
- Send a portable white noise machine and ask them to turn it on for naps. It signals nap time the way it does at home and drowns out distracting noise. Offer the portable sound machine affiliate link; that machine is a good size for daycare and runs without being plugged in.
- Ask them to watch sleep cues but also watch the clock, so the child isn't put down too early.
- Send the child's own sleep sack from home.
- Write out a simple cheat sheet or nap instructions for the caregiver. This is helpful, not pushy.

title: Fill-in-the-blank letter a parent can give their caregiver
retrieve_when: a parent wants help communicating their sleep requests to a daycare or caregiver, especially if they feel awkward bringing it up or aren't sure what to ask for.
body:
This is a fill-in-the-blank letter a parent can give their caregiver. It is caregiver-facing, not parent-facing. Offer it when a parent wants help communicating their sleep requests to a daycare or caregiver, especially if they feel awkward bringing it up or aren't sure what to ask for. When you share it, remind the parent to fill in their child's name, wake windows, and nap times, and that they can adjust the requests to fit their situation.

When offering it, keep the framing consistent with Jessie's philosophy: this is helpful advocacy, not overstepping, and they should pair it with grace for a caregiver who can't accommodate everything. The letter intentionally keeps it to three simple requests, because a short, kind, specific ask is the most likely to actually get honored.

Template:

> To Whom It May Concern / Dear ______,
>
> We are the parents of [child's name]. One of our main goals for [him/her] right now is better quality sleep, both at night and during the day, since we know it affects mood, development, and so much more. We're doing our best to keep [him/her] from getting overtired during the day. We know you have many children to care for, so we just have three simple requests for [his/her] daytime sleep. Thank you so much for working with us on this!
>
> [Child's name]'s age-appropriate wake windows are about [x to x] hours. When [he/she] has been awake that long, please offer a chance to sleep. Please move [him/her] away from the activity and put [him/her] in the sleep sack we've provided. Please turn on the sound machine we've provided nearby to soften the surrounding noise and activity.
>
> A rough sample of the schedule we'd like to follow:
> Nap 1: [X-X]
> Nap 2: [X-X]
>
> We hope the work we're doing at home makes it easier for [him/her] to fall asleep and stay asleep at daycare too. Thank you again for helping [child's name] get the sleep [he/she] needs!
>
> Best,
> [parent's name]

title: The short-nap fix at daycare (depends on how the child falls asleep)
retrieve_when: a child takes very short naps at daycare and the parent wants to help lengthen them.
body:
This one depends on how the child falls asleep.

- If the child is assisted to sleep: when they wake from a short nap (less than 45 minutes), the caregiver should assist them right back to sleep. This may mean taking them to a darker space to make that transition back down.
- If the child falls asleep independently: the caregiver should leave them in their sleep space for 15 to 20 minutes after they wake. This motivates the child to keep napping and lengthen the nap on their own.

title: When daycare drops a nap before the child is ready
retrieve_when: a daycare moves a child off a nap (or onto one nap, or off naps entirely) on the center's timeline, before the child is developmentally ready.
body:
Daycares often move a whole room onto one nap (or off naps entirely) on their own timeline, regardless of whether a specific child is ready for it. This is one of the most common daycare frustrations parents bring up, and there's usually nothing the parent can do to stop it.

Reassure them: their child can handle this better than they expect, and the caregiver can handle it too. The fix is the same as always. On daycare days the child may be a little overtired from the dropped nap, so lean on an early bedtime that night. On home days, the parent can keep the child on the number of naps that's actually right for their age.

If the parent wants to push back, they can ask the caregiver to keep their child on their current nap schedule. But if the daycare won't or can't, it's not worth a fight. Control the home days, use early bedtime, and trust the child to adapt.

It is not unreasonable to float the option that parents can always consider looking for different child care arrangements to help advocate for their child's sleep needs.

title: The drive-home car nap (keep it from wrecking bedtime)
retrieve_when: a daycare child falls asleep in the car on the drive home in the late afternoon, and that catnap is affecting bedtime.
body: This is a daily problem for daycare families: the child crashes on the drive home around late afternoon, and that little car nap influences bedtime. If the parent can keep the child awake in the car, that's the goal, even if it means singing, talking, windows down, whatever it takes. A late catnap will push bedtime later and can be tough. If the child does fall asleep, keep it short and then lean on an appropriate bedtime. The bigger principle holds: when the choice is a late nap or an early bedtime, choose the early bedtime. Night sleep is more restorative than a late-day catnap, so an early bedtime is almost always the better trade.

title: Helping a caregiver (nanny or family member) get confident
retrieve_when: a parent has a nanny, grandparent, or other family caregiver who needs to learn how the child sleeps, or lacks confidence putting the child down.
body:
Practice the routine together. Have the caregiver put the child down while the parent watches, or watch the parent do it. If the child fusses, the parent can show how they handle it. A few days of shadowing builds the caregiver's confidence in how the child sleeps.

### Stage 1 → Nutrition and Sleep

*Backs the Nutrition and Sleep card (Foundations CARD 5): a universal opener (`nutrition_and_sleep_opener`), a baby variant (`nutrition_and_sleep_baby`, age route <24mo, with a 12mo+ older-baby version), and a toddler variant (`nutrition_and_sleep_toddler`, 24-29mo). Feeding in the Goodnight House method is responsive and cue-led, never clock-led.*

title: Nutrition and sleep: scope and philosophy (the opener)
retrieve_when: a parent asks about feeding, nursing, bottles, solids, night feeds, snacking, or how nutrition affects sleep.
body:
This is Jessie's nutrition philosophy. Always defer to the parent's pediatrician and lactation consultant for a child's individual medical and nutritional needs, and always reinforce feeding on hunger cues.

Scope: this covers how feeding affects sleep, which is in scope; anything clinical or specific about breastfeeding, weight gain, milk transfer, supply, etc. is out of scope and gets referred out to a pediatrician or lactation consultant.

title: How a child's daily caloric intake supports their sleep
retrieve_when: a parent asks how feeding amounts or snacking affect sleep, why full daytime feeds matter, or is worried about night wakings from hunger.
body:
Every baby has a baseline amount of calories they need in a 24-hour period. As they grow, their baseline grows. After the newborn stage, these calories can either remain spread across day and night, or gradually shift toward daytime feeding.

Short frequent feedings ("snacking") can lead to:

- Incomplete or inefficient feedings
- Increased night wakings due to hunger
- Discomfort that mimics reflux (gassiness, spitting up, etc.)

Supporting fuller, more effective daytime feedings allows your baby to:

- Take more at each feed
- Need fewer total feeds
- Meet caloric needs during the daytime hours

Frequent snacking and never getting a full, satisfying feed can also cause tummy discomfort and spitting up, and it often runs alongside chronic overtiredness, so both feeding and sleep get thrown off, with too much of the eating and waking ending up overnight instead of during the day. Working on feeding and sleep together is what gets things back on track.

title: Toddler milk: comfort and connection, not primary nutrition
retrieve_when: a parent of a toddler (roughly 24 months and up) asks about milk or nursing, worries about continued nursing, is nursing to sleep, or has a toddler grazing on milk all day, nursing very frequently day or night, or carrying a bottle or sippy cup of milk everywhere.
body:
For toddlers, milk is comfort and connection, not primary nutrition. By toddlerhood, food leads and milk plays a supporting role. Coach toward milk at mealtimes and a predictable nursing routine rather than all-day grazing or feeding to sleep. Never shame a parent for continued nursing; the goal is rhythm, not weaning.

Jessie has worked with plenty of toddlers caught in a "snacking" cycle with nursing or bottles. It looks different than with younger babies, but the pattern is similar. If your toddler nurses very frequently throughout the day (more than 4-5x) or nurses throughout the night, or carries a bottle or sippy cup of milk everywhere, this frequent milk consumption can affect their sleep and circadian rhythm. Strategies families find helpful:

- Offer milk only at mealtimes or snack times in the highchair. This reinforces that milk is for nutrition. Ensure your child is also drinking plenty of water throughout the day.
- Switch from bottles to sippy cups. This can help break the grazing habit.
- Create a predictable nursing routine. Example: nurse upon waking, after naps, and at the start of bedtime. Predictable timing allows continued breastfeeding and bonding without impacting sleep.
- Prioritize whole, unprocessed foods. Research shows this can improve sleep. Limit processed foods, soft drinks, and food dyes.

title: Never frame this as reducing calories
retrieve_when: a parent asks about cutting back, reducing, or restricting feeds or calories (often hoping it will help sleep), or the amount of a child's daytime feeds comes up.
body: Always feed the child when the parent believes they're hungry. The goal is fuller, more complete daytime feeds, never restriction, skipped meals, or fewer calories. The goal is to avoid the snacking pattern and aim for good full feedings, not cutting back.

title: Hunger vs. habit: pause, assess, then respond
retrieve_when: a parent is unsure whether a feed is true hunger or a habit / resettling feed, asks whether to drop or withhold a feed, wants to tell hunger wakings from habit wakings, or is night weaning and wondering whether a night waking is hunger or habit.
source: The Goodnight House Healthy Sleep Guide, "Hunger vs. Habit" (patterns reproduced verbatim)
body:
When a parent is night weaning or wondering whether a night waking is due to hunger or habit, the assistant helps the parent think it through rather than deciding for them.

The assistant asks questions, compares the parent's description to the common patterns below, and returns the read to the parent. It does not declare with certainty that a waking is hunger or habit. Hunger is individual, and when in doubt, the parent should feed their child. The assistant remains in a coaching posture and defers medical questions to the child's pediatrician.

Use the hunger-vs-habit guidance to help parents tell true hunger from feeding-to-resettle. Never push a parent to withhold a feed the child genuinely needs. The frame is "pause, assess, then respond," not "don't feed." When in doubt, feeding a hungry baby is always right.

Framing from the guide: *"Sometimes our children wake out of habit instead of out of true hunger. Below is a helpful guide for understanding hunger vs. habitual wakings:"*

**Hunger**

- Baby can be laid down wide awake and falls asleep easily after a feeding.
- Baby is alert and able to take a full feeding and then falls asleep knowing they are in the bassinet or crib and/or knowing you have left the room.
- Baby is showing hunger cues after a long stretch of sleep.
- Your baby only requires one feeding during the night, typically in the early morning hours, and is able to fall back to sleep unassisted.

**Habit**

- Your baby continues to cry after a feeding and/or requires a significant amount of help to fall back to sleep.
- Your baby only suckles for a short time and falls right back to sleep. Your baby may wake immediately after being laid back down and want to suckle a little more.
- Your baby wants to feed every few hours through the night (excluding growth spurts, which typically include increased daytime feedings as well).
- Your baby requires frequent feedings through the night and must be completely asleep before being laid down.

**Important:** These are common patterns, not diagnostic criteria. The assistant should use them to guide the conversation, not to make definitive conclusions about an individual child.

title: Refer breastfeeding and feeding-medical questions out (pediatrician or lactation consultant)
retrieve_when: a parent raises a clinical feeding or breastfeeding concern, such as supply, milk transfer, latch, whether the baby is transferring enough, pain, weight gain, reflux, or ties.
body:
The assistant is a sleep resource, not a feeding or medical provider, and should say so plainly when a question crosses that line. For specific breastfeeding or feeding-medical concerns (supply, milk transfer, latch, whether a baby is transferring enough, pain, weight gain, reflux, ties), the assistant does not diagnose or advise. It warmly encourages the parent to consult their pediatrician or a lactation consultant.

When referring to a lactation consultant, make Jessie's specific, practical version of the referral, not a generic one: suggest the parent ask for a weighted feed to assess milk transfer, and encourage them to look for a lactation consultant who takes sleep into consideration. The assistant can name plainly that this isn't medical advice, in the same warm, direct way Jessie does, framing the referral as the genuinely helpful next step rather than a brush-off.

**Guardrail:** never give clinical feeding advice (supply, transfer, medication, reflux treatment, ties, etc.). Refer out every time, and reassure the parent that getting the right eyes on it is the smart move.

title: Do not give strict feeding schedules; stay principle-level on timing
retrieve_when: a parent asks how often or when to feed, or wants a feeding schedule or set feeding times.
body:
Jessie intentionally does not give families a feeding schedule, and the assistant should not either. Feeding is responsive and cue-led, not clock-led.

When a parent asks how often or when to feed, the assistant offers the principle and stops there: aim for at least 4 full feeding sessions a day when milk is still the child's main nutrition, full feeds over frequent snacking, and always feed on hunger cues. If on solids, offer 3 meals per day at family meal times. It does NOT convert this into specific feeding times, a feeding clock, or a prescribed interval. If a parent pushes for an exact schedule, the assistant gently explains that Jessie's approach keeps feeding responsive to the child's cues rather than on a fixed clock, and that the goal is full feeds and avoiding the snacking pattern, not hitting set times.

**Guardrail:** principle, not prescription. "At least 4 full feeds (when milk is primary), feed on cues, avoid snacking" is the ceiling of specificity. Never output a feeding timetable. The "4 full feeds" floor is a milk-is-primary number; do not apply it to toddlers.

Cross-reference: night-feed weaning guidance lives in a separate block, not here. This resource builds full daytime feeds; the weaning decision (parent's choice, pediatrician-cleared, typically after sleep improves) is handled there.

title: Handling "how often / how far apart should I feed?"
retrieve_when: a parent asks how often or how far apart to feed during the day.
body:
Always start with "feed your child when they're hungry," paired with the feed-on-demand reframe (feeding on demand does not mean feeding at every cry; babies cry for many reasons; as babies grow, feeds naturally space out).

The assistant may offer that for a healthy baby past the newborn stage, growing well, with no feeding or weight-gain concerns, whose main source of nutrition is breastmilk or formula, it is generally fine for daytime feeds to space up to about every 3.5 to 4 hours. Frame this as a ceiling and as permission to space feeds up to there, not as a target to hit and never as a clock to feed by.

Always pair the number with the pediatrician backstop: whenever the 3.5 to 4 hour guidance is given, pair it with a clear recommendation to check with their pediatrician, especially if there are any weight or growth concerns. If the parent mentions any feeding difficulty, weight or growth concern, reflux, supply worry, or similar, do NOT give the spacing number; refer to the pediatrician and/or a lactation consultant instead (see the referral rule and the weighted-feed guidance).

On the other side of spacing: if a baby older than about 5 months isn't going more than roughly 3 hours between feeds on average (emphasize average), it's a good idea to check in with a lactation consultant. Ask for a weighted feed, assess milk transfer, and discuss feeding frequency to rule out any underlying issues. If no concerns are found, gently working to space feedings a bit more can be helpful. In many cases it's a pattern of snacking that leads to sleep disruptions and tummy discomfort.

**Standing disclaimer (attach whenever spacing or intervals are discussed):** "This is general guidance to support sleep, not medical or feeding advice. Every baby is different, and your pediatrician and a lactation consultant are the right people to advise on your individual child's feeding needs."

**Never:** output a feeding timetable or set feeding times; push spacing for any baby with feeding, weight, or growth concerns.

title: "Feed on demand" doesn't mean feed at every cry (responsive, not automatic or rigid)
retrieve_when: a parent feeds in response to every cry, asks what "feed on demand" means, worries they're feeding too much or too little, or follows a rigid by-the-clock feeding approach.
body:
When you leave the hospital, everyone tells you to feed on demand, but this is rarely explained or defined. Most parents hear that as: anytime the baby cries, try feeding. But babies cry for more than hunger. When feeding becomes the default response to every cry, we can completely miss that a baby is tired, uncomfortable, or needs something else.

Jessie often sees parents fall into one of two extremes. On one end, some parents offer a feeding in response to every cry, without pausing to consider other needs. While this comes from a loving place, it can mean missing cues that the baby is overtired, undertired, uncomfortable, or needs a diaper change. On the other end are parents who follow a rigid "by the clock" schedule, feeding only at predetermined times regardless of what their baby is communicating.

Both approaches overlook the same thing: the baby's cues. The goal is to watch the baby, stay flexible, and respond to what they're telling you in the moment. Some days a baby will need an extra feeding, during growth spurts. That is responsive parenting, not a step backward.

title: Why babies wake at night, and why not to auto-feed every waking
retrieve_when: a child is waking at night and the parent wants to understand why, or defaults to feeding at every waking.
body:
If your child is waking at night, it is often for one of a few reasons:

- Hunger
- Overtired (didn't get enough day sleep)
- Under-tired (got too much day sleep)
- Habits around sleep
- Poor sleep hygiene (a poor sleep environment, etc.)

If your default response to every waking is to feed without first understanding why they woke, it can disrupt their circadian rhythm, blur hunger cues, and reinforce nighttime feedings that aren't biologically needed.

Always feed your child when they're hungry, but remember that responsive parenting doesn't mean automatic feeding. Babies wake for many reasons, and it's okay to pause, assess, and then respond.

title: Why nursing or feeding to sleep makes cries hard to read
retrieve_when: a baby is nursed or fed to sleep and the parent struggles to tell whether wakings are hunger or habit, or asks why separating feeding from sleep matters.
body:
A baby who is nursed or fed to sleep ends up associating two different things with the same act. They associate nutrition with nursing or bottle feeding, and they associate nursing or bottle feeding with sleep. So when they wake and cry, it's genuinely hard to know what they need: are they hungry, or are they just looking for the thing that puts them to sleep?

When you lay a healthy sleep foundation and separate feeding from falling asleep, those cries become much easier to read. If a baby had a good full feed an hour ago, a parent can reason that this waking probably isn't hunger, and look at other needs instead. Helping a child associate feeding with nutrition (not sleep) is part of what makes the rest of the plan work.

title: Tips for complete feedings
retrieve_when: a parent wants help getting fuller, more complete feeds, or the baby seems distracted or snacky at feeds.
body:
- Feed in a calm, low-distraction environment if your baby seems distracted. Try a quiet room with a sound machine if needed.
- Pause to burp or re-engage your baby mid-feed to keep them alert and actively eating.
- Check milk temperature and ensure nipple flow is age-appropriate.

title: Background on feeding frequency (assistant-facing, not parent talking points)
retrieve_when: a parent worries about spacing feeds or about not feeding every 2.5-3 hours, and the assistant needs grounding to respond calmly.
body:
This background exists so the assistant stays calm, confident, and non-alarmist when a parent worries about spacing feeds. It is for the assistant's understanding, not parent talking points.

Most parents have heard that babies should feed every 2.5 to 3 hours. That guidance comes from the newborn period, when frequent feeding establishes milk supply and ensures weight gain. It often gets carried forward to older babies without a strong basis. After the newborn stage, major guidelines move toward responsive, cue-based feeding rather than a fixed clock interval, and feeding frequency among healthy babies varies widely.

title: Dream feeds
retrieve_when: a parent asks about dream feeds, offering a feed while the baby is still asleep (typically before the parent's own bedtime, often 9-11 PM) to provide calories before the baby naturally wakes hungry and to stretch the longest sleep.
body:
A dream feed is a feeding offered while a baby is still asleep, typically before the parent goes to bed for the night. The goal is to provide calories before the baby naturally wakes from hunger, potentially allowing both the baby and parent to achieve a longer stretch of sleep. A typical dream feed is offered between 9:00 PM and 11:00 PM, though timing varies between families.

**The Goodnight House perspective.** Dream feeds are a tool, not a requirement. Some families find them helpful, particularly during the newborn stage and early infancy; other babies see little benefit, and some babies actually sleep worse when their sleep is interrupted for a dream feed. There is very little high-quality research demonstrating whether dream feeds are effective, and most recommendations are based on anecdotal experience rather than strong evidence. Because every baby responds differently, parents may choose to try a dream feed and evaluate whether it improves sleep for their individual child.

**When dream feeds are most helpful.** They tend to be most useful during the newborn period, in younger infants with multiple night feedings, when a baby consistently wakes shortly after the parent falls asleep, when additional calories are beneficial, or when a family is looking to maximize their longest stretch of sleep. The goal is often to shift a feeding that would have occurred shortly after the parent went to bed to a more convenient time.

**When dream feeds become less helpful.** As babies mature, dream feeds often become less effective. Around 4-6 months of age, many babies become more alert and may wake more fully during the feeding. In some cases, dream feeds may disrupt sleep, lead to difficulty settling afterward, create an unnecessary habitual feeding, or fragment overnight sleep. If a baby consistently wakes fully during the dream feed or struggles to return to sleep afterward, it may be time to discontinue it. Generally consider weaning it between 4-6 months if it's no longer clearly helping, and after 6 months the goal should be to work toward discontinuing it, as it likely won't be helpful any longer. Don't offer a dream feed as the fix for every night waking; first consider whether the waking is actually hunger-related.

**How to offer a dream feed.** The goal is usually to keep the baby as sleepy as possible while still allowing them to feed: keep lights dim, keep interactions minimal, feed as you normally would, burp if needed, and return the baby to their sleep space after the feeding. There is no specific amount a baby must consume during a dream feed; some babies take a full feeding while others only take a small amount.

**Safety considerations.** Dream feeds should be performed the same way daytime feeds are performed. Do not prop bottles, do not feed a baby while lying flat, and do not feed while excessively drowsy or at risk of falling asleep yourself.

**Dropping a dream feed.** Most dream feeds eventually become unnecessary. Signs a dream feed may no longer be helping include the baby sleeping the same with or without it, waking fully during the feeding, struggling to settle afterward, or the feeding seeming habitual rather than hunger-driven. Because the dream feed is parent-led, parents often just don't realize they can go ahead and stop; the parent is the one doing it, often out of habit. Families can test a shorter or smaller dream feed for a few nights and then trial not doing it to see how the baby responds. Families may also choose to gradually reduce the amount offered, gradually shorten the feeding, or stop altogether. There is no single correct approach. The detailed night-feed weaning specifics live in Stage 4.

**Important considerations and medical boundary.** Before attempting to eliminate a dream feed, consider whether the baby is developmentally ready, whether weight gain is appropriate, whether the pediatrician has expressed any feeding concerns, and whether the baby is receiving adequate daytime calories. Night feedings should not be eliminated solely for sleep reasons if a baby still requires them nutritionally. Route feeding-medical questions to the pediatrician (see the feeding-referral rule). The assistant should walk the parent through the hunger-vs-habit information, or direct them to that resource in the app library (the Hunger vs Habit PDF).

title: Why solids matter more after 12 months (toddler barely eating solids, still mostly nursing)
retrieve_when: the log or the parent indicates a child over 12 months is eating very little solid food and is still primarily nursing or bottle-feeding, especially alongside night wakings that could be hunger-driven.
body:
When the log or a parent indicates a child over 12 months is eating very little solid food and is still primarily nursing or bottle-feeding, especially alongside overnight wakings that could be hunger-driven, the assistant can note that the lack of solids may be contributing to genuine overnight hunger, and suggest working toward about three full meals a day on top of breast milk or formula.

For a child over 12 months who isn't getting much in the way of solids, it's worth understanding why solid food becomes increasingly important after the first year. Some reasons:

- **Meeting growing nutritional needs.** Breast milk continues to provide many wonderful benefits, but toddlers also rely on solid foods to meet increasing needs for calories, protein, iron, zinc, healthy fats, fiber, and other key nutrients that support growth and development.
- **Iron remains especially important.** Iron needs stay relatively high during the toddler years to support healthy brain development, growth, and red blood cell production, and breast milk is no longer a significant iron source. Iron-rich foods become an important part of meeting those needs.
- **Oral-motor development.** Learning to chew different textures, bite, move food around the mouth, and drink from cups are important developmental skills that develop through regular opportunities to eat a variety of foods.
- **Developing a healthy relationship with food.** The toddler years are when children begin learning family mealtime routines, self-feeding skills, and acceptance of a wide variety of foods. Frequent nursing can sometimes reduce appetite for these important learning opportunities.
- **Supporting healthy hunger and fullness cues.** If a child is nursing very frequently throughout the day, there may be less opportunity to arrive at meals hungry enough to eat meaningful amounts of solid food. Spacing milk feeds can help support a more predictable appetite for meals and snacks.
- **Sleep may improve.** Nutrition is rarely the sole cause of sleep difficulties, but some toddlers who nurse frequently consume a large portion of their calories overnight. Encouraging more daytime calories through meals and snacks can help reduce genuine overnight hunger and may make night weaning easier (if night weaning is the family's goal). The detailed night-weaning specifics live in Stage 4.

**Boundaries.** Frame this as adding solids and arriving at meals hungry, never as restricting milk or cutting calories, and always feed on hunger cues. "Three full meals a day" is a structure suggestion, not a feeding clock; don't output feeding times or intervals. Don't assert hunger as the certain cause of the wakings. Route any feeding difficulty, a child who genuinely won't eat, or weight or growth concerns to the pediatrician.

title: Transcript — "Nutrition and Sleep" (`AUDIO_NUTRITION_AND_SLEEP`)
retrieve_when: a parent whose child receives overnight feeds/calories asks about nutrition and sleep, the snacking cycle, feeding frequency, reverse cycling, overnight micro-feedings, snooze-button feedings, distracted feeds, or references the "Nutrition and Sleep" audio.
delivered_to: families who indicate their child receives overnight feeds/calories. ⚠️ confirm the exact intake field name (e.g. overnight_feeds / night_feeds).
body:
Nutrition and sleep go hand in hand.

One of the biggest things I see with the families I work with is babies and toddlers who are stuck in what I call a "snacking cycle."

This is when a child nurses, takes a bottle, or eats very frequently throughout the day and night. They eat so often that their stomach never really has a chance to get completely full or completely empty. They're basically just being topped off all day long.

This can lead to inefficient or incomplete feedings. It can contribute to increased night wakings due to hunger, and it can sometimes cause tummy discomfort that parents mistake for reflux. Things like gassiness, squirming, frequent spit-up, or general discomfort can all be part of the picture.

Every baby and toddler has a baseline number of calories they need in a 24-hour period. As they grow, that baseline grows too.

After the newborn stage, those calories can either remain spread throughout the day and night, or they can gradually shift toward daytime feedings. Because of this, I spend a lot of time helping families support fuller, more effective daytime feedings.

When babies and toddlers take in more calories during the day, they often need fewer feedings overall. They can meet more of their nutritional needs during waking hours rather than relying on frequent overnight feedings.

Sometimes babies and toddlers who take in a large amount of their calories overnight naturally take in fewer calories during the day. This is often referred to as reverse cycling.

This same concept applies to older babies and toddlers who are still nursing or taking bottles frequently throughout the day and night. Sometimes a child can become stuck in a snacking pattern where they're taking in small amounts very frequently rather than taking in fuller feedings, meals, or snacks during the daytime hours.

One of the first things I encourage families to look at is feeding frequency.

If your baby is older than about five months and is rarely going more than 2.5 to 3 hours between feedings during the day, it may be worth checking in with your pediatrician or a lactation consultant. They can assess milk transfer, perform a weighted feed if appropriate, and help rule out any underlying feeding concerns.

If no concerns are found, gently working toward a little more spacing between feedings can often be very helpful.

Another thing that can help is reducing distractions during feedings.

If your baby seems more interested in looking around than eating, try feeding in a calm, low-distraction environment. A quiet room, dim lighting, or even a sound machine can sometimes help.

For babies who are extremely distracted, some families find it helpful to loosely swaddle during feeds only. This is not for sleep and should not be reintroduced for sleep. The goal is simply to help contain busy little hands long enough for them to focus on eating.

You can also pause during feeds to burp, change a diaper, reposition them, or re-engage them if they seem to be getting sleepy or losing interest.

For bottle-fed babies, it's also worth checking milk temperature and making sure the nipple flow is appropriate.

If milk is coming out too slowly, babies can become frustrated or lose interest. If it's coming out too quickly, feeding can feel overwhelming. Sometimes small adjustments here can make a big difference.

When it comes to nighttime calories, it's important to remember that frequent small feeds can really add up.

Even if each feeding seems tiny, babies and toddlers can sometimes consume a surprising amount of milk overnight through multiple "micro-feedings."

A few clues that this might be happening include very large overnight diapers, diapers that are completely saturated in the morning, or a child who isn't particularly interested in their first feeding or meal of the day.

These can all be signs that they're taking in a surprising amount of calories overnight.

As sleep improves, overnight calories often begin to decrease naturally and daytime intake often increases to compensate.

The goal here is not necessarily to eliminate nighttime feedings. Many babies still need legitimate nighttime calories, and every child is different.

The goal is simply to ensure that daytime feeding is as effective as possible and that nutrition isn't unintentionally contributing to sleep disruptions.

Another strategy many families find helpful is what I call a snooze button feeding.

If your baby consistently wakes between about 4:00 and 5:00 AM, you can try offering a small feeding to help extend the night.

The key is keeping this feeding small. You don't want it replacing the first feeding of the day.

Instead, think of it as a bridge between the early morning waking and your desired wake time. It should be just enough to keep your child comfortable and help them settle back to sleep until morning. For an older toddler, a small, quick snack, such as half of a banana, can work well here.

For toddlers over 12 months, it's also important to make sure they're getting regular opportunities for meals and snacks throughout the day. As children get older, solid foods become an increasingly important source of calories, nutrients, and energy.

Sometimes toddlers continue to rely heavily on nursing, bottles, or overnight calories and end up eating very little during the day. In some cases, this can contribute to hunger overnight and make sleep more challenging.

If your toddler is nursing or taking a bottle very frequently and only eating small amounts of solid food, it may be worth taking a closer look at how their calories are distributed throughout the day and night.

One final nutrition tip while you're actively working through your child's sleep plan is to avoid introducing new foods whenever possible.

The reason being: if your child has a sensitivity to a newly introduced food, it can become very difficult to tell whether nighttime disruptions are related to sleep or related to the food.

During sleep support, I generally encourage families to keep as many variables as possible the same. That way, if your child starts waking more, becomes uncomfortable, or seems fussier than usual, it's easier to identify what's actually causing it.

Once sleep is more stable, you can absolutely continue introducing new foods as normal. But while you're actively working through sleep support, keeping nutrition predictable can be really helpful.

title: Avoid introducing new foods during active sleep support
retrieve_when: a parent asks whether they can introduce new foods, start solids, or change their child's diet while actively working through the sleep plan; or reports new wakings or fussiness after recently introducing a new food.
body: While a family is actively working through the sleep plan, Jessie generally encourages them to avoid introducing new foods whenever possible. If a child has a sensitivity to a newly introduced food, it can become very difficult to tell whether nighttime disruptions are related to sleep or related to the food. During sleep support, keeping as many variables as possible the same makes it easier to identify what's actually causing a change: if the child starts waking more, becomes uncomfortable, or seems fussier than usual, the cause is clearer. Once sleep is more stable, families can absolutely continue introducing new foods as normal. This is a "keep it predictable for now" guideline for the active working period, not a long-term restriction.

title: Snooze-button feeding (a small early-morning feed to bridge to wake time)
retrieve_when: a parent asks about feeding a child who wakes around 4:00–5:00am, or wants a way to bridge an early-morning waking to the desired wake time without starting the day.
body: If a baby consistently wakes between about 4:00 and 5:00 AM, a parent can try offering a small feeding to help extend the night. Jessie calls this a snooze button feeding. The key is keeping it small: it should not replace the first feeding of the day. Think of it as a bridge between the early-morning waking and the desired wake time, just enough to keep the child comfortable and help them settle back to sleep until morning. For an older toddler, a small, quick snack such as half a banana can work well. Avoid feeding the child all the way to sleep; if they rarely settle back after the small feed, a quick check-in is fine instead. (Early-morning wakings during specific nap transitions are also addressed in Troubleshooting.)

title: When feeding has become the default response to every waking
retrieve_when: a parent feeds at every cry or night waking, isn't sure whether a waking is hunger or something else, or worries that feeding has become their automatic go-to. Also when coaching a family through separating feeding from falling asleep.
body:
It is very common for feeding to become the default response to every cry or night waking. Most parents fall into this pattern without even realizing it, especially when everyone is exhausted. The assistant should be encouraging and reassuring, never shaming.

When feeding becomes the automatic response to every waking, it can become difficult to tell what the child is actually needing in that moment. Sometimes they may be hungry, but other times they may simply need comfort, help connecting sleep cycles, or reassurance.

If a child who is not genuinely hungry is routinely fed back to sleep, several things can happen:

- It can reinforce the snacking pattern discussed in Stage 1, making it harder for the child to take full feedings during the day and, in some cases, contribute to reverse cycling.
- It may contribute to tummy discomfort or gas if the child is repeatedly feeding when they are not hungry.
- It can make it harder for the child to learn other ways of falling asleep when hunger is not the reason they woke.

Always remind parents that the goal is never to withhold feeds from a hungry child. Children should always be fed when they are genuinely hungry. The goal is simply to separate feeding from falling asleep so that feeding is no longer the default response to every waking.

title: Interpreting feeding gaps (day vs. night: long overnight = good, long daytime = watch)
retrieve_when: the assistant is reviewing a feeding log and interpreting gaps between feeds, a parent worries about a long overnight stretch, or a milk-primary child is going unusually long between daytime feeds.
body:
The assistant should interpret daytime and nighttime feeding gaps differently. The same length of time between feeds does not mean the same thing during the day as it does overnight.

**Overnight.** Longer stretches between overnight feeds are generally a positive sign. As children begin sleeping longer, they naturally consolidate feeds and go longer without eating overnight. Do not flag a healthy long overnight stretch as a concern. Instead, reassure parents that this is often a sign of progress.

**Daytime.** For children under 12 months, or older children whose primary nutrition is still breast milk or formula, daytime milk feeds should generally not be spaced more than 4 hours apart. This is a maximum interval, not a feeding schedule — a 4-hour daytime gap is completely acceptable. Only draw attention to gaps longer than 4 hours, since prolonged daytime gaps may contribute to underfeeding during the day, increased night wakings, or reverse cycling. The guidance is simply to avoid routinely exceeding 4 hours between daytime milk feeds. (See `resource_feed_spacing_guidance` for the full daytime spacing guidance and the pediatrician/lactation backstops.)

**Exception.** Once a child is over 12 months and solids have become the primary source of nutrition, this 4-hour guideline no longer applies. These children can naturally go longer than 4 hours between daytime milk feeds without concern.

**Coaching tip.** When reviewing feeding logs, celebrate longer overnight stretches as progress while paying closer attention to unusually long daytime gaps for children who still rely primarily on milk for nutrition.

title: Night feed coaching: the 4-hour rule (a gut check, not an instruction)
retrieve_when: coaching overnight feeds and the 4-hour mark comes up — a parent asks whether to feed because it's been about 4 hours, or is feeding by the clock overnight.
body:
When coaching night feeds, the 4-hour mark is a gut check, not an instruction to feed.

Once it has been about 4 hours since the last feed, hunger becomes a reasonable possibility and should be considered. It does not mean the child automatically needs to eat.

Likewise, a child who was already sleeping through the night before starting the plan should not be fed simply because 4 hours have passed.

Instead, the assistant should consider the child's established feeding pattern, use the normal hunger-discernment coaching, and help the parent think through whether hunger is likely.

Growth spurts, illness, or other circumstances can make a child genuinely hungry at times they normally wouldn't be. Never rule hunger out simply because it isn't typical.

If the parent believes their child is genuinely hungry, always support feeding.

The purpose of the 4-hour guideline is to prevent reflexively feeding by the clock—not to prevent feeding a hungry child.

title: Stage 2 method overview (the map)
retrieve_when: orienting to how Stage 2 (the Sleep Ladder) is structured — a parent asks why their plan is ordered a certain way, how the two tracks (bedsharing vs. other families) work, where a discovered sleep association fits on the ladder, or how the ladder hands off to Stage 3; or the assistant needs the big-picture Stage 2 map. The per-track step detail lives in resource_bedsharing_ladder_steps and resource_main_ladder_steps.
body:
**Purpose:** the Goodnight House Sleep Ladder. Sleep habits often form gradually, and they can be changed gradually too. The plan reduces the support the child needs to fall asleep one step at a time (see resource_step_down_pattern and resource_one_small_change_at_a_time). Stage 2 is continued prep work: it sets the child up for success before the plan introduces asking the child to put themselves to sleep (Stage 3). Each family enters the ladder at their heaviest association and steps down from there. A recurring principle across every step: use the least amount of intervention necessary.

**The shape of every Stage 2 plan:** it opens with the intro, the Habit Changing audio, and a Before You Begin pacing note; the middle is the family's step-down work; it closes with the General Tips. The exception is the already-ahead family, whose Stage 2 is the acknowledgment card alone.

**Opening blocks** (for any family with a sleep association to reduce):
- Reducing Sleep Assistance intro: the ladder framing.
- Habit Changing audio from Jessie (see resource_audio_habit_changing).
- Before You Begin: pacing. Some families move through Stage 2 in just a few days, others take about a week or so, depending on their comfort level. What matters most is that they keep moving forward (see resource_plan_timeline).
- Skip Ahead note (conditional): for the non-bedsharing family who reported being unable to transfer their child to the crib. If after a few days of applying Stage 1 and attempting these steps they still can't transfer successfully, they may move ahead to Stage 3. This is not a bypass: their step-down steps still fire and are still worth completing. Reducing the associations (feeding, motion) before Stage 3 makes the work there much easier. The note is permission to move ahead when the transfer keeps failing despite real attempts.
- Milk vs. low-calorie drinks note (conditional): for the child who falls asleep with both milk and a low-calorie drink (water, juice, and the like). Milk and formula contain significant calories, so the steps treat them carefully: the goal is to stop offering them as a way to fall asleep, never to withhold a feed when the child is genuinely hungry. A low-calorie drink is different: it's not nourishment, it's a habit that formed around sleep, so it can simply be phased out and replaced with other soothing. Anything in the steps about hunger or feeding refers to milk or formula only. Example: a family who feeds at bedtime but offers water through the night. The hunger notes don't apply to those water wakings. (See resource_hunger_vs_habit.)

**The Sleep Ladder: bedsharing families** (bedsharing, child sleeping in the parents' bed, and the parent chose at intake to move their child out of the bed gradually). The detailed step-by-step guidance for this track is in resource_bedsharing_ladder_steps.

**The Sleep Ladder: all other families** (not bedsharing, or child already sleeping in their own room, or the night-one family after the transition). Same ladder, different starting point. The detailed step-by-step guidance for this track is in resource_main_ladder_steps.

**Part-night bedsharing** (child starts the night in their own sleep space but ends up in the parents' bed after a waking): at night wakings, use the least intervention necessary to assist back to sleep and return the child to the crib rather than bringing them into the bed, continuing until the parents' own bedtime or around midnight, and pushing toward the full night in the crib over 1-3 nights. By night 3, aim to be fully committed to all-night crib sleep.

**The already-ahead family** (no bedsharing, no feeding to sleep, no motion, no pacifier — covers holding-still, in-crib soothing, and fully independent children): a single acknowledgment card. Based on the questionnaire, their child is falling asleep with little or no assistance; there are no Stage 2 steps to work through, and the card tells them to tap through to Stage 3. The General Tips are suppressed; the card stands alone as their whole Stage 2 plan. The exception: a pacifier-only family (pacifier but no other association) runs the pacifier reduction work plus the General Tips instead of the acknowledgment card.

**Add-ons** (fire alongside the main work on any track):
- Begin Reducing Pacifier Use, for pacifier families, with The Pacifier audio (full protocol in resource_pacifier_stage2_reduce; the audio is age-banded: resource_audio_the_pacifier_under_12mo and resource_audio_the_pacifier_12mo_to_2yr). This is reduction only: limit the pacifier to sleep times and begin offering chances to fall asleep without it. The lovey and the keep-or-drop pointer show at 12 months and older; the full drop is the parent's decision in Stage 3 (see resource_pacifier_keep_or_drop_by_age; safety in resource_pacifier_safety).
- Contact behaviors tip, for the child who pinches, twists, pulls hair, rubs, scratches, or grips a parent to fall asleep: gently stop the behavior every time and stay completely consistent. The assistant handles the per-behavior specifics in chat (see resource_contact_behaviors).

**General Tips** (close every Stage 2 plan except the acknowledgment-card plans):
- Naps: the main priority is protecting quality day sleep. Continue assisting naps however works best for the family (see resource_naps_during_stage_2).
- Feeding (shows for families with active night feeds): always feed the child when hungry. The goal is separating feeding from sleep, not reducing calories. This is different from night weaning (see resource_never_reduce_calories).
- Night Wakings: pause about 5-10 minutes before intervening. Less for a very sensitive child; use best judgment.
- Crying: even very gradual changes take getting used to, and some crying is to be expected. Use the Cry Scale (lower-right corner of the app) for how to respond.
- Need More Support: questions, highly sensitive children, breaking steps down further, extenuating circumstances — open the chat tab and ask the assistant.

title: Transcript — "Habit Changing" (`AUDIO_HABIT_CHANGING`)
retrieve_when: a Stage 2 family asks why the approach is gradual, why not just lay the child down awake and walk away, how sleep habits form or change, what the cue-routine-reward loop or scaffolding means, or what the Sleep Ladder is and why the plan starts with their child rather than a predetermined method. Also when a parent references the Stage 2 "Habit Changing" audio.
delivered_to: Stage 2 families (any family with a sleep association to reduce: bedsharing, feeding to sleep, motion to sleep, or pacifier). NOT delivered to fully-independent families or "already there" families — those have no association to reduce, so they skip Stage 2 steps and unlock Stage 3.
body:
Hey, Jessie here! Before you dive into your plan, I want to take just a few minutes to explain the thinking behind it, because I find that when parents understand the why, they actually feel a lot more confident working through the steps.

At its core, sleep training is really about habit changing. That's it. When you do the same thing every single time your child goes to sleep, feed them, rock them, whatever it is, they get used to that. They start to expect it. And eventually, their brain begins associating that specific thing with falling asleep. That's a habit.

And habits aren't a bad thing. We all have them and they can be positive or negative. The problem is when a habit stops working for your family, when something that felt fine six months ago is now waking everyone up five times a night. That's when it's time to change it.

In psychology, habits are often explained as a simple loop: cue, routine, reward. So with sleep, the cue might be feeling tired or waking between sleep cycles. The routine might be nursing, rocking, bouncing, or laying with a parent. And the reward is falling back asleep.

At some point, you started doing whatever helped everyone get back to sleep. And because it worked, you naturally kept doing it. That's how habits form. It doesn't mean you did anything wrong. It just means your child's brain learned, "This is how sleep happens."

Here's the great thing though: As parents, our kids' habits around sleep typically start with us, which means we can change them, too. You are not helpless when it comes to your child's sleep.

The great thing about changing habits is that it can often be done gradually. I never want to just hand a parent a plan that starts with, "okay, on night one, lay your child down awake and walk away." That's a huge leap! Your Goodnight House plan takes a holistic approach. It addresses your whole child and includes strategies meant to set your child up for sleep success.

In psychology, there's something called scaffolding. Think about teaching a child to walk. When your baby is learning to walk, you don't just stand them up and let go. You hold both hands first. Then maybe just one hand. Then you step back a little, let them try, catch them if they fall, try again. You're always right there, you're just gradually stepping back and giving them more of the skill to practice themselves.

Changing sleep habits can work the same way. You can start where your child is right now and take steps down from there. This is the foundation of The Goodnight House Sleep Ladder.

Whatever your child is most dependent on to fall asleep is the top of your ladder. The bottom of the ladder is falling asleep independently. You can gradually walk your child down the ladder and help them gain confidence around falling asleep independently, one step at a time.

This will look different for different families, depending on the individual child. There is no single sleep approach that works for every child. Instead of starting with a predetermined method, your plan starts with your child. Your personalized plan is designed to guide your child through their own unique Sleep Ladder and determine which sleep strategies are most appropriate for your individual child and family.

title: Bedsharing edge case: child sleeps on top of the parent
retrieve_when: a bedsharing family in Stage 2 says their child must physically sleep on top of a parent in order to fall asleep or stay asleep (with or without continuous nursing). This is an uncommon situation not screened for at intake.
body:
This is an uncommon situation that is not screened for during intake. If a parent asks about it, coach using the guidance below rather than inventing new strategies. **Present the guidance conversationally, one step at a time, in response to the parent's specific question. Do not overwhelm parents by explaining the entire process unless they ask.** Always reassure parents that children should be fed whenever they are genuinely hungry: the goal is simply to separate feeding from sleep, never to reduce calories or intentionally withhold feeds.

This edge case applies to children who must physically sleep on top of a parent in order to fall asleep or stay asleep. Some children simply need to lie on top of the parent. Others also nurse continuously while doing so. The first goal is always to help the child become comfortable sleeping beside the parent rather than on top of the parent.

**If the child does not nurse.** Goal: help the child become comfortable lying beside the parent. Typical timeline: 2–3 nights.
- Beginning at bedtime, help the child lie beside you rather than on top of you.
- Use whatever soothing your child needs to feel settled beside you, including rocking, patting, cuddling, physical contact, or your voice.
- Do not introduce feeding as a new sleep strategy. The goal is to keep moving forward, not backward.
- During the night, continue assisting your child back to sleep however necessary.
- Once your child is consistently comfortable falling asleep beside you, return to the family's normal sleep plan and continue following the plan exactly as written from that point forward.

**If the child also nurses continuously.** This is different from the standard continuous latch plan. Normally, Jessie's first step is to begin gently unlatching before the child is fully asleep. However, when a child both nurses continuously and must sleep on top of the parent, changing both habits at the same time is often too large of a leap. For these families, temporarily override the normal first step in the plan.

Goal: help the child become comfortable nursing beside the parent before working on the latch. Typical timeline: 1–3 nights.
- Beginning at bedtime, nurse your child in a side-lying position beside you rather than on top of you.
- At this stage, do not focus on reducing the amount of nursing.
- If your child needs to remain latched to fall asleep, allow it. If the latch naturally releases once your child is asleep, that's wonderful. If not, don't force it yet.
- Continue nursing this way during overnight wakings as well.
- The only goal during this step is helping your child become comfortable nursing and falling asleep beside you rather than on top of you.

Once your child is consistently comfortable nursing beside you, this edge case has been resolved. At that point, return to the family's normal sleep plan and continue with the next step as written. The plan will then guide the family through gradually separating nursing from sleep and continuing the rest of the bedsharing transition.

**What to expect.** This transition should not take weeks; most families are ready to move on after 1–3 nights. Even very gradual changes feel unfamiliar at first, and some crying is normal and expected. Parents should intentionally but gently guide these changes rather than waiting for the child to choose them independently. Use the Cry Scale in the app to help determine when to increase support and when to hold steady. Continue prioritizing daytime sleep by assisting or rescuing naps as needed.

title: The parent's habit loop (coaching reference)
retrieve_when: a parent is struggling to stay consistent during habit changing, feels discouraged, feels like they're "doing it wrong," or keeps slipping back into the old sleep-association response. AI coaching reference — not parent-facing.
body:
This expands on the parent-facing Habit Changing audio. Use these concepts to coach parents who are struggling to stay consistent or feel discouraged during the habit-changing process.

**The parent has a habit loop too.** When parents hear their child cry, they naturally want to help. Over time, parents often develop their own habit loop right alongside their child's. For example:

- Cue: The child cries or wakes.
- Routine: The parent rocks, feeds, or uses the usual sleep strategy.
- Reward: Everyone gets back to sleep.

There is no blame in this. It is simply how exhausted brains work. Under stress and sleep deprivation, habits become deeply ingrained because they help us get through the night with the least amount of effort. Help parents recognize that changing sleep habits means changing both the child's habits and their own.

**Parents have tremendous influence.** One of the most encouraging parts of habit changing is that parents have tremendous influence over the process. Parents cannot control whether a child resists a change, but they can control the response that consistently follows. Because that response becomes part of the child's habit loop, parents have meaningful influence over helping new habits develop. This is why consistency matters so much. When the response changes consistently over time, children gradually learn new expectations and new ways of falling asleep.

**Coaching tone.** When parents feel discouraged, remind them that they are not "doing it wrong" simply because the old habit feels automatic. Changing habits is difficult for everyone, including adults. It takes intention, repetition, and consistency before the new response begins to feel natural. Frame this as empowering rather than discouraging. Parents are not stuck with the current pattern. By consistently changing their own response, they can help their child build a new habit over time.

title: Trust the process (consistency, confidence, adjusting to change)
retrieve_when: a parent is discouraged in the early nights of habit changing, worries the plan isn't working because the first nights feel harder, or asks how to stay consistent and confident through the change.
body:
**Trust the process.** Sometimes this stage feels harder before it feels easier, and that is completely normal. When you begin changing a sleep habit, your child is learning a new way of falling asleep and falling back asleep. During the first few nights, they may be frustrated by the change or seem unsure of what to do. That doesn't mean the plan isn't working. It often means they are adjusting to something new.

**Consistency is communication.** One of the clearest forms of communication you have with your child is consistency. Young children can't sit down and have a conversation about new expectations. Instead, they learn by experiencing the same loving response over and over again. When your response is consistent, your child will begin to understand what to expect. That predictability not only helps them learn the new sleep habit more quickly, but also provides a sense of security. Changing directions from one night to the next, or midway through a sleep attempt, can be confusing because it becomes much harder for your child to understand what you're asking them to learn.

**Your confidence matters.** Children are incredibly perceptive. They often take their emotional cues from the adults caring for them. As you work through these changes, try to remain as calm and confident as you can. Even if you're feeling uncertain on the inside, your steady presence communicates that your child is safe and that everything is okay. That doesn't mean your child will immediately love the change. They may still become frustrated. But when you remain calm and confident, you're helping communicate, "This is different, but it's okay. I'm here with you." This mindset is helpful throughout the entire sleep plan.

title: The step-down pattern and adjusting the pace (expect a hard night per step; resize steps to fit the child)
retrieve_when: a parent is discouraged after a hard night that followed progress, thinks the plan stopped working because a new step brought fresh difficulty, asks why things got harder again after improving, wants to abandon the plan after a hard step, or has a child breezing through steps faster than the plan's pace.
body:
Many families notice a "step-down" pattern during habit changing.

Here's what that often looks like:

- You make a small change and the first night is difficult.
- The next night, or the night after, things start to improve.
- Once that step feels comfortable, it's time for the next small change.
- That new step may bring another difficult night or two before things settle again.

This pattern can repeat throughout the habit-changing process.

Nothing is wrong, and it doesn't mean the plan has stopped working. Some children are simply more sensitive to change, even when the changes are very gradual.

Not every family experiences this. Some children move through each step with very little frustration and hardly seem to notice the changes at all. Others need a day or two to adjust each time.

If parents understand this pattern ahead of time, they're much less likely to become discouraged or think they need to change course after one difficult night.

**Adjusting the pace.** One of the strengths of Jessie's framework is that it can be adjusted to match each child's starting point. If a parent says a step was much harder than expected, reassure them first — a difficult night does not mean they failed or that they need to abandon the plan. Instead, help them understand what they learned: "It sounds like {{child_name}} may have been a little more attached to that sleep habit than we realized." Treat this as helpful information, not a setback.

Rather than abandoning the framework, simply make the next step a little smaller. For example:

- If moving from feeding to fully awake was too large of a jump, continue feeding until drowsy and finish getting the child to sleep with rocking or patting instead.
- If reducing rocking was too difficult, temporarily add back a little more movement and reduce it more gradually over the next few nights.

Frame this as meeting the child where they are, not going backward.

Likewise, if a parent says their child is breezing through a step, encourage them to keep the momentum going. They do not need to spend extra nights on a step their child has already mastered. If appropriate, encourage them to move to the next step or skip a step that clearly isn't needed.

The goal stays the same. The pace simply changes to fit the child.

title: Your child's feelings matter (crying is okay; you control the response, not the feeling)
retrieve_when: a parent is upset by their child's crying or frustration during the change, worries the tears mean something is wrong, or feels they must stop every tear immediately.
body:
As you begin making changes to your child's sleep habits, it's normal for them to become frustrated. Young children have a limited number of ways to communicate, and crying is one of the primary ways they express themselves.

That doesn't mean something is wrong or that the plan isn't working.

It's okay for children to express that they don't like a change. As parents, we don't need to stop every tear immediately. Instead, we can stay with them, comfort them, reassure them, and hold the loving boundary we've chosen.

Even very gradual changes don't always prevent tears. Every child has a different temperament. Some children hardly notice these small changes, while others have a much bigger response to the exact same change.

Parents cannot control how their child feels or reacts. What they can control is how they respond. They can offer comfort, reassurance, and support while confidently leading their child through the change.

title: One small change at a time (keep every comfort except the one habit you're changing)
retrieve_when: a parent worries they're taking away all their child's comfort, feels overwhelmed by the change, or asks whether they can still rock, cuddle, pat, or sing while changing one sleep habit.
body:
Remind parents that they are not taking away all of their child's comfort.

Throughout this stage, they should continue using every soothing strategy available to them except the one sleep habit they are intentionally changing.

For example, if the goal is to stop feeding to sleep, parents can still rock, cuddle, pat, sing, or offer reassurance. The only thing changing is feeding to sleep.

Keeping everything else familiar helps children adjust because they still have many ways to feel comforted while learning one new sleep skill.

If parents become discouraged, remind them that they are not expecting their child to learn everything overnight. They are making one small change at a time while continuing to provide plenty of comfort and support.

title: Bringing in another caregiver (for feeding/nursing-to-sleep associations)
retrieve_when: a family is changing a feeding- or nursing-to-sleep association and another caregiver is available at bedtime or overnight, or a parent mentions the child will only nurse to sleep with the nursing parent but settles other ways with someone else.
body:
One strategy Jessie often recommends is having another caregiver help with bedtime or overnight wakings whenever it is practical. This is especially helpful when the primary caregiver is the one who typically nurses or feeds to sleep. Children quickly learn different expectations with different caregivers. If Dad or another caregiver has never regularly fed the child to sleep, the child is often less frustrated by the change because they aren't expecting to nurse or feed with that person in the first place. The assistant should encourage parents that another caregiver is fully capable of comforting their child. This is not about replacing Mom, creating distance, or giving up control. It's simply another way to support the child through a habit change.

**A great sign.** If a parent says something like "My baby will only nurse to sleep with me, but my husband can rock them to sleep," or "Our nanny can get them to sleep without nursing," reassure the parent that this is actually very encouraging. It tells us two important things:

1. The child is capable of falling asleep without nursing.
2. That caregiver may be especially helpful at bedtime or during wakings when they are already available.

If Dad is home in the evenings, encourage him to take the lead at bedtime or respond to some overnight wakings while the family is working through habit changes. If the family has another caregiver who is already involved at sleep times (such as a live-in au pair or overnight nanny), they may also be a good option.

**When it works best.** This strategy tends to work especially well for families who are not bedsharing or who only bedshare part of the night. It may be less effective for families who exclusively bedshare, since the child often expects to fall asleep next to the nursing parent. Even then, it is still worth trying. Some children do very well having another caregiver rock, cuddle, or soothe them to sleep instead. Present this as an option to consider, not a requirement.

title: Coaching principles: when parents become discouraged
retrieve_when: a parent is discouraged, wants to quit or change course, or feels the method isn't working — a checklist for how the assistant should coach in that moment.
body:
When parents become discouraged:

- Look for objective progress in the logs.
- Reframe setbacks as information.
- Look for encouraging signs.
- Preserve momentum whenever possible.
- Reduce the size of the step rather than abandoning the framework.
- Troubleshoot foundations before assuming the method isn't working.
