title: Which wake window to adjust first, by age (solid sleepers only)
retrieve_when: a settled sleeper's schedule needs light fine-tuning and the assistant needs to know which wake window or nap to adjust first for the child's age (7-8mo, 9-11mo, 16-17mo, 18-23mo, 2yr). Not for a getting-started family, and not during the active plan.
body:
**Use only once the child is sleeping well (falling asleep independently with consolidated night sleep). Do not apply this to a getting-started family. During the active plan the schedule is held consistently, not tuned; short naps and slightly drifting timing are expected then and are handled with the dark sleep space and an early bedtime, not wake-window adjustments.**

Once a child is a settled sleeper and the schedule genuinely needs fine-tuning, this is the lever to reach for first at each age. Make one small change, then watch 3-5 days before changing anything else (see resource_general_troubleshooting and resource_schedule_advancement_troubleshooting for the full framework).

- 7-8 months (settled on 2 naps): the morning wake window is usually the first thing to adjust. If naps are drifting too early in the day or bedtime is creeping too early, stretch the morning window first, from about 2.5 toward 3 hours.
- 9-11 months: the morning wake window again. Stretch it from about 3 toward 3.25 hours if naps drift early or bedtime creeps earlier than the parent would like.
- 16-17 months (settled on 1 nap): the afternoon wake window (after the nap, before bed) is the first thing to adjust. If bedtime is taking a long time or the child is waking early, move bedtime slightly later or earlier in 15-minute steps until you find the sweet spot.
- 18-23 months and 2 years: nap timing is the first thing to adjust. If the nap shortens or bedtime starts taking longer, try moving the nap about 15 minutes later. Small adjustments go a long way at this age.

This age-specific guidance previously lived in the schedule cards; it was moved here so it only reaches settled sleepers, not families still working the plan.
gate_evidence: solid sleepers only; not for a getting-started family

title: When a parent worries their child can't handle the assigned schedule (getting-started reassurance)
retrieve_when: a getting-started parent worries their child can't handle the assigned schedule: says there's no way the child will make it, that the wake windows are too long, that the child will be a mess, that the child is on more naps than the schedule calls for, or points to short or 30-minute naps as a reason the schedule won't work.
body:
**Assistant-facing guardrails (do not say these to the parent).** Hold the assigned schedule. Do not re-route the child, change the nap count, drop or add a nap, or adjust wake windows based on the child's current naps. Never tell a parent the schedule is chosen by age or nap count, and never call a child's sleep "disorganized"; that reasoning is internal only (for the why, see resource_nap_transition_routing). A getting-started child's short naps, early wakings, and being on more naps than the schedule are expected, and are not signs the schedule is wrong or that the child isn't ready.

When a parent worries their child can't handle the schedule, or points to short or 30-minute naps or more naps than the schedule calls for, reassure them warmly and hold the schedule with these tools:

- Keep the child in the dark sleep space for the full nap period, even when they wake early. That quiet rest is still restorative, and it protects the next nap and bedtime (see resource_dark_sleep_space_full_period).
- Use an early bedtime on short-nap days, as early as 6:00pm, so the child doesn't get overtired (see resource_early_bedtime_framework).
- Normalize short naps as common right now, and explain that staying consistent with the schedule is what helps them lengthen over time. Lengthening and independent naps are worked on later, in Stage 4.

Keep the tone warm and confident. The parent isn't doing anything wrong, and the schedule is built to work.

Parent-facing example: "I understand, 30-minute naps can feel like they can't possibly be enough. This is exactly why Jessie has you keep your little one in the dark sleep space for the full nap period, even when they wake early. That quiet rest is still restorative, and it protects the next nap and bedtime. On the days naps run short, lean on an early bedtime, as early as 6:00pm, so they don't get overtired. Short naps are really normal right now, and staying consistent is what helps them stretch out. You're not doing anything wrong."

title: Early Waking and the Nap
retrieve_when: a parent asks what time the nap should be after the child woke earlier than the usual morning wake time, whether the nap should move earlier after an early wake, whether an early wake means shifting the whole schedule earlier, or how to handle the day after a very early start.
body:
**Core principle**

The schedule is built from the consistent morning wake time, not from the time the child first wakes. The nap time is set from the morning wake time, so the morning wake time matters more than the nap. When the morning wake time stays consistent, the nap can usually stay consistent too.

If the day starts early, with light, food, activity, and normal interaction, the whole schedule can start shifting earlier. In that case, the goal is not to fine-tune the nap. The goal is to get through the day and go back to a consistent morning wake time the next day.

**Ask this first**

Before giving nap advice, ask: "When the child woke early, did they stay in the dark sleep space until the usual wake time, or were they up and starting the day from the early time?" The answer decides which guidance to give.

**Path 1: the morning wake time was held** (the child woke early but stayed in the dark sleep space until the usual morning wake time)

- Reassure the parent.
- Keep the nap at the usual time.
- Aim for the earlier end of the nap window rather than stretching to the later end.
- Do not shift the whole schedule earlier because of one early morning.
- Do not troubleshoot unless the pattern repeats for several days.
- If the nap runs short, use an early bedtime as the catch-up.

Parent-facing example: "Perfect, that's exactly right. Keep the nap at the usual time even though she may be a little tired going in, and aim for the earlier end of the nap window. One early morning like this is really normal, so I wouldn't read much into today. If the nap runs short, an early bedtime can be your catch-up."

**Path 2: the day started early** (the child was taken out of the sleep space and started the day from the early waking time)

- Do not fine-tune the nap.
- Treat today as a get-through-the-day situation.
- Protect day sleep however the parent can.
- Allow an earlier nap if needed.
- Use an early bedtime to make up lost sleep.
- Put the main focus on holding the usual morning wake time the next day.

This is not a schedule change. It is a way to get through an overtired day. The real fix is the next morning: keep the child in the dark sleep space until the usual wake time so the rest of the schedule can stay consistent again.

Parent-facing example: "Totally understandable. On a morning like that, just do what you need to do to get through the day, and don't worry too much about the exact nap time. If the nap needs to be a little earlier today, that's okay. The bigger piece is tomorrow morning: when she wakes early, keep the room dark, calm, and quiet until the usual wake time before starting the day. That morning wake time is what the rest of the schedule is built from. Lean on an early bedtime tonight if she's worn out."

**If the family is new to the plan**

Do not imply the parent did something wrong. If they have not learned the morning wake time yet, treat this as expected and teach it gently.

Parent-facing example: "That makes sense. This is one of those pieces that often takes a few days to get used to. For today, just get through the day and use an early bedtime if needed. Tomorrow morning, the goal is to keep her in the dark sleep space until the usual wake time, so the rest of the day has a consistent starting point."

**If the family has already been coached on the morning wake time**

Stay warm and do not scold. Remind the parent that the morning out-of-crib time matters, and that it often takes families a few days to hold it consistently.

Parent-facing example: "This is a common thing to find tricky at first, so don't be hard on yourself. For today, protect sleep however you can and use an early bedtime if needed. Tomorrow morning, put your energy into holding the usual wake time in the dark room. That is the piece that helps the rest of the schedule stay consistent."

**Decision order for the assistant**

1. Ask whether the morning wake time was held.
2. If yes, keep the nap at the usual time and use an early bedtime if needed.
3. If no, do not fine-tune the nap. Help the family get through today and reset the next morning.
4. If the family is new to the plan, teach the morning wake time without blame.
5. If the early waking repeats for several days, treat it as a pattern and use the related morning wake time resources.

**Guardrails.** Do not answer the nap question before asking whether the morning wake time was held. Do not automatically move the nap earlier because the child woke early. Do not treat one early morning as a schedule problem. Do not over-focus on exact nap timing when the day already started early. Do not forget the early bedtime as the catch-up tool.

title: Travel (car rides, flights, hotels, vacations, and coming home) — overall approach and returning home
retrieve_when: a family mentions upcoming travel or a vacation and asks how to approach sleep in general, how much to protect the routine, or how to get back on track after returning home.
body:
The assistant should reason from the principles below and adapt them to the family's specific situation rather than trying to match their situation to an exact example.

**Core Principles**

- Protect the routine where you can, but be flexible. A settled sleeper can have an off day or two and usually settles back into their routine quickly once home.
- Recreate the sleep environment as closely as possible. Darkness, white noise, familiar sleep cues, and the normal bedtime routine are the biggest factors you can control.
- You cannot make a child sleep. You can only offer the opportunity. If naps don't happen, don't panic. Focus on making up for missed sleep with an earlier bedtime when appropriate.
- Once you return home, jump straight back into the normal routine. Most settled sleepers return to normal within a few days.

**Planning Around Travel**

If possible, plan long drives or flights around the child's normal nap schedule, especially for children who struggle with on-the-go sleep. However, reassure parents that this is not always realistic, and flexibility is part of traveling with children.

During travel, encourage parents to do as much of the normal sleep routine as the situation allows, understanding that travel days rarely go exactly as planned.

**Returning Home**

When the trip is over, return directly to the child's normal routine.

Do not gradually ease back into the schedule or continue using extra sleep supports that were only needed during travel.

Most settled sleepers return to their usual sleep patterns within a few nights.

**Guardrails**

The assistant should NOT:

- tell parents their child slept "too much" on the go,
- treat one rough trip as a regression,
- recommend restarting sleep work because of travel,
- prescribe rigid schedule corrections during vacation,
- make parents feel like travel has undone months of progress.

Travel is a temporary disruption, not a setback. Protect what you reasonably can, enjoy the trip, and return to the normal routine once you're home.

title: Travel (car rides, flights, hotels, vacations, and coming home) — car rides, flights, and arrival
retrieve_when: a family asks about sleep during the trip itself: long car rides, flights, on-the-go naps, or bedtime once they arrive after a travel day.
body:
This covers sleep on the travel days themselves: long car rides, flights, and what to do once you arrive.

**Long Car Rides**

Parents commonly worry that naps during long drives will ruin the schedule or that missed naps will derail sleep.

Reassure them:

- Some children sleep much more than usual in the car. This is normal. Car naps are generally less restorative than crib naps, so many children are still appropriately tired by bedtime.
- Some children barely sleep at all in the car. This is also normal. Parents cannot force sleep. If the child doesn't nap, offer an earlier bedtime after arriving.

Helpful strategies include:

- Plan restroom breaks and pit stops around nap times when possible.
- Complete as much of the normal nap routine as practical before getting back into the car.
- Offer a lovey or comfort item if the child uses one.
- Use a portable sound machine when practical.
- Remember that offering the opportunity for sleep is the parent's job; whether the child sleeps is not.

**Flights**

The same principles apply during air travel.

At the child's normal nap time:

- Complete as much of the normal sleep routine as possible.
- Offer a lovey or comfort item if the child uses one.
- Close the window shade and reduce stimulation when possible.
- If appropriate, consider bringing the child's car seat if they sleep significantly better in it.

Again, remind parents that they cannot force sleep during travel.

**Once You Arrive**

If the child missed naps or slept poorly during travel, offer an earlier bedtime when appropriate (often about 30-60 minutes earlier).

Even if the child slept during travel, an early bedtime is often still helpful because on-the-go naps are generally less restorative than naps in the normal sleep environment.

title: Travel (car rides, flights, hotels, vacations, and coming home) — sleep environment, room sharing, and naps at the destination
retrieve_when: a family asks about sleep at the destination: darkening an unfamiliar room, recreating the sleep environment, room sharing in a hotel or while camping, or naps during the vacation.
body:
This covers sleep once you are at your destination: recreating the sleep environment, room sharing, and naps during the trip.

**Recreating the Sleep Environment**

This is one of the biggest predictors of travel success.

Encourage parents to recreate the child's normal sleep environment as closely as possible by prioritizing:

- a very dark sleep space,
- white noise,
- the child's normal bedtime routine,
- familiar sleep cues and comfort items when appropriate.

Suggestions for darkening the room include:

- travel blackout curtains,
- aluminum foil with painter's tape,
- a SlumberPod,
- or any other safe method that significantly reduces light.

(See `resource_slumberpod` for product-specific information.)

**Room Sharing While Traveling**

Room sharing often makes sleep more difficult because the child can see or hear the parents.

Whenever possible, encourage parents to create a visual barrier between themselves and the child.

Ideas include:

- using a SlumberPod,
- placing the pack-and-play in the vanity area or hallway of a hotel room,
- using a closet if it is safe and well ventilated,
- hanging sheets to create a visual divider,
- creating a barrier while camping.

Some children tolerate room sharing well. Others benefit significantly from visual separation. Encourage parents to do what works best for their family.

**Naps During Vacation**

Encourage parents to balance protecting sleep with enjoying their vacation.

General guidance:

- Prioritize one good crib (or hotel-room) nap each day whenever practical.
- If both naps cannot happen in the sleep environment, prioritize the morning nap for children on two naps. Well-rested children are often more successful with an afternoon stroller, carrier, or car nap.
- It is okay to have some flexible days. Parents should not feel trapped at the hotel because of the schedule.

The goal is not perfect sleep while traveling. The goal is maintaining enough structure that the child continues sleeping well overall.
subtopic: sleep environment, room sharing, and naps at the destination

title: Daylight savings (fall back and spring forward) — how to choose a strategy
retrieve_when: a family asks in general how to handle the daylight savings time change, how to decide on a strategy, or what approach to take.
body:
This resource applies only to the one-hour daylight savings time change. Large time-zone changes and permanent moves are separate topics.

The assistant should reason from the principles below rather than automatically recommending a specific strategy.

**Core Principles**

1. **Match the strategy to the child, not the calendar.** Consider the child's sleep flexibility and current sleep before recommending a plan. Children with strong, independent sleep habits usually tolerate daylight savings much better than children who are already struggling.
2. **Scale the response to the problem.** A one-hour clock change is only a one-hour shift. Most families do not need an elaborate plan, and many children naturally adjust within a few days.
3. **Choose the simplest effective approach.** If sleep is already going well, start with the least complicated option. Reserve gradual adjustments for children who are particularly sensitive to schedule changes.
4. **Let the environment do some of the work.**
   - **Fall back:** protect against early morning light with a dark room and by checking for light leaks.
   - **Spring forward:** protect against evening light with blackout curtains, a calm bedtime routine, and limiting stimulating activities and screens before bed.
5. **The direction matters.**
   - **Fall back:** children often wake "too early."
   - **Spring forward:** children often appear to sleep later.
   - When making gradual adjustments, move the schedule in **15-30 minute** increments in the appropriate direction.

**What NOT to Do**

- Don't automatically give every family a multi-day adjustment plan.
- Don't treat a few off days as a regression.
- Don't choose a strategy before considering the child's sleep flexibility and current sleep.

**Step 1: Assess the Child**

Before recommending a strategy, consider both sleep flexibility and current sleep.

**Sleep Flexibility**

- **Flexible sleeper:** usually adapts well to travel, schedule changes, and occasional missed naps.
- **Moderately sensitive sleeper:** may need a few days to adjust.
- **Highly sensitive sleeper:** sleep is often disrupted by even small schedule changes.

**Current Sleep**

Consider:

- Is sleep currently going well?
- Is the child already waking early?
- Is the child falling asleep independently?
- Is the child generally well rested?

Children with healthy, established sleep habits typically adjust much more easily than children whose sleep is already struggling.

**Step 2: Prepare the Environment**

Regardless of which strategy is chosen:

**Fall Back**

Pay special attention to early morning light.

- Keep the room very dark during the early morning hours.
- Check for new light leaks after the clocks change.

**Spring Forward**

Pay special attention to evening light.

- Use blackout curtains if needed.
- Keep the bedtime routine calm and predictable.
- Limit stimulating activities and screens before bedtime.

**Step 3: Set Expectations**

A one-hour clock change is only a one-hour schedule shift.

Most children do not need weeks to adjust.

Many children begin adjusting naturally before the clocks change by gradually shifting their own sleep timing. Temporary changes in sleep timing for a few days are common and do not usually require major intervention.

**Jessie's General Approach**

If sleep is already going well, Jessie generally recommends starting with the simplest approach.

Most families do not need an elaborate daylight savings plan. Children with strong sleep habits often adjust well when parents simply maintain a consistent schedule, keep expectations realistic, and stay patient for a few days.

For children who are especially sensitive to schedule changes, a gradual adjustment before or after the time change is often worth considering.

**Quick Decision Framework**

Before recommending a strategy, ask:

- Is sleep currently going well?
- How sensitive is the child to schedule changes?
- Would this family rather prepare beforehand or adjust afterward?

Then recommend the **simplest strategy** that fits both the child and the family.

title: Daylight savings (fall back and spring forward) — fall back (clocks move back)
retrieve_when: a family asks about the fall daylight savings change (clocks move back / 'fall back'), or reports a child waking too early after the clocks changed in fall.
body:
This is the fall daylight savings change, when the clocks move back.

**Fall Back (Clocks Move Back)**

Children often appear to wake "too early" because their body clock is still operating on the previous schedule.

**Option A: Immediate Shift**

Best for:

- Flexible sleepers
- Independent sleepers
- Children without existing early morning waking problems

Approach:

Change the clocks and immediately begin following the new schedule for wake time, meals, naps, and bedtime.

Many children adjust with little difficulty.

**Option B: Gradual Adjustment Before the Change**

Best for:

- Sensitive sleepers
- Children who struggle with schedule changes

Approach:

Move the entire schedule later by about **15 minutes every 1-2 days** during the week leading up to the time change.

Shift wake time, meals, naps, and bedtime together.

If sleep begins deteriorating, pause and hold the current schedule for several days before making another adjustment.

**Option C: Mini Pre-Adjustment**

Best for:

Families who want some preparation without making a full week of adjustments.

Approach:

Shift the schedule **30-60 minutes later** the day before the time change, then follow the normal schedule once the clocks change.

**Option D: Adjust After the Change**

Best for:

Families who prefer not to prepare ahead of time.

Approach:

Accept the earlier waking initially, then gradually move wake time, naps, and bedtime later by **15-30 minutes every few days** until the child is back on schedule.

Morning sunlight, consistent meal timing, and calm evenings can support the adjustment.

title: Daylight savings (fall back and spring forward) — spring forward (clocks move forward)
retrieve_when: a family asks about the spring daylight savings change (clocks move forward / 'spring forward'), or reports a child sleeping later or bedtime trouble after the clocks changed in spring.
body:
This is the spring daylight savings change, when the clocks move forward.

**Spring Forward (Clocks Move Forward)**

Children often appear to sleep later because their body clock is still operating on the previous schedule.

**Option A: Immediate Shift**

Best for:

- Flexible sleepers
- Older children
- Children with well-established sleep habits

Approach:

Follow the new clock immediately.

Some families may choose to slightly shorten naps that day if bedtime would otherwise become excessively late.

**Option B: Wake at the New Time**

Best for:

Families who prefer to stay on schedule immediately.

Approach:

Wake the child close to the normal wake time on the new clock and proceed with the usual daily schedule.

This often leads to a faster adjustment.

**Option C: Gradual Adjustment After the Change**

Best for:

Sensitive sleepers who need a slower transition.

Approach:

Allow the child to wake later initially, then gradually move wake time, naps, and bedtime earlier by **15-30 minutes every day or two** until the desired schedule is reached.
